# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Modbus Function Code Identification

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> What is a Function Code in Modbus? Identify the function codes used for (i) reading holding registers and (ii) writing a single coil.

**Model Answer:**

A Function Code in Modbus is a **single-byte numeric identifier** in the request frame sent by the master to a slave, specifying the **action** the slave must perform (read, write, diagnostics, etc.). It occupies the second byte of the Modbus PDU (Protocol Data Unit).

- (i) Reading Holding Registers: **Function Code 0x03 (decimal 3)**.
- (ii) Writing a Single Coil: **Function Code 0x05 (decimal 5)**.

For example, FC 0x06 is used for writing a **single holding register**, while FC 0x10 (decimal 16) is used for writing **multiple holding registers**.

### Question 2: HART Communication Modes

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Explain the two operating modes of HART communication. How many devices can be connected in multidrop mode?

**Model Answer:**

HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer) supports two operating modes:

1. **Point-to-Point Mode:** The traditional 4-20 mA analog current loop carries the **primary process variable (PV)**, while a low-energy **FSK digital signal** at ±0.5 mA is superimposed to carry secondary variables, configuration, and diagnostics. Only **2 devices** (one master, one slave) per loop.

2. **Multidrop Mode:** Up to **15 field devices** can be connected in parallel on a single twisted pair. The analog current of each device is **fixed at 4 mA** (the digital channel is used for all measurement transfer). Each device has a unique poll address (1-15).

**Justification:** Multidrop is preferred when many measurements (e.g., tank levels) need to be read with **low speed and high reliability** without the cost of running individual 4-20 mA pairs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks): Legacy Protocol Comparative Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 | CO3]**
> **(a)** Describe the architecture, frame structure, and operational model of **Modbus RTU** with a neat diagram. Compare it with **Modbus TCP/IP** highlighting the encapsulation differences. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **HART protocol** layered over the 4-20 mA current loop. Discuss its frame structure, multidrop operation, and the role of FSK modulation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Modbus RTU vs Modbus TCP/IP (7 Marks):**

- **Modbus RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):**
  - Operates over **RS-485** or **RS-232** serial physical layers.
  - Uses **binary encoding** for compactness and high speed (up to **115.2 kbps**).
  - **Frame Format:** `Slave Address (1 byte) | Function Code (1 byte) | Data (N bytes) | CRC-16 (2 bytes)`.
  - **Master-Slave Model:** The master initiates every transaction. Up to **247 slaves** on a single bus (addresses 1-247).
  - **Silent Interval:** Frames are separated by a **3.5 character time** silence to mark frame boundaries.

- **Modbus TCP/IP:**
  - Operates over **Ethernet** (TCP/IP stack), using **port 502**.
  - The original RTU payload is encapsulated inside a **MBAP (Modbus Application) Header**:
    - `Transaction ID (2) | Protocol ID (2, =0) | Length (2) | Unit ID (1)`.
  - **CRC is omitted** because TCP/IP already provides error checking (and Ethernet has its own CRC).
  - The **Unit ID** replaces the slave address and is used to route through gateways to downstream serial slaves.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Aspect | Modbus RTU | Modbus TCP/IP |
|---|---|---|
| Physical | RS-485 | Ethernet |
| Speed | 115.2 kbps | 100 Mbps+ |
| Error Check | CRC-16 | TCP checksum + IP |
| Addressing | Slave address byte | Unit ID + IP |

> **Examiner Tip:** Students commonly confuse the **MBAP header** with the standard Modbus PDU. Remember: in Modbus TCP, the **CRC-16 is removed** because the lower layers handle integrity.

**Part (b) — HART Protocol Architecture (7 Marks):**

- **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer)** is a hybrid analog + digital protocol. The **4-20 mA** current loop represents the **primary variable** (e.g., pressure in bar), while a **1200 bps FSK signal** superimposed at ±0.5 mA carries the **digital channel** (secondary variables, device diagnostics, configuration).

- **FSK Modulation:** Uses the **Bell 202** standard: **1200 Hz** represents binary **"1"**, and **2200 Hz** represents binary **"0"**. The average DC value of the FSK signal is **zero**, so it does not disturb the 4-20 mA analog measurement.

- **Frame Structure:**
  - `Preamble (5-20 bytes of 0xFF)` → synchronizes the receiver.
  - `Start Delimiter (1 byte)` → marks frame start.
  - `Address (1 or 5 bytes)` → poll address (1-15) or unique tag.
  - `Command (1 byte)` → HART universal/common/practice command.
  - `Data (0-N bytes)`.
  - `Checksum (1 byte, XOR parity)`.

- **Multidrop Operation:**
  - Up to **15 field devices** on a single twisted pair.
  - All devices fix their current at **4 mA**.
  - The HART master polls each device by its unique poll address.
  - Useful in tank farms and remote well-head monitoring where wiring cost dominates.

**Diagram Reference:** See the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.3 above for the HART layered architecture.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks): Protocol Translation and IIoT Convergence

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | CO4]**
> **(a)** Discuss the limitations of **legacy industrial protocols** in modern IIoT deployments. Explain with examples why direct cloud integration is not feasible. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable **block diagram**, describe the architecture of an **IIoT edge gateway** that translates **Modbus RTU** and **HART** data into **MQTT** topics. Explain the role of **OPC UA** in this context. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Limitations of Legacy Protocols (7 Marks):**

1. **Lack of Native IP Support:**
   - Modbus RTU, HART, Profibus DP, and FF H1 use serial/fieldbus physical layers. They cannot directly route over Ethernet/Internet.
   - Example: A Modbus RTU frame cannot be sent as-is to an AWS IoT Core endpoint.

2. **No Built-in Security:**
   - These protocols have **no encryption, no authentication, and no integrity protection**.
   - A simple HART multidrop bus can be passively tapped to extract process secrets.

3. **Limited Semantic Modeling:**
   - Modbus registers are raw 16-bit numbers. There is no metadata describing units, ranges, or quality codes.
   - IIoT platforms require **semantic interoperability** (e.g., a JSON description of what register 40012 means in engineering units).

4. **No Cloud-Native Addressing:**
   - Cloud systems use URIs, topics, and REST APIs. Legacy protocols have **flat, small address spaces** (e.g., 65536 Modbus registers).

5. **Bandwidth and Speed Constraints:**
   - HART runs at only **1200 bps**. Polling 15 devices at multidrop with diagnostic data can take minutes — unsuitable for real-time analytics.

6. **Single-Vendor Lock-in and Lack of Standard Data Models:**
   - Many older Profibus devices use proprietary GSD files; integrating them across vendors is non-trivial.

**Conclusion:** These limitations necessitate the use of **protocol translation gateways** and **semantic mediation layers**.

**Part (b) — IIoT Edge Gateway Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Field Layer} &\rightarrow \text{Modbus RTU slaves (RS-485)} \rightarrow \text{Gateway Modbus Master Port} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Buffer and Decode to JSON} \\
&\rightarrow \text{OPC UA Server (Address Space Model)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{MQTT Client Publisher} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Cloud Broker (e.g., AWS IoT Core, HiveMQ)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Architectural Components:**

1. **Field Interface Layer:** RS-485 transceiver for Modbus RTU, FSK modem for HART multidrop.

2. **Protocol Adaptation Layer:** Embedded library (e.g., **libmodbus**, **HART-IP stack**) that polls registers, parses frames, validates CRCs.

3. **Semantic Mapping:** Mapping engine that converts `Register 40012 = 0x019A` to `{ "pressure_bar": 41.0, "unit": "bar", "quality": "Good" }`.

4. **OPC UA Server:** Exposes a **standardized, secure, semantic information model** (Address Space with Objects, Variables, Methods). OPC UA acts as a **broker between OT and IT**.

5. **MQTT Publisher:** The gateway publishes to topics like `plant/area1/device7/pressure` with **QoS 1** for at-least-once delivery.

6. **Cloud Broker:** Subscribers (dashboards, ML models, historians) receive normalized data.

**Role of OPC UA:**
- Provides a **vendor-neutral, platform-independent information model**.
- Supports **transport security (X.509 certificates)** — solving the legacy security gap.
- Acts as the **unified semantic layer** into which Modbus, HART, and Profibus data are normalized before being exposed to the cloud.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** claim that Modbus TCP/IP provides security. It does not. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Do not** confuse Modbus function codes with register addresses. FC 0x03 is the *operation*; 40012 is the *address*. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Do not** say HART is purely digital or purely analog — it is a **hybrid** protocol. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Do not** omit the CRC-16 calculation in Modbus RTU. If asked, mention the polynomial 0xA001 (reflected form of 0x8005). (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Do not** write the BACnet acronym as "BACnET" or "Backnet". (Lose 0.5 mark.)
> 6. **Do not** draw Profibus and ProfiNet as the same thing — Profibus is serial; ProfiNet is Ethernet-based. (Lose 1 mark.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Legacy Industrial Protocols**

- **Modbus RTU:** Master-slave over RS-485, FC codes, CRC-16 polynomial 0xA001, port 502 for Modbus TCP.
- **Modbus TCP:** Removes CRC, adds MBAP header, encapsulates over Ethernet.
- **HART:** 4-20 mA analog + 1200 bps FSK (Bell 202) digital, 15-device multidrop limit.
- **Profibus DP:** RS-485, up to **12 Mbps**, token-passing, deterministic.
- **Profibus PA:** **31.25 kbps**, intrinsically safe, bus-powered (MBP).
- **Foundation Fieldbus H1:** **31.25 kbps**, distributed function blocks (PID in field).
- **Foundation Fieldbus HSE:** **100 Mbps Ethernet** backbone.
- **BACnet:** ANSI/ASHRAE 135, building automation, supports multiple physical layers.
- **OPC UA:** Modern, secure (X.509), semantic, cross-platform IIoT bridge.
- **IIoT Gateway Pattern:** Legacy protocol → OPC UA semantic layer → MQTT → Cloud.
- **4-20 mA Scaling:** Always linear; $4$ mA = 0%, $20$ mA = 100%.
- **RS-485 Limits:** **1200 m** max length, **32 devices** (extendable with repeaters).
- **Interoperability:** Legacy protocols are translated, not replaced, in brownfield IIoT.
- **Security:** All legacy protocols lack native security — must be added at gateway/firewall level.
- **Determinism:** Profibus and Foundation Fieldbus are deterministic; Modbus and HART are not.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Communication Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT755 - INDUSTRIAL IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modern Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Modern Communication Protocols in IIoT?

In the context of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), **Modern Communication Protocols** are standardized sets of rules, message formats, and data exchange conventions that govern how heterogeneous industrial devices (sensors, actuators, PLCs, gateways, edge nodes, and cloud platforms) communicate, negotiate, and share data across unreliable, constrained, or high-latency networks.

Unlike traditional enterprise protocols (e.g., classic HTTP over TCP), modern IIoT protocols are engineered to address four core challenges:
1. **Constrained Bandwidth & Energy** — Devices often run on batteries over LPWAN, BLE, or cellular NB-IoT.
2. **Asymmetric Publish/Subscribe Models** — One-to-many and many-to-many data distribution.
3. **Heterogeneous Network Topologies** — Mesh, star, hybrid industrial deployments.
4. **Real-time, Deterministic, and Secure Industrial Messaging** — Required for SCADA, DCS, and Industry 4.0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECIT755 Module 3):**
> *Modern Communication Protocols are application-layer messaging and data exchange standards optimized for resource-constrained, distributed, and real-time industrial environments. They include lightweight protocols (MQTT, CoAP), enterprise messaging standards (AMQP), and industrial-grade interoperability frameworks (OPC UA, DDS).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postal System"

Think of an IIoT industrial plant as a **massive smart city**:
- **HTTP (traditional web protocol)** is like sending a registered letter: the sender waits for the post office to confirm delivery, the letter is heavy (lots of headers), and you only get one recipient.
- **MQTT** is like a **newspaper subscription**: the publisher prints a topic (e.g., "BoilerPressure"), and any subscriber who subscribed gets the newspaper instantly — fast, lightweight, and one-to-many.
- **CoAP** is like a **lightweight vending machine query**: tiny HTTP-like requests ("Give me the temperature") with very small headers, optimized for low-power devices.
- **AMQP** is like a **banking wire transfer system**: highly reliable, transactional, queued, and confirmed with acknowledgements.
- **OPC UA** is the **universal translator** in a multinational factory — every machine, regardless of vendor, speaks a common, secure language.
- **DDS** is the **air-traffic-control radio**: real-time, peer-to-peer, ultra-low latency, with quality-of-service guarantees.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Modern Communication Protocols

Modern IIoT communication protocols can be classified along **five engineering dimensions**:

| Dimension | Description | Representative Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Request-Response, Pub/Sub, Streaming | HTTP, MQTT, DDS |
| Transport Layer | TCP, UDP, or both | MQTT (TCP), CoAP (UDP), DDS (both) |
| Message Footprint | Header size & payload efficiency | CoAP (4-byte header), HTTP (heavy) |
| QoS & Reliability | Levels of delivery guarantees | MQTT QoS 0/1/2, DDS QoS policies |
| Security Model | Built-in TLS, DTLS, or external | OPC UA (built-in), CoAP (DTLS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation for KTU Examinations:**
> Examiners frequently ask students to **compare MQTT vs CoAP vs HTTP** in terms of header size, transport protocol, and use-case. Memorize the table above — it is a high-yield answer template for 7-mark questions.

---

## 1.4 The "Big Six" Modern Communication Protocols

The KTU 2024 PECIT755 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the following six protocols (commonly tested):

1. **MQTT** — Message Queuing Telemetry Transport
2. **CoAP** — Constrained Application Protocol
3. **AMQP** — Advanced Message Queuing Protocol
4. **HTTP/HTTPS (REST)** — HyperText Transfer Protocol
5. **OPC UA** — Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture
6. **DDS** — Data Distribution Service

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Quick Recall:** **"M-CAH-OD"** → MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP, OPC UA, DDS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Protocol-by-Protocol Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)

**Origin:** Designed by IBM in 1999 for linking oil pipeline telemetry over satellite links. Standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922** and **OASIS MQTT v5.0** (2019).

**Architecture — The Publish/Subscribe Paradigm:**

MQTT uses a **broker-mediated star topology**. Three actors exist:
- **Publisher** — IoT sensor that pushes data tagged with a *topic* (e.g., `factory/line1/temp`).
- **Subscriber** — Application that subscribes to topics of interest.
- **Broker** — Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) that filters and forwards messages.

**Key Design Principles:**
- **Topic-based filtering** with hierarchical strings separated by `/` (e.g., `home/+/temp`).
- **Wildcards:** `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level).
- **Persistent sessions** for offline clients.
- **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** — A predefined message broadcast by the broker when a client disconnects ungracefully.

**QoS Levels (Critical for KTU Exams):**

| QoS Level | Name | Delivery Guarantee | Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget | No ACK | Lossy sensor data, telemetry |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicates | PUBACK handshake | Reliable but tolerant of dupes |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Single delivery | 4-step handshake (PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP) | Billing, financial, control commands |

**Why It Matters:** The MQTT message header is a fixed **2-byte minimum** (compared to HTTP's hundreds of bytes), making it ideal for **low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks** typical in IIoT deployments.

---

### 2.1.2 CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)

**Origin:** Defined in **RFC 7252** (IETF, 2014). Designed by the IETF **CoRE (Constrained RESTful Environments)** working group.

**Architecture:** CoAP is a **RESTful protocol** that mimics HTTP semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) but operates over **UDP** (not TCP), drastically reducing overhead.

**Key Design Principles:**
- **4-byte binary header** (vs HTTP's variable-length text header).
- **Confirmable (CON)** and **Non-confirmable (NON)** messages.
- **Optional reliability** built on top of UDP via retransmission timers.
- **Resource discovery** via `.well-known/core` URI.
- **Observability** (RFC 7641) — a server pushes resource state changes to subscribed clients.
- **Security via DTLS** (Datagram TLS) — equivalent to TLS for UDP.

**Message Format (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
\text{CoAP Message} = \underbrace{\text{Ver (2b)}}_{\text{01 = v1}} \mid \underbrace{\text{T (2b)}}_{\text{Type}} \mid \underbrace{\text{TKL (4b)}}_{\text{Tok Len}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Code (8b)}}_{\text{Method/Resp}} \mid \underbrace{\text{MsgID (16b)}}_{\text{ID}} \mid \text{Token} \mid \text{Options} \mid \text{Payload}
$$

**Why It Matters:** CoAP is the de-facto standard for **constrained nodes** in 6LoWPAN networks (IEEE 802.15.4), smart energy metering, and building automation (BACnet-over-CoAP).

---

### 2.1.3 AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)

**Origin:** Created by JPMorgan Chase in 2003 for financial messaging. Standardized as **OASIS AMQP 1.0** (ISO/IEC 19464).

**Architecture:** AMQP is a **brokered, connection-oriented, reliable messaging protocol** with strong **transactional semantics**. Unlike MQTT's lightweight design, AMQP provides:
- **Three message delivery guarantees**: At-most-once, At-least-once, Exactly-once.
- **Queues and Exchanges** (direct, topic, fanout, headers).
- **Flow control** via credits.
- **Transactions** (txn atomic commit/rollback).

**Components:**
- **Exchange** — Receives messages from publishers and routes them.
- **Queue** — Stores messages until consumers retrieve them.
- **Binding** — Rules linking exchanges to queues.

**Why It Matters:** AMQP is the **enterprise-grade backbone** for IIoT — used in industrial messaging brokers like **RabbitMQ** and **Apache Qpid**. Ideal for SCADA, ERP integration, and high-reliability manufacturing execution.

---

### 2.1.4 HTTP / HTTPS (REST)

**Origin:** RFC 2616 (HTTP/1.1), RFC 7540 (HTTP/2), RFC 9110 (HTTP/3).

**Architecture:** Request-response over **TCP (port 80)** or **TLS (port 443)**. RESTful APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on URI-identified resources.

**Limitations in IIoT:**
- **Heavy headers** (hundreds of bytes minimum).
- **TCP connection overhead** — 3-way handshake per request.
- **Stateless** — Each request is independent.
- **No native pub/sub** — Requires polling or WebSocket extensions.

**Use Case:** Web dashboards, cloud ingestion endpoints, firmware-over-the-air (FOTA) updates, where bandwidth is plentiful and human-machine interaction dominates.

---

### 2.1.5 OPC UA (Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture)

**Origin:** Developed by the **OPC Foundation** (2008+). Successor to classic OPC (DA, HDA, AE) for industrial automation interoperability.

**Architecture:** OPC UA is a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** framework providing:
- **Information modelling** (address space with nodes, types, methods).
- **Transport-agnostic communication** (binary, HTTPS, WebSockets, AMQP).
- **End-to-end security** (authentication, authorization, encryption, auditing).
- **Platform independence** (.NET, Java, C++, Python).

**Why It Matters:** OPC UA is the **gold standard for Industry 4.0 interoperability** — enabling seamless, vendor-neutral data exchange between PLCs, SCADA, MES, and ERP systems. **IEC 62541** is the international standard.

---

### 2.1.6 DDS (Data Distribution Service)

**Origin:** Standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. Latest revision: **DDS v1.4** (2015), with **DDS Security v1.1** (2018).

**Architecture:** DDS is a **broker-less, peer-to-peer, real-time publish/subscribe middleware** for distributed systems.

**Key Features:**
- **Global Data Space (GDS)** — A virtual shared memory where publishers and subscribers exchange typed data.
- **22 QoS Policies** — including DEADLINE, LATENCY_BUDGET, RELIABILITY, DURABILITY, HISTORY, LIFESPAN.
- **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** wire protocol for interoperability.
- **Discovery built-in** — Participants auto-discover each other.

**Why It Matters:** DDS is the protocol of choice for **mission-critical real-time systems** — autonomous vehicles, aerospace (DDS in Boeing 787 and Airbus A350), defense, robotics, and smart-grid synchrophasor networks.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Matrix

### 2.2.1 Quick-Reference Protocol Header Sizes

| Protocol | Min. Header | Transport | Header Unit | Typical Payload |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MQTT v5.0 | **2 bytes** | TCP | Bytes | Up to 256 MB |
| CoAP | **4 bytes** | UDP | Bytes | Up to 65 KB |
| AMQP 1.0 | **8 bytes** (frame) | TCP | Bytes | Variable |
| HTTP/1.1 | **~200-800 bytes** | TCP | Bytes | Variable |
| OPC UA (binary) | **~24 bytes** | TCP / HTTPS | Bytes | Variable |
| DDS (RTPS) | **~20+ bytes** | UDP / TCP / SHM | Bytes | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical/Comparison Pattern:**
> A frequent 7-mark question is: *"Compare MQTT and CoAP. Which is suitable for constrained devices and why?"* Use the table above as the answer backbone.

### 2.2.2 QoS / Reliability Model Summary

$$
\text{Delivery Guarantee} = f(\text{ACK handshake steps}, \text{Transport retransmission}, \text{Dedup at receiver})
$$

| Protocol | At-most-once | At-least-once | Exactly-once |
|---|---|---|---|
| MQTT | QoS 0 | QoS 1 | QoS 2 (4-step) |
| CoAP | NON message | CON (with retry) | CON + Token dedup |
| AMQP | Settled mode | Unsettled | Transactions (txn) |
| HTTP | — | TCP retransmit | App-layer only |
| OPC UA | — | Best-effort | SecureChannel + App ACKs |
| DDS | BEST_EFFORT | RELIABLE | RELIABLE + KEEP_LAST + App |

### 2.2.3 Theoretical Throughput Equation (Engineering Utility)

For a protocol of **header size** $H$ bytes, **payload size** $P$ bytes, over a link of **bandwidth** $B$ bps and **round-trip time** $R$ seconds, the **effective goodput** $G$ for request-response is:

$$
G = \frac{P}{H + P + \left(\frac{B \cdot R}{8}\right)} \cdot B \quad \text{[bps]}
$$

> This equation is rarely used in numerical exam questions but demonstrates **why smaller headers matter in high-RTT LPWAN networks** (e.g., satellite IIoT links where $R$ dominates).

### 2.2.4 Protocol Selection Decision Tree (Engineering Utility)

| Constraint | Recommended Protocol |
|---|---|
| Battery-powered, lossy, low-bandwidth | **MQTT or CoAP** |
| Real-time, peer-to-peer, no broker tolerance | **DDS** |
| Enterprise integration, transactional, ERP | **AMQP** |
| Industrial vendor-neutral interoperability | **OPC UA** |
| Web/cloud, full-stack REST | **HTTP/HTTPS** |
| Constrained RESTful over UDP/6LoWPAN | **CoAP** |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MQTT** powers AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, and Google Cloud IoT — connecting billions of devices.
- **CoAP** is the standard in smart metering (DLMS/COSEM over CoAP) and Thread networking.
- **AMQP** is the backbone of RabbitMQ, used in financial trading floors and Siemens MindSphere.
- **OPC UA** is mandated in **Industrie 4.0 RAMI 4.0 reference architecture** and in the German **Plattform Industrie 4.0** initiative.
- **DDS** is used in NASA space systems, autonomous vehicles (Autoware), and US Navy combat systems.
- **HTTP** remains the standard for cloud REST APIs and device management portals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this module is conceptual-architectural, the derivations focus on **numerical comparisons of header overhead** and **QoS handshake expansions** — both high-yield KTU problem types.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Bandwidth Overhead Comparison

**Problem:** A sensor publishes 100 messages per hour, each with a **payload of 50 bytes**. Compare the total bytes transmitted per hour (payload + header) for MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP/1.1. Use the minimum header sizes from the formula sheet.

**Step 1 — State the General Formula:**

$$
T = N \cdot (H + P)
$$

where:
- $T$ = total bytes transmitted
- $N$ = number of messages
- $H$ = header size in bytes
- $P$ = payload size in bytes

**Step 2 — Substitute for MQTT ($H = 2$, $P = 50$, $N = 100$):**

$$
T_{\text{MQTT}} = 100 \cdot (2 + 50) = 100 \cdot 52 = 5200 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for CoAP ($H = 4$, $P = 50$, $N = 100$):**

$$
T_{\text{CoAP}} = 100 \cdot (4 + 50) = 100 \cdot 54 = 5400 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute for HTTP/1.1 ($H = 200$ conservative, $P = 50$, $N = 100$):**

$$
T_{\text{HTTP}} = 100 \cdot (200 + 50) = 100 \cdot 250 = 25000 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Overhead Ratio (HTTP vs MQTT):**

$$
\text{Overhead Ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{HTTP}}}{T_{\text{MQTT}}} = \frac{25000}{5200} \approx 4.81
$$

**Conclusion:** MQTT transmits **~4.81× less data** than HTTP for the same payload, making it dramatically more efficient for IIoT.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MQTT QoS 2 Handshake (4-Step)

**Problem:** Show the message exchange for MQTT QoS 2 (Exactly Once delivery) and derive the **total number of packets** exchanged per single publication.

**Step 1 — Identify the Four Messages:**

| Step | Sender → Receiver | Message | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Publisher → Broker | **PUBLISH** | Data sent |
| 2 | Broker → Publisher | **PUBREC** | Publish received |
| 3 | Publisher → Broker | **PUBREL** | Publish release |
| 4 | Broker → Publisher | **PUBCOMP** | Publish complete |

**Step 2 — Count Packets:**

$$
N_{\text{QoS2}} = 4 \text{ packets per logical message}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to QoS 0 and QoS 1:**

$$
N_{\text{QoS0}} = 1, \quad N_{\text{QoS1}} = 2, \quad N_{\text{QoS2}} = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Derive Overhead Multiplier:**

$$
\text{Overhead Factor} = \frac{N_{\text{QoS2}}}{N_{\text{QoS0}}} = 4
$$

**Conclusion:** QoS 2 quadruples the message count, justifying its use **only when exactly-once semantics are critical** (e.g., billing, control commands).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CoAP Confirmable Message Retransmission

**Problem:** A CoAP Confirmable (CON) message is sent. If the first transmission is lost, how many total UDP packets are sent after $r$ retransmissions, given an initial timeout of $T_0$ doubling exponentially?

**Step 1 — Exponential Backoff Formula:**

$$
T_k = T_0 \cdot 2^{k-1}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, r
$$

**Step 2 — Total Wait Time After $r$ Retransmissions:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{r} T_0 \cdot 2^{k-1} = T_0 \cdot (2^r - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Total UDP Packets Transmitted (1 original + $r$ retries):**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 1 + r
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:** $T_0 = 1$ s, $r = 3$ retries:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 1 \cdot (2^3 - 1) = 7 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ UDP packets}
$$

**Conclusion:** CoAP's reliability is **configurable and bounded**, offering a middle ground between UDP's "fire-and-forget" and TCP's connection overhead.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Side-by-Side Protocol View

| Property | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP | HTTP | OPC UA | DDS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP | TCP | TCP | TCP / HTTPS | UDP / TCP / SHM |
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Req/Resp + Obs | Pub/Sub + Queues | Req/Resp | SOA / Req-Resp | Pub/Sub |
| Header Min | 2 B | 4 B | 8 B | ~200 B | ~24 B | ~20 B |
| Security | TLS | DTLS | TLS / SASL | TLS | Built-in | DDS-Security |
| QoS Levels | 3 | 2 (CON/NON) | 3 | App | App | 22 policies |
| Standard | OASIS | IETF RFC | OASIS / ISO | IETF | IEC 62541 | OMG |
| Use Case | Telemetry | Sensors | Enterprise | Cloud | Industry 4.0 | Real-time |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Shortcut Formula for Comparison Questions:**
> $$\text{Score} = \frac{\text{QoS Levels} \times \text{Security Strength}}{\text{Header Size} \times \text{Handshake Overhead}}$$
> Higher score = better suited for **mission-critical** IIoT applications. Lower header + higher QoS generally wins for constrained IIoT.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Node 1] -->|PUBLISH topic=factory/temp| B[MQTT Broker]
    C[Sensor Node 2] -->|PUBLISH topic=factory/pressure| B
    B -->|Forward| D[SCADA Dashboard]
    B -->|Forward| E[Cloud Analytics]
    B -->|Forward| F[Mobile App]
    G[Actuator] -->|SUBSCRIBE factory/cmd| B
    B -->|PUBLISH cmd| G
```

**Explanation:** The broker is the **central decoupling point** — publishers and subscribers need not know each other's IP addresses. This is the **defining feature** of MQTT.

---

## 4.2 CoAP Request/Response with Observation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CoAP Client App] -->|GET temperature| B[CoAP Server Sensor]
    B -->|2.05 Content temp=23.5C| A
    A -->|Register Observe| B
    B -->|Notification: temp=24.0C| A
    B -->|Notification: temp=24.2C| A
```

**Explanation:** The **Observe pattern** (RFC 7641) allows CoAP to act like a lightweight pub/sub mechanism while keeping the HTTP-like semantics.

---

## 4.3 AMQP Exchange-Queue-Binding Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Publisher 1] -->|msg| EX[Exchange: topic type]
    P2[Publisher 2] -->|msg| EX
    EX -->|Binding: factory.#| Q1[Queue: SCADA Data]
    EX -->|Binding: factory.cmd| Q2[Queue: Command Buffer]
    Q1 --> C1[Consumer: Dashboard]
    Q2 --> C2[Consumer: Actuator]
```

**Explanation:** AMQP **decouples routing logic (exchanges)** from **storage (queues)** — a powerful enterprise pattern.

---

## 4.4 OPC UA Information Model — Address Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Root Node] --> Obj[Objects Folder]
    Root --> Typ[Types Folder]
    Root --> View[Views Folder]
    Obj --> Dev1[Device: Boiler]
    Obj --> Dev2[Device: Conveyor]
    Dev1 --> Var1[Variable: Temperature]
    Dev1 --> Var2[Variable: Pressure]
    Dev1 --> Meth1[Method: StartBoiler]
    Typ --> Ref[ReferenceType: HasComponent]
    Typ --> Ref2[ReferenceType: HasProperty]
```

**Explanation:** OPC UA's **information modelling** lets engineers describe complex industrial assets as typed object hierarchies, enabling semantic interoperability across vendors.

---

## 4.5 DDS Global Data Space — Brokerless P2P

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node A: RobotArm] --- N2[Node B: VisionSystem]
    N2 --- N3[Node C: Controller]
    N3 --- N1
    N1 <-->|Topic: JointAngle| GDS[(Global Data Space)]
    N2 <-->|Topic: ObjectPose| GDS
    N3 <-->|Topic: Trajectory| GDS
```

**Explanation:** There is **no central broker** — all participants communicate peer-to-peer via the **RTPS protocol**, achieving sub-millisecond latency.

---

## 4.6 Master Comparison: Protocol Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New IIoT Application] --> Q1{Need real-time less than 1ms?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| DDS1[Use DDS]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Battery powered and lossy link?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Need RESTful semantics?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CoAP1[Use CoAP over UDP]
    Q3 -->|No| MQTT1[Use MQTT over TCP]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Industrial vendor neutral?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| OPC1[Use OPC UA]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Transactional enterprise integration?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| AMQP1[Use AMQP]
    Q5 -->|No| HTTP1[Use HTTP REST to Cloud]
```

**Explanation:** This decision flow is **examiner-friendly** — draw it in the answer script to score full marks on protocol selection questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define MQTT. List its three QoS levels and briefly state the delivery guarantee of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, broker-based publish/subscribe messaging protocol designed for resource-constrained IoT devices over unreliable networks. It is standardized as **OASIS MQTT v5.0 / ISO/IEC 20922**.

The three QoS levels are:

| QoS | Name | Guarantee |
|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget; message may be lost |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Message is acknowledged; duplicates possible |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | 4-step handshake ensures single delivery |

> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing QoS, 1 mark for guarantees)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is CoAP? Mention two key differences between CoAP and HTTP.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is an IETF-standardized (RFC 7252) RESTful application-layer protocol designed for resource-constrained IoT devices. It mimics HTTP semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) but operates over **UDP** rather than TCP.

**Two key differences:**

| Aspect | CoAP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | UDP (with optional DTLS) | TCP (with TLS) |
| Header Size | 4 bytes minimum | 200–800 bytes |
| Reliability | Built-in retransmission (CON messages) | Relies entirely on TCP |
| Overhead | Low — ideal for 6LoWPAN / IoT | High — best for web/cloud |

> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differences)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long Answer (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern:**
> Both questions below were **either-or options** in the actual 2023/2024 KTU ESE. Attempt any **ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3 – Choice 1]**

#### (a) Explain the architecture of MQTT in detail. With neat diagrams, describe the role of the broker, topics, and wildcards. Compare QoS 0, QoS 1, and QoS 2 levels. **(7 Marks)**

#### (b) Explain the Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP) in detail. Discuss its message format, the Observe extension, and explain how DTLS is used for security. Compare CoAP with HTTP. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**1. MQTT Architecture — 3 marks**

MQTT follows a **publish/subscribe (pub/sub) pattern** mediated by a **central broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX). The three components are:
- **Publisher** — Produces data tagged with a *topic* string.
- **Subscriber** — Expresses interest in one or more topics.
- **Broker** — Receives all messages, filters by topic, and forwards to interested subscribers.

The broker **decouples** publishers from subscribers — neither needs to know the other, enabling **one-to-many** and **many-to-many** communication efficiently.

**2. Topics and Wildcards — 2 marks**

Topics are **hierarchical UTF-8 strings** separated by `/`. Example: `factory/line1/sensorA/temperature`.

**Wildcards:**
- `+` (single-level) — matches one level, e.g., `factory/+/temperature`.
- `#` (multi-level) — matches all remaining levels, e.g., `factory/#`.

**3. QoS Comparison — 2 marks**

| QoS | Packets | Guarantee | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | At-most-once | Lossy, fast |
| 1 | 2 (PUBACK) | At-least-once | Possible duplicates |
| 2 | 4 (PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP) | Exactly-once | High overhead |

**Conclusion:** MQTT's design makes it the **de-facto IIoT telemetry protocol** with minimal overhead (2-byte header), making it optimal for constrained devices.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**1. CoAP Overview — 2 marks**

CoAP (RFC 7252) is a **RESTful, UDP-based** protocol designed by the IETF CoRE working group. It enables constrained devices (8-bit MCUs, 10s of KB RAM) to participate in **resource-oriented** web architectures.

**2. Message Format — 2 marks**

A CoAP message has four fields in its 4-byte base header:

| Field | Bits | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Ver | 2 | Version (01) |
| T | 2 | Type: CON, NON, ACK, RST |
| TKL | 4 | Token Length (0–8) |
| Code | 8 | Method (GET=0.01) or Response (2.05) |
| MsgID | 16 | Message ID for matching |
| Token | 0–8 B | Request/Response correlation |
| Options | Variable | URI, Content-Format, etc. |
| Payload | 0–65 KB | Application data |

**3. Observe Extension — 1 mark**

RFC 7641 defines the **Observe option** that allows a client to **register interest** in a resource. The server then **pushes notifications** whenever the resource state changes — converting CoAP into a lightweight pub/sub mechanism.

**4. DTLS Security — 1 mark**

CoAP uses **DTLS (Datagram TLS, RFC 6347)** to provide equivalent security to TLS-over-TCP. It supports:
- **Pre-shared keys**
- **Raw public keys**
- **X.509 certificates**

**5. CoAP vs HTTP — 1 mark**

| Property | CoAP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | UDP | TCP |
| Header | 4 B | Hundreds |
| Multicast | Yes | No |
| Observe/Push | Yes | No (need WebSocket) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3 – Choice 2]**

#### (a) Explain the Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP). Describe its components — exchanges, queues, bindings — and the four types of exchanges. **(7 Marks)**

#### (b) Explain OPC UA and DDS in detail. Compare them in terms of architecture, QoS support, security, and industrial use cases. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**1. AMQP Overview — 1 mark**

AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) is an open standard for **reliable, brokered, transactional messaging** in enterprise systems. Standardized as **OASIS AMQP 1.0 / ISO/IEC 19464**. Used in **RabbitMQ, Apache Qpid, ActiveMQ Artemis**.

**2. Components — 3 marks**

- **Exchange** — Receives messages from publishers; routes based on rules.
- **Queue** — Buffers messages until consumers retrieve them.
- **Binding** — Link rule connecting an exchange to a queue (with routing key).
- **Channel / Connection** — A TCP connection multiplexes many AMQP channels.

**3. Four Exchange Types — 2 marks**

| Exchange | Routing Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | Exact routing key match | Targeted delivery |
| **Topic** | Pattern match with `*` and `#` | Pub/sub on categories |
| **Fanout** | Ignore routing key; broadcast | Broadcast notifications |
| **Headers** | Match message header attributes | Content-based routing |

**4. Message Lifecycle — 1 mark**

Publisher → Exchange → Binding → Queue → Consumer → Ack.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**1. OPC UA — 3 marks**

- **Full form:** Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture
- **Standard:** IEC 62541
- **Architecture:** Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) with **information modelling** (typed address space, nodes, references, methods).
- **Transport:** Binary TCP, HTTPS, WebSockets — vendor-neutral.
- **Security:** Built-in authentication, authorization, encryption, auditing — end-to-end.
- **Use Case:** Industry 4.0, PLC-to-cloud, MES-ERP integration.

**2. DDS — 3 marks**

- **Full form:** Data Distribution Service
- **Standard:** OMG (Object Management Group)
- **Architecture:** **Brokerless, peer-to-peer** pub/sub middleware with **Global Data Space (GDS)**.
- **Transport:** UDP, TCP, or shared memory; uses **RTPS** wire protocol.
- **QoS:** **22 fine-grained policies** — DEADLINE, RELIABILITY, LATENCY_BUDGET, DURABILITY, HISTORY, LIFESPAN, etc.
- **Use Case:** Autonomous vehicles, aerospace, defense, robotics, smart grid.

**3. OPC UA vs DDS — 1 mark**

| Aspect | OPC UA | DDS |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Client-server (broker optional) | Brokerless P2P |
| QoS | Service-level | 22 fine policies |
| Real-time | Moderate | Sub-millisecond |
| Modelling | Yes (rich) | Yes (typed topics) |
| Sweet Spot | Industrial interoperability | Mission-critical real-time |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Modern Communication Protocols Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MQTT QoS 2 with TCP reliability** — QoS 2 is **application-layer** deduplication, *not* transport-layer. Do not equate it with TCP ACK.
> 2. **Stating "CoAP runs on TCP"** — CoAP runs on **UDP**. This is the **most-failed item** in KTU 2023/2024 answer sheets.
> 3. **Confusing OPC UA with classic OPC (DA/HDA/AE)** — OPC UA is the **modern, cross-platform, secure** successor. Mentioning "Unified Architecture" explicitly is mandatory.
> 4. **Writing "DDS uses a broker"** — Wrong! DDS is **brokerless**. State "Global Data Space" and "RTPS" instead.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention wildcards (`+`, `#`) in MQTT** — Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this.
> 6. **Omitting IEC/ISO standard numbers** — Always cite (e.g., **IEC 62541** for OPC UA, **OASIS AMQP 1.0**).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise the night before the exam.**

- **MQTT** = Pub/Sub, **TCP**, **2-byte** header, **3 QoS levels** (0/1/2), broker-based, OASIS standard.
- **CoAP** = REST, **UDP**, **4-byte** header, **CON/NON**, **Observe** for pub/sub, **DTLS** security, RFC 7252.
- **AMQP** = Broker + Queues + Exchanges (direct, topic, fanout, headers), **transactional**, OASIS / ISO 19464.
- **HTTP/HTTPS** = Heavy headers, TCP, REST, used for **cloud / FOTA**, not ideal for constrained IIoT.
- **OPC UA** = **IEC 62541**, **SOA**, information modelling, **built-in security**, Industry 4.0 gold standard.
- **DDS** = OMG, **brokerless P2P**, **RTPS** wire protocol, **22 QoS policies**, real-time / mission-critical.
- **MQTT** → AWS IoT, Azure IoT, telemetry.
- **CoAP** → 6LoWPAN, smart meters, Thread.
- **AMQP** → RabbitMQ, enterprise SCADA.
- **OPC UA** → Siemens, Bosch, Industrie 4.0.
- **DDS** → Boeing, Airbus, autonomous cars.
- **Decision rule:** *Real-time → DDS; Constrained Telemetry → MQTT; RESTful + UDP → CoAP; Enterprise → AMQP; Interoperability → OPC UA.*
- **Key numbers to memorize:** MQTT header 2 B; CoAP header 4 B; QoS 2 = 4 packets; DDS = 22 QoS policies; HTTP header ~200+ B.
- **Standards body map:** IETF → CoAP, HTTP. OASIS → MQTT, AMQP. OMG → DDS. IEC → OPC UA.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Smaller header + smart QoS = IIoT winner."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proximity Network Communication Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT755 - INDUSTRIAL IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proximity Network Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Proximity Network Communication Protocols?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Proximity Network Communication Protocols* are a class of short-range, low-power wireless (and wired) communication standards specifically designed for **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** deployments where sensor nodes, actuators, and edge devices exchange data over distances ranging from a few centimetres up to roughly **100 metres**. These protocols operate primarily in the **sub-1 GHz** and **2.4 GHz ISM bands**, employing topologies such as **point-to-point, star, mesh, and peer-to-peer** to support asset tracking, condition monitoring, smart metering, and factory automation use-cases.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus (PECIT755 – Industrial IoT Applications, Module 3), proximity protocols occupy the middle layer of the **IIoT protocol stack**, sitting *above* the Physical/MAC layers of the OSI model and *below* the higher-level application frameworks (MQTT, CoAP, OPC-UA). They bridge the gap between short-range **PAN (Personal Area Network)** standards and wide-area **LPWAN** standards like LoRaWAN or NB-IoT.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Floor Conversation"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Analogy:**
> Imagine a large manufacturing plant with hundreds of machines, sensors, and workers. Each one needs to "talk" to nearby equipment — but they cannot all shout across the entire factory. Instead, they use **hand signals (NFC tap), short walkie-talkies (BLE), department-wide radio channels (ZigBee mesh), and dedicated control cables (Modbus RTU)**. Proximity protocols are exactly these **short-range "talk rules"** for industrial devices, optimized for **speed, energy efficiency, and reliability** over a small geographic footprint.

### 1.3 Why Proximity Protocols Matter in IIoT

The Industrial IoT demands communication that is:

- **Low Power** — sensor nodes often run on coin-cell or harvested energy
- **Low Cost** — thousands of nodes per plant
- **Low Latency** — control loops need millisecond response
- **Robust** — must survive metal reflections, EMI from motors, and harsh environments
- **Secure** — industrial data is highly sensitive

These requirements are *not* satisfied by Wi-Fi (high power) or Cellular (long range, high cost), which is why an entire family of proximity protocols has emerged.

### 1.4 The Proximity Protocol Family (Syllabus Map)

| Family Member | Typical Range | Topology | Primary IIoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NFC** | < 10 cm | P2P | Equipment tagging, commissioning |
| **RFID (passive)** | < 10 m | Reader-Tag | Asset/inventory tracking |
| **BLE** | < 100 m | Star/P2P | Wearables, beacons, tool tracking |
| **ZigBee** | 10–100 m | Mesh | Wireless sensor networks |
| **Z-Wave** | < 30 m | Mesh | Building automation |
| **Modbus RTU/TCP** | Wired/100 m | Master-Slave | PLC ↔ sensor wiring |
| **M-Bus (Wireless)** | < 500 m | Star/Mesh | Smart metering |
| **Wirepas** | < 100 m | Mesh (decentralized) | Large-scale lighting, metering |
| **Dash7** | < 10 km | Star/Tree | Logistics, cold chain |

### 1.5 Key Technical Metrics to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard IIoT Proximity Metrics (bold for emphasis):**
> - **Frequency Band:** **2.4 GHz** (worldwide ISM), **868 MHz** (Europe), **915 MHz** (US/Asia)
> - **Data Rate Range:** **20 kbps** (Dash7) to **2 Mbps** (BLE 5.0)
> - **Latency Target:** **< 15 ms** for control loops (BLE 4.2), **< 100 ms** for monitoring (ZigBee)
> - **Node Density:** Up to **65,000** devices per ZigBee coordinator
> - **Power Profile:** **< 15 mA** average current for coin-cell operation

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Layered Model

Every proximity protocol can be decomposed using a **simplified 4-layer reference model** for IIoT:

$$\text{Application Layer} \rightarrow \text{Network Layer} \rightarrow \text{MAC Layer} \rightarrow \text{Physical Layer}$$

- **Application Layer** → Vendor profile (e.g., ZigBee Cluster Library, Modbus Function Codes)
- **Network Layer** → Addressing, routing, mesh management
- **MAC Layer** → Channel access (CSMA-CA, TDMA, polling)
- **Physical Layer** → Modulation (DSSS, OFDM, GFSK) and RF frontend

### 2.2 Protocol-by-Protocol Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 NFC (Near Field Communication)
- **Operating Principle:** Inductive coupling at **13.56 MHz**
- **Range:** < **0.1 m** (must be touched or nearly touched)
- **Modes:** Reader/Writer, Peer-to-Peer, Card Emulation
- **Standards:** ISO/IEC 14443, ISO/IEC 15693, NFC Forum
- **Data Rates:** **106, 212, 424, 848 kbps**
- **Use Case:** Commissioning IIoT gateways, secure parameter injection.

#### 2.2.2 RFID
- **Passive Tags:** Harvest energy from reader's RF field (no battery)
- **Active Tags:** Battery-powered, transmit on their own
- **Bands:** LF (125 kHz), HF (13.56 MHz), UHF (860–960 MHz)
- **EPC Class 1 Gen 2** is the dominant UHF standard for supply-chain IIoT.

#### 2.2.3 BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)
- **Version Trail:** 4.0 → 4.2 → 5.0 → 5.4 (most relevant for IIoT)
- **Channel Layout:** **40 channels** in 2.4 GHz ISM, each **2 MHz** wide
- **Advertising:** Channels **37, 38, 39** (used for discovery)
- **Modulation:** GFSK with index **0.5**
- **Max Output Power:** **+10 dBm** (10 mW)
- **Connection Interval:** **7.5 ms** to **4 s**
- **Mesh:** Introduced in BLE 5.0 (flood-based, managed flooding)

#### 2.2.4 ZigBee
- **Standard:** IEEE **802.15.4** PHY/MAC + ZigBee Alliance Network layer
- **Bands:** **2.4 GHz** (16 channels), 915 MHz (US), 868 MHz (EU)
- **Topology:** Star, Tree, **Mesh**
- **Addressing:** 16-bit short address (max **65,536 nodes**)
- **Profiles:** ZigBee Cluster Library (ZCL) for industrial sensors
- **Routers:** Always-on mains-powered nodes; End Devices can sleep.

#### 2.2.5 Z-Wave
- **Band:** **908.42 MHz** (US), **868.42 MHz** (EU)
- **Topology:** Source-routed mesh (up to **232 nodes**)
- **Modulation:** GFSK / BFSK
- **Use Case:** Building automation (HVAC, lighting, security)

#### 2.2.6 Modbus
- **Variants:** Modbus RTU (RS-485 serial), Modbus TCP (Ethernet), Modbus ASCII
- **Topology:** Master-Slave, up to **247 slaves** per master on RS-485
- **Frame:** Function code + data + CRC
- **Use Case:** Backbone protocol for industrial PLCs and SCADA

#### 2.2.7 M-Bus (Meter-Bus)
- **Standard:** EN **13757**
- **Variants:** Wired M-Bus, **Wireless M-Bus (wM-Bus)** at 868 MHz
- **Topology:** One master (meter collector), many slaves (meters)
- **Modes:** S, T, C, F, N (different duty cycles for battery meters)

#### 2.2.8 Wirepas
- **Architecture:** Decentralized mesh — every node is autonomous
- **Band:** 2.4 GHz, Sub-GHz
- **Capacity:** Designed for **millions of nodes**
- **Use Case:** Smart lighting, smart metering, asset tracking

#### 2.2.9 Dash7
- **Band:** **433 MHz**, 868 MHz, 915 MHz
- **Range:** Up to **10 km** (outdoor line-of-sight)
- **Modulation:** GFSK, 2-GFSK
- **Features:** **BLAST** networking (Burst, Low-latency, Asynchronous, Sequential, Transitive)
- **Use Case:** Cold-chain, defense, logistics.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the most-tested formulas, constants, and metrics for proximity protocols. Memorize these for ESE/CE.

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| **Received Power** | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$ | dBm | Link budget equation |
| **Maximum Nodes (ZigBee)** | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | nodes | 16-bit short address |
| **Maximum Nodes (Z-Wave)** | $232$ | nodes | Hard mesh limit |
| **BLE Channel Spacing** | $2$ | MHz | Total 40 channels |
| **BLE Advertising Channels** | $3$ | channels | 37, 38, 39 |
| **NFC Operating Frequency** | $13.56$ | MHz | Inductive coupling |
| **ZigBee Data Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $250$ | kbps | O-QPSK modulation |
| **Modbus RTU Max Slaves** | $247$ | slaves | On RS-485 bus |
| **RSSI → Distance (log-distance model)** | $d = 10^{\frac{A - RSSI}{10n}}$ | metres | $A$ = RSSI at 1 m, $n$ = path-loss exponent |
| **Battery Life Estimate** | $\text{Life} = \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)}}{\text{Average Current (mA)}}$ | hours | Divide by 8760 for years |
| **Air Time per Packet (BLE)** | $T_{air} = \frac{8 \cdot L_{payload} + 328}{R_{bit}}$ | seconds | $L$ in bytes, $R$ in bits/s |
| **Duty Cycle (European 868 MHz)** | $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \leq 1\%$ or $10\%$ | ratio | ETSI EN 300 220 limit |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing:** ZigBee mesh connects vibration, temperature, and pressure sensors to a programmable logic controller (PLC) which then exposes data via **Modbus TCP** to the SCADA layer.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** BLE beacons on motors stream vibration spectra to a gateway; the gateway runs ML inference at the edge.
- **Cold-Chain Logistics:** Dash7 tags travel with pallets, updating temperature logs across transcontinental journeys.
- **Smart Metering:** Wireless M-Bus connects 1000+ residential gas/water meters to a single collector that forwards to the utility cloud.
- **Tool Tracking in Aerospace:** Passive UHF RFID tags survive harsh autoclave cycles; NFC is used to authenticate tooling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates three **worked analytical problems** typical of KTU 14-mark questions. Each derivation is fully explicit; no step is skipped.

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Link Budget for a ZigBee Sensor Link

**Problem Statement:**
A ZigBee sensor at 2.4 GHz transmits with **$P_t = +8$ dBm**. The transmit antenna gain is **$G_t = 2$ dBi**, the receive antenna gain is **$G_r = 2$ dBi**, and the path is **$d = 50$ m** in free space. Compute the received power $P_r$ at the gateway.

### Step 1 — Identify the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

Use the standard FSPL formula with $f = 2400$ MHz and $d = 0.05$ km.

$$\begin{aligned}
FSPL \, (\text{dB}) &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45 \\
&= 20\log_{10}(0.05) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.45
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(0.05) &= 20 \times (-1.30103) = -26.0206 \, \text{dB} \\
20\log_{10}(2400) &= 20 \times 3.38021 = 67.6042 \, \text{dB} \\
\text{Constant} &= 32.45 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Terms

$$\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= -26.0206 + 67.6042 + 32.45 \\
&= 74.0336 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Link Budget Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL \\
&= (+8) + (+2) + (+2) - 74.0336 \\
&= -62.0336 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Linear Microwatts (for intuition)

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r \, (\text{mW}) &= 10^{\frac{-62.0336}{10}} = 10^{-6.20336} \approx 6.27 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{mW} \\
P_r \, (\mu\text{W}) &\approx 0.627 \, \mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** ZigBee receivers typically need **−85 dBm** sensitivity (O-QPSK @ 250 kbps). Our $P_r = -62$ dBm leaves a **link margin of ~23 dB**, comfortably tolerating walls, multipath, and fading inside a factory hall.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — BLE Air-Time for an Industrial Telemetry Packet

**Problem Statement:**
A vibration sensor broadcasts a **20-byte** payload using BLE 4.2 at the default **1 Mbps** PHY. Compute the air time per advertising packet.

### Step 1 — Recall the BLE Air-Time Formula

For BLE 4.x (1 Mbps uncoded PHY):

$$T_{air} = \frac{8 \cdot L_{payload} + 328}{R_{bit}}$$

where the 328-bit offset accounts for preamble (8 bits), access address (32 bits), PDU header (16 bits), CRC (24 bits), and inter-frame spacing.

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{payload} &= 20 \, \text{bytes} \\
8 \cdot L_{payload} &= 8 \times 20 = 160 \, \text{bits} \\
R_{bit} &= 1 \times 10^6 \, \text{bits/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Numerator and Final Time

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 160 + 328 = 488 \, \text{bits} \\
T_{air} &= \frac{488}{1{,}000{,}000} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} \\
T_{air} &= 488 \, \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Estimate Battery Life

If the sensor uses a **240 mAh coin cell** and advertises once every **1000 ms**, average current is approximately the active current times the duty cycle:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Duty Cycle} &= \frac{488 \, \mu\text{s}}{1{,}000{,}000 \, \mu\text{s}} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \\
I_{\text{active}} &\approx 7 \, \text{mA} \quad \text{(typical BLE SoC TX at 0 dBm)} \\
I_{\text{avg}} &= 7 \, \text{mA} \times 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.4 \, \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

Battery life (ignoring self-discharge):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Life} &= \frac{240 \, \text{mAh}}{0.0034 \, \text{mA}} \approx 70{,}588 \, \text{hours} \\
\text{Life in years} &\approx \frac{70{,}588}{8760} \approx 8.06 \, \text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** By keeping the payload short and using *advertising* mode instead of *connections*, an industrial sensor can run **8+ years** on a coin cell — this is the cornerstone of wireless IIoT sensor economics.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Estimating Distance from RSSI

**Problem Statement:**
A ZigBee node measures **RSSI = −65 dBm** at **1 metre**. At a remote location, RSSI = **−92 dBm** with a path-loss exponent of **$n = 2.8$** (typical indoor industrial environment). Estimate the distance.

### Step 1 — Use the Log-Distance Path-Loss Model

$$d = 10^{\frac{A - RSSI}{10 \cdot n}}$$

where $A$ is the RSSI measured at 1 m (the reference). So $A = -65$ dBm.

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 10^{\frac{-65 - (-92)}{10 \times 2.8}} \\
&= 10^{\frac{27}{28}} \\
&= 10^{0.9643}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Exponent

$$\begin{aligned}
d &\approx 9.21 \, \text{metres}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** RSSI-based ranging is **highly sensitive** to multipath and orientation. For IIoT applications requiring cm-level accuracy (e.g., tool tracking on a factory floor), combine RSSI with **Angle-of-Arrival (AoA)** or **UWB** ranging — this is why BLE 5.1 introduced the AoA/AoD feature.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix as a quick reference during the exam. It is also a frequent source of **comparison questions** worth 7–14 marks.

| Protocol | Range | Topology | Band | Modulation | Max Nodes | Power Profile | Typical IIoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NFC | < 0.1 m | P2P | 13.56 MHz | ASK | 1 | Very Low | Commissioning |
| RFID (UHF) | < 10 m | Reader–Tag | 860–960 MHz | Backscatter | ∞/zone | Passive/Active | Asset tracking |
| BLE 5 | < 100 m | Star/Mesh | 2.4 GHz | GFSK | 32 767 | Low | Tool tracking, beacons |
| ZigBee | 10–100 m | Mesh | 2.4 GHz / Sub-GHz | O-QPSK | 65 536 | Very Low | WSN |
| Z-Wave | < 30 m | Mesh | 868/908 MHz | GFSK | 232 | Low | Building automation |
| Modbus RTU | wired | Master–Slave | RS-485 | UART | 247 | N/A | PLC backbone |
| Wireless M-Bus | < 500 m | Star | 868 MHz | GFSK | 1000+ | Very Low | Smart metering |
| Wirepas | < 100 m | Mesh | 2.4 GHz / Sub-GHz | GFSK | Millions | Low | Lighting, metering |
| Dash7 | < 10 km | Star/Tree | 433/868/915 MHz | GFSK | 1000+ | Low | Cold chain |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IIoT Proximity Protocol Stack — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional stack** showing how proximity protocols interact with edge gateways and cloud applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cloudA[Cloud SCADA and Analytics Platform]
    edgeA[Industrial Edge Gateway OPC UA MQTT Broker]
    proto1[Modbus TCP and Modbus RTU]
    proto2[ZigBee Mesh Network Coordinator]
    proto3[BLE 5.0 Star and Mesh]
    proto4[Wireless M-Bus Collector]
    proto5[NFC and RFID Reader]
    proto6[Dash7 Sub GHz Gateway]
    proto7[Wirepas Decentralized Mesh]

    cloudA --> edgeA
    edgeA --> proto1
    edgeA --> proto2
    edgeA --> proto3
    edgeA --> proto4
    edgeA --> proto5
    edgeA --> proto6
    edgeA --> proto7

    proto1 --> devA[PLCs and VFDs]
    proto2 --> devB[Wireless Sensor Nodes]
    proto3 --> devC[Wearables and Tool Beacons]
    proto4 --> devD[Smart Meters]
    proto5 --> devE[Asset Tags]
    proto6 --> devF[Logistics Containers]
    proto7 --> devG[Smart Lighting Fixtures]
```

## 4.2 BLE Connection / Advertising State Machine

This diagram visualizes the **BLE link-layer state machine** — the most-tested internal behaviour in KTU viva and 3-mark questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> standbyState
    standbyState --> advertisingState : start advertising on 37 38 39
    advertisingState --> connectionState : scanner sends CONNECT_REQ
    advertisingState --> standbyState : timeout or disable
    connectionState --> connectedDataState : connection established
    connectedDataState --> connectionState : connection events 7.5 ms to 4 s
    connectedDataState --> standbyState : terminate or link loss
    connectionState --> standbyState : user terminate
    standbyState --> [*]
```

## 4.3 ZigBee Mesh Topology Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coord[Coordinator Gateway]
    r1[Router Node A]
    r2[Router Node B]
    r3[Router Node C]
    e1[End Device 1 Sleep]
    e2[End Device 2 Sleep]
    e3[End Device 3 Sleep]
    e4[End Device 4 Sleep]

    coord <--> r1
    coord <--> r2
    coord <--> r3
    r1 <--> r2
    r2 <--> r3
    r1 --- e1
    r1 --- e2
    r2 --- e3
    r3 --- e4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Routers (mains-powered) always maintain the mesh. End devices (battery-powered) attach to a "parent" router and sleep for >99% of the time. This is the structural reason ZigBee scales to thousands of low-power nodes.

## 4.4 Modbus Master-Slave Polling Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master PLC
    participant S1 as Slave Sensor 01
    participant S2 as Slave VFD 02
    participant S3 as Slave Meter 03

    M->>S1: Function 03 Read Holding Register
    S1-->>M: Response Register Data
    M->>S2: Function 06 Write Single Register
    S2-->>M: Write Acknowledge
    M->>S3: Function 04 Read Input Register
    S3-->>M: Response Telemetry Data
    Note over M: Polling cycle repeats every 100 ms
```

## 4.5 NFC Tag Commissioning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Unconfigured IIoT Sensor]
    tagA[NFC Tag Holds Bootstrap Credentials]
    tapA[Engineer Taps Phone to Sensor]
    readA[Reader Emits 13.56 MHz Field]
    xferA[Tag Modulates Field with Wi-Fi SSID and PSK]
    joinA[Sensor Connects to Plant Wi-Fi]
    authA[Mutual TLS to Cloud Platform]
    doneA[Sensor Reports Telemetry]

    startA --> tagA
    tagA --> tapA
    tapA --> readA
    readA --> xferA
    xferA --> joinA
    joinA --> authA
    authA --> doneA
```

---
