## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define an Industrial Gateway and list any four of its key functions.**

**Model Answer:**
An Industrial Gateway is a ruggedized device that bridges field-level industrial networks (Modbus, Profibus, CAN) with IP-based enterprise/cloud networks (MQTT, OPC-UA, HTTP). It performs **protocol translation, data aggregation, edge processing, and security enforcement**. Additionally, it provides buffering, time synchronization, and OT/IT network segmentation. It is the convergence point of the IIoT stack. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for any 4 functions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between an Industrial Gateway and a Commercial Router in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Industrial Gateway | Commercial Router |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer of Operation** | Application + Network (L3–L7) | Network (L3) only |
| **Protocol Awareness** | Understands Modbus, Profinet, OPC-UA | IP packet routing only |
| **Environment** | Ruggedized, −40°C to +75°C, EMC-hardened | Office/indoor environment |
| **Real-Time Capability** | Deterministic, sub-ms cycles | Best-effort |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of an Industrial Gateway with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Industrial Gateway architecture consists of **four layers**:

1. **Physical & Link Layer (L1–L2):** Provides electrical/optical interfaces (RS-485, RS-232, CAN, Ethernet, M-Bus). Includes galvanic isolation, surge arrestors, and EMC filters for harsh industrial environments. Implements bit-timing and bus arbitration (e.g., CSMA/CA in CAN).

2. **Field Protocol Stack (L3–L5):** Implements industrial protocols as master or slave. For Modbus RTU, it constructs request frames (Function Code 0x03 for read, 0x06 for write) and parses responses. For Profinet, it handles Real-Time (RT) and IRT (Isochronous Real-Time) classes with cycle times down to 250 μs.

3. **Middleware & Processing Engine:** Runs an RTOS (VxWorks, QNX) or embedded Linux (Yocto). Hosts containers (Docker, balenaCloud) for isolation, local time-series DB, rule engine, and scripting (Python, Lua, Node-RED).

4. **Northbound IT Interface:** Publishes data using IP-based protocols. MQTT is preferred for its lightweight pub-sub model and QoS levels. OPC-UA for industrial interoperability, CoAP for constrained devices. TLS 1.3 ensures confidentiality and integrity.

**Role of the architecture:** The layered design enables **separation of concerns** — field engineers can swap sensor protocols without affecting the cloud application, and IT teams can change the cloud broker without touching the OT side. This decoupling is essential for **maintainability, scalability, and cybersecurity** in Industry 4.0 plants.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 may be reproduced — 2 marks; layer-wise explanation — 5 marks.)*

---

**(b)** An Industrial Gateway polls 15 Modbus RTU devices at 9600 bps. Each transaction is 22 bytes including inter-frame gaps. The cycle period is 250 ms. Calculate: (i) Frame transmission time, (ii) Total bus traffic time, (iii) Bus utilization percentage. Comment on whether the bus is overloaded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Frame transmission time:**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{F \times 8}{R} = \frac{22 \times 8}{9600} = \frac{176}{9600}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 0.01833 \text{ s} = 18.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Total bus traffic time:**

$$
T_{total} = N \times T_{frame} = 15 \times 18.33 = 275 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Bus utilization percentage:**

$$
B_{util}(\%) = \frac{T_{total}}{T_c} \times 100 = \frac{275}{250} \times 100 = 110\%
$$

**Comment:** Since $B_{util} > 100\%$, the bus is **overloaded**. The gateway cannot complete polling within one cycle. To fix: either increase baud rate to **≥ 115200 bps** (which reduces $T_{frame}$ to ~1.53 ms, utilization drops to 9.2%), or reduce the number of devices polled, or extend $T_c$ to ≥ 300 ms.

*(3 marks for i, 2 marks for ii, 2 marks for iii; full marks require all steps.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the protocol conversion process in an Industrial Gateway when interfacing a Modbus RTU field device with an MQTT-based cloud broker. Include the data flow, payload structure, and QoS considerations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Data Flow Steps:**

1. **Modbus Request:** Gateway (master) sends `0x01 0x03 0x00 0x00 0x00 0x02 0xC4 0x0B` to slave ID 1, requesting 2 holding registers starting at address 0.

2. **Modbus Response:** Slave replies with `0x01 0x03 0x04 0x00 0x64 0x00 0xC8 0x5C 0x8B` — 4 bytes of data: 100 (0x0064) and 200 (0x00C8).

3. **Parsing & Scaling:** Registers are combined (big-endian) → 32-bit integer. If register 0 is temperature in 0.1°C units → 100 × 0.1 = 10.0°C; register 1 is pressure in kPa → 200 kPa.

4. **JSON Payload Construction:**

```json
{
  "deviceId": "TX-01",
  "timestamp": "2024-07-15T10:30:00.123Z",
  "values": {
    "temperature_c": 10.0,
    "pressure_kpa": 200
  },
  "quality": "Good"
}
```

5. **MQTT Publish:**

```
Topic: factory/line1/tx01/telemetry
Payload: <JSON above>
QoS: 1 (at least once)
Retain: false
```

6. **Broker Acknowledgment:** Broker sends PUBACK to gateway. Subscriber (cloud dashboard) receives message.

**QoS Considerations:**

- **QoS 0:** Fire-and-forget; minimum overhead; suitable for high-frequency, loss-tolerant data (e.g., vibration streaming).
- **QoS 1:** At least once; PUBACK handshake; suitable for **telemetry, alerts, KPIs** (most common in IIoT).
- **QoS 2:** Exactly once; 4-step handshake; expensive; used for **billing-grade metering, regulatory alarms**.

**Payload Format Choice:** JSON is human-readable but verbose (~150% overhead vs CBOR). For bandwidth-constrained LPWAN gateways, **CBOR or Protocol Buffers** are preferred.

*(Diagram carries 2 marks; step-wise explanation 3 marks; QoS discussion 2 marks.)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the cybersecurity threats specific to Industrial Gateways and the countermeasures deployed at each architectural layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Threats:**

1. **Unauthorized Device Impersonation:** Attacker spoofs a Modbus slave ID and injects false sensor readings (e.g., fake "all-clear" temperature to hide an overheating reactor).
2. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Interception of MQTT traffic between gateway and cloud.
3. **Firmware Tampering:** Malicious firmware update pushed to the gateway.
4. **DoS / DDoS:** Flooding the gateway with Modbus requests, exhausting the bus.
5. **Lateral Movement:** Compromised gateway used as pivot to reach the OT/ICS network.
6. **Replay Attacks:** Re-sending old valid Modbus commands to repeat unauthorized actions.

**Countermeasures (Layer-wise):**

| Layer | Countermeasure |
|---|---|
| **Field (OT) Side** | MAC whitelisting, port-level access control, Modbus Security (Modbus/TLS using TLS 1.3) |
| **Gateway Middleware** | Signed firmware, secure boot (TPM/SE), role-based access control (RBAC), SELinux/AppArmor sandboxes |
| **Northbound (IT) Side** | TLS 1.3 for MQTT/HTTPS, X.509 mutual authentication, certificate pinning, IPsec VPN to cloud |
| **Network Layer** | VLAN segmentation, DMZ between OT and IT (per Purdue Model), stateful firewall with deep packet inspection (DPI) for Modbus/DNP3 |
| **Monitoring** | SIEM integration, anomaly detection (e.g., sudden spike in Modbus writes = attack signature) |
| **Physical** | Tamper-evident enclosures, lockable ports, hardware root-of-trust |

**Standards Compliance:** IEC 62443 (Industrial Automation & Control Systems Cybersecurity), NIST 800-82, ISA-99.

*(2 marks for threats, 3 marks for layer-wise countermeasures, 2 marks for standards.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Gateway" with "Router" or "Switch":** Always emphasize *protocol translation at the application layer* and *OT/IT convergence*. Generic networking definitions fetch 0 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Numericals:** Every calculated latency, throughput, and buffer size must carry its unit (ms, bps, samples, KB). Missing units → 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. **Omitting Block Diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions:** Even a hand-drawn ASCII or Mermaid-equivalent sketch earns 1–2 marks.
> 4. **One-sided discussion of security:** Mention **both** OT and IT sides; references to **IEC 62443 / Purdue Model** are high-value keywords.
> 5. **Skipping comments in numerical answers:** Always close with a feasibility/comment sentence — e.g., "Utilization 110% indicates overload; recommend baud rate increase."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **Industrial Gateway** is **not a router** — it performs **application-layer protocol translation** between OT (Modbus, Profibus, CAN) and IT (MQTT, OPC-UA, HTTP) networks.
- Key functions: **Protocol translation, aggregation, edge analytics, buffering, security, segmentation**.
- Industrial gateways are characterized by **ruggedization**: −40°C to +75°C, EMC compliance (IEC 61850-3), high MTBF (≥ 200,000 h).
- **Bus utilization formula** $B_{util} = \frac{N \cdot T_{frame}}{T_{cycle}}$ must be < 80% for headroom.
- **End-to-end latency** is the sum of $L_{poll} + L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{tx}$; for vibration/predictive maintenance, must be ≤ 100 ms.
- **MQTT QoS levels**: QoS 0 (fire-forget), QoS 1 (at least once — most common), QoS 2 (exactly once — metering).
- **Buffer size** for outage survival: $M = f_s \times T_{outage,max}$.
- Gateway classes: **Light (per-machine)**, **Mid (per-cell)**, **Heavy Edge Server (per-plant)** — choice depends on protocol count, AI workload, and real-time requirements.
- **Cybersecurity standards**: IEC 62443, NIST 800-82, Purdue Model (Levels 0–5).
- **Northbound protocols** dominate the IT side: MQTT, OPC-UA, CoAP, HTTP/REST.
- **Southbound protocols** dominate the OT side: Modbus, Profibus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, BACnet, CAN, DNP3.
- Always mention the **Purdue/ISA-95 model** when explaining the gateway's position — it sits at **Level 2–3 boundary**.
- Remember the **derivation pattern**: write the formula → substitute values → evaluate → **comment on engineering significance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT755 - INDUSTRIAL IOT APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is a specialized **web transfer protocol** for use with **constrained nodes and constrained networks** in the Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem, formally standardized by the **IETF** under **RFC 7252** (released in June 2014). CoAP is designed to provide a **RESTful (Representational State Transfer) architectural style** of communication between resource-constrained devices (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers). It achieves this by mimicking the **HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** semantics—using familiar methods such as `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`—but re-engineering the transport mechanism to operate efficiently over **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** instead of TCP, drastically reducing header overhead, power consumption, and bandwidth requirements. > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric — Header Size:** CoAP uses a **4-byte base header** (expandable to 32 bytes with optional fields) compared to HTTP/1.1 headers that often exceed **1–2 KB** per request. This is a reduction of approximately **400×** in header footprint. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a busy post office that handles millions of letters daily. **HTTP is like registered post with multiple forms, signatures, tracking receipts, and confirmation slips** — extremely reliable but very heavy on paperwork. A tiny temperature sensor in a remote factory does not have the energy, memory, or bandwidth to fill out all that paperwork for a single temperature reading every few minutes. **CoAP is the equivalent of a smart short-message-service (SMS) with a tracking ID and an optional return-receipt option.** The sensor simply packs the data into a small 4-byte envelope, optionally asks for a confirmation, and sends it. If a confirmation is not received, it tries again. This makes CoAP a perfect compromise between **reliability** and **efficiency** for IoT. ## 1.3 Core Design Goals of CoAP > [!NOTE] > The four pillars that CoAP is designed to satisfy, as per RFC 7252: 1. **Fulfilling the REST architecture** for constrained devices (compatible with HTTP at the application layer). 2. **Low header overhead** to minimize parsing complexity and bandwidth usage. 3. **Asynchronous transaction support** — handling many concurrent requests on low-power devices. 4. **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) and content-type support** — enabling easy integration with web services. ## 1.4 Where CoAP Fits in the IoT Protocol Stack | Layer | Protocol | |---|---| | Application | **CoAP** (RFC 7252) | | Transport | UDP (typically port **5683**) | | Security (optional) | DTLS (Datagram TLS) | | Network | IPv6 / RPL / 6LoWPAN | | Link / Physical | IEEE 802.15.4 / LoRaWAN / BLE | ## 1.5 The CoRE (Constrained RESTful Environments) Link Format > [!IMPORTANT] > CoAP devices typically advertise their resources using the **CoRE Link Format** (RFC 6690). This allows a CoAP client to discover all available resources on a server via a well-known URI: `/.well-known/core`. > > Example discovery response: > ``` > ;rt="temperature";if="sensor", > ;rt="actuator";if="switch" > ``` # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 CoAP Message Format (RFC 7252 Specification) A CoAP message is encoded in a **binary format** to ensure minimal overhead. It is composed of three main parts: a **4-byte base header**, **optional fields**, and a **payload**. > [!NOTE] > **Base Header Structure (32 bits / 4 bytes):** > > | Bits | Field | Width | Description | > |---|---|---|---| > | 0–1 | **Ver (Version)** | 2 bits | Protocol version, currently set to **1** | > | 2–3 | **T (Type)** | 2 bits | Message type: CON, NON, ACK, RST | > | 4–7 | **TKL (Token Length)** | 4 bits | Token length, **0–8 bytes** | > | 8–15 | **Code** | 8 bits | Request/Response code (class.detail) | > | 16–31 | **Message ID** | 16 bits | Duplicate-detection identifier | ## 2.2 The Four CoAP Message Types > [!IMPORTANT] > CoAP defines **four reliability modes** to give developers fine-grained control over transmission behavior. This is one of the most frequently tested areas in KTU exams. 1. **CON — Confirmable (Type = 0)** - Requires an **ACK** from the receiver. - Implements a **stop-and-wait** reliability mechanism with **exponential back-off retransmission**. - Default initial timeout: **2 seconds (2000 ms)**. Each retry doubles the timeout (4s, 8s, 16s, …). - Default max retransmissions: **4** (configurable). 2. **NON — Non-Confirmable (Type = 1)** - **Fire-and-forget.** No ACK required. Used for repeated sensor data (e.g., temperature readings) where losing one packet is acceptable. 3. **ACK — Acknowledgment (Type = 2)** - Confirms receipt of a CON message or carries a response to a CON request. 4. **RST — Reset (Type = 3)** - Indicates the receiver context is **missing** (e.g., the server was restarted and forgot the in-flight request). ## 2.3 Code Field — Class.Detail Encoding The 8-bit **Code** field uses the format **c.dd**, where: - `c` = the **class** (3 bits, top byte), e.g., 0=OK-like, 2=Success, 4=Client Error, 5=Server Error. - `dd` = the **detail** (5 bits, bottom byte). > [!NOTE] > **Memorize this table — it is a KTU-favorite question:** | Code | Class Detail | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 0.01 | 0.01 | GET request method | | 0.02 | 0.02 | POST request method | | 0.03 | 0.03 | PUT request method | | 0.04 | 0.04 | DELETE request method | | 2.01 | 2.01 | Created (POST/PUT success) | | 2.02 | 2.02 | Deleted (DELETE success) | | 2.03 | 2.03 | Valid (ETag matches) | | 2.04 | 2.04 | Changed (PUT success) | | 2.05 | 2.05 | Content (GET success) | | 4.00 | 4.00 | Bad Request | | 4.04 | 4.04 | Not Found | | 5.00 | 5.00 | Internal Server Error | | 5.01 | 5.01 | Not Implemented | ## 2.4 Reliability — Stop-and-Wait with Exponential Back-off > [!IMPORTANT] > CoAP's reliability model is **borrowed from the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer's CSMA/CA behavior** but adapted for the application layer. The retransmission timeout follows the equation: $$ T_{n} = T_{initial} \times 2^{(n-1)} $$ where: - $T_n$ = Timeout before the $n$-th retransmission - $T_{initial}$ = Initial timeout, default = **2 s** - $n$ = Retransmission attempt number A **random jitter** (slight randomization) is added to each timeout to avoid the *thundering-herd* problem when many devices reset simultaneously. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Quick-reference engineering values and formulas for CoAP:** | Parameter / Concept | Value / Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Default CoAP UDP Port | **5683** | CoAPs (DTLS-secured) = **5684** | | Base Header Size | **4 bytes** (32 bits) | Expandable up to **~32 bytes** with options | | Token Field Size | **0–8 bytes** | Length specified in TKL nibble | | Message ID Size | **16 bits** | Range: 0 to **65,535** (cyclic) | | Max Concurrent Messages | $2^{16}$ per endpoint | Limited by Message ID space | | Initial Retransmission Timeout | $T_0 = \mathbf{2\ s}$ | Configurable in implementation | | Back-off Multiplier | **2** | Doubles each retry | | Max Retransmissions (default) | **4** | Total: 5 attempts (1 + 4) | | Option Delta Width Encoding | 4 bits, **0–2 bytes** | Self-delimiting numeric encoding | | Payload Marker | **0xFF** byte | One byte before payload | | Max Option Count | **12** in default header, extensible | Up to 2 bytes delta/length | ## 2.6 Options — The CoAP Way of Carrying Metadata > [!IMPORTANT] > CoAP uses **Type-Length-Value (TLV)** encoded options, similar in spirit to IPv4 options. There are **10 standard options** defined in RFC 7252: | Option Number | Name | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | If-Match | ETag for conditional requests | | 3 | Uri-Host | Hostname of the resource | | 5 | Uri-Path | Path segment of the resource URI | | 7 | Uri-Port | UDP port of the resource | | 8 | Uri-Path | Path segment | | 11 | Uri-Path | Path segment | | 12 | Content-Format | MIME type of payload (e.g., application/json = 50) | | 15 | Uri-Query | Query string parameter | | 17 | Accept | Acceptable content formats | | 35 | Proxy-Uri | Full URI for proxy requests | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility In **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** deployments, CoAP is used for: - **Smart energy metering** — sending 15-minute load profiles over LPWAN. - **Predictive maintenance** — vibration sensors streaming to a CoAP-to-HTTP gateway. - **Building automation** — BACnet-over-CoAP bridges for HVAC control. - **Smart agriculture** — soil moisture and pH sensors in field deployments where cellular backhaul is expensive. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Time Before Final Failure on a CON Message The KTU board sometimes asks students to calculate the **worst-case delivery latency** of a CoAP Confirmable message. Let us derive this rigorously. > [!NOTE] > **Assumptions (RFC 7252 defaults):** > - $T_0 = 2\ \text{s}$ (initial ACK timeout) > - $N = 4$ (maximum retransmissions) > - Random jitter ignored (deterministic worst case) ### Step 1 — Express the $n$-th timeout The retransmission timeout before the $n$-th retry is: $$ T_n = T_0 \cdot 2^{(n-1)} $$ ### Step 2 — Total wait time across all retries Total time until the client gives up (i.e., after $N$ failed retransmissions) is the **sum of all per-retry timeouts**: $$ T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} T_n = \sum_{n=1}^{N} T_0 \cdot 2^{(n-1)} $$ ### Step 3 — Recognize the geometric series The sum forms a finite **geometric series** with first term $T_0$ and common ratio $r = 2$: $$ S = T_0 \cdot \frac{(r^N - 1)}{r - 1} = T_0 \cdot (2^N - 1) $$ ### Step 4 — Plug in the defaults Substitute $T_0 = 2\ \text{s}$ and $N = 4$: $$ T_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot (2^4 - 1) = 2 \cdot 15 = 30\ \text{s} $$ ### Step 5 — State the engineering interpretation > The protocol will give up trying to deliver a Confirmable message after a maximum of **30 seconds** of network silence. After this point, the application layer is notified of a failure, allowing it to take corrective action (e.g., mark the sensor as offline, switch to a cached value, or trigger an alarm). ### Step 3.1.1 — Extended Analytical Matrix: Comparative Protocol Analysis The following table is a **high-yield analytical comparison** that frequently appears in KTU 14-mark questions: | Feature | **HTTP/1.1** | **CoAP** | **MQTT** | |---|---|---|---| | Transport | TCP | UDP (default) | TCP | | Header Size | Variable, often **>500 B** | **4–32 B** | **2 B** (fixed) | | Messaging | Request–Response | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe | | Reliability | TCP guarantees | Confirmable ACK | QoS 0/1/2 | | Security | TLS | DTLS | TLS | | Power | High | Low | Medium | | Best for | Web browsers | Sensor/Actuator | Many-to-many telemetry | | Standards | RFC 7230+ | RFC 7252 | OASIS Standard | | Resource Discovery | HTTP headers | **CoRE Link Format** | Topic-based | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Decoding a Sample CoAP Message Suppose we capture the following hex bytes from the wire: ``` 40 01 00 01 B4 35 74 65 6D 70 FF 44 32 35 ``` We decode this byte-by-byte to verify our understanding. ### Step 1 — Decode the first 4-byte header $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Byte 0:} &\quad \texttt{0x40} = 0100\,0000_2 \\ \text{Version} &= 01 \quad (\text{version } = 1) \\ \text{Type} &= 00 \quad (\text{CON}) \\ \text{TKL} &= 0000 \quad (\text{no token, 0 bytes}) \\ \\ \text{Byte 1:} &\quad \texttt{0x01} = 0000\,0001_2 \\ \text{Code} &= 0.01 \quad (\text{GET method}) \\ \\ \text{Bytes 2–3:} &\quad \texttt{0x00 0x01} \\ \text{Message ID} &= 0x0001 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Decode the option The next byte is `0xB4`: $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{0xB4} &= 1011\,0100_2 \\ \text{Delta} &= 1011_2 = 11 \quad (\text{Option 11} = \text{Uri-Path}) \\ \text{Length} &= 0100_2 = 4 \quad (\text{4-byte value follows}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Decode the value The 4 bytes `74 65 6D 70` spell out the ASCII string `temp`. ### Step 4 — Recognize the payload marker The byte `0xFF` is the **payload marker**, signalling that everything after is payload data. ### Step 5 — Decode the payload The bytes `44 32 35` represent the ASCII string `D25` (e.g., a temperature reading of 25.0 °C). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The entire **complete request + response-style payload** consumed only **12 bytes** on the wire. The same data via HTTP would require a full TCP connection (3-way handshake), a multi-line HTTP header, and likely more than **200 bytes** total. This is the **engineering motivation** for CoAP in constrained IIoT. ## 3.3 Token-Based Asynchronous Transaction Matching CoAP clients and servers can have **multiple in-flight transactions simultaneously**. The **Token** field (0–8 bytes) is used to match an asynchronous response back to its original request. > [!NOTE] > The Token is randomly generated by the client. The server **echoes** the same Token in its response. The client uses the Token to look up the original request context from its local transaction table. > > Token is **independent** of Message ID. Message ID is for **duplicate detection at the message layer**; Token is for **transaction matching at the application layer**. The same Message ID can theoretically be reused for a different transaction once the original is complete, while a Token can remain valid for the duration of the application-level exchange. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CoAP Message Format — Bit-Level Structural Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HDR["HEADER 4 BYTES"] A["Ver: 2 bits
= 01"] B["Type: 2 bits
CON/NON/ACK/RST"] C["TKL: 4 bits
0 to 8"] D["Code: 8 bits
class.detail"] E["Message ID: 16 bits
0 to 65535"] end F["Token: 0 to 8 bytes
client-defined correlation ID"] G["Options: TLV encoded
delta + length + value"] H["Payload Marker: 1 byte
0xFF if payload present"] I["Payload: 0 to N bytes
sensor data, JSON, CBOR"] HDR --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I ``` ## 4.2 CoAP Confirmable Transaction Flow (CON/ACK) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as CoAP Client participant S as CoAP Server Note over C,S: 1. Client sends CON GET /temp
Message ID = 0x0001 C->>S: CON [GET] MID=1 Token=A1 Note over S: 2. Server processes request S->>C: ACK [2.05 Content] MID=1 Token=A1 Payload="25.0C" Note over C: 3. Transaction complete
Message ID 0x0001 is free Note over C,S: 4. Network loss scenario C->>S: CON [GET] MID=2 Token=B2 Note right of S: Packet lost in transit Note over C: 5. ACK Timeout: T0 = 2 s C->>S: CON [GET] MID=2 Token=B2 (Retry 1) S->>C: ACK [2.05 Content] MID=2 Token=B2 ``` ## 4.3 CoAP Non-Confirmable Transaction (Fire-and-Forget) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as IoT Sensor
Non-Confirmable participant GW as CoAP Gateway Note over Sensor,GW: Periodic telemetry stream
No ACK expected Sensor->>GW: NON [POST] /temp {value: 25.1} Sensor->>GW: NON [POST] /temp {value: 25.3} Sensor->>GW: NON [POST] /temp {value: 25.5} Note over GW: Gateway tolerates
occasional loss ``` ## 4.4 CoAP Block-Wise Transfer (RFC 7959) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Large Resource
e.g., 4 KB firmware image"] --> B["Block 1: 512 bytes
Block Option: 0/0/256"] B --> C["Block 2: 512 bytes
Block Option: 1/0/256"] C --> D["Block 3: 512 bytes
Block Option: 2/0/256"] D --> E["Block 4: 512 bytes
Block Option: 3/0/256
More-flag = 0"] F["Sender
512-byte chunks"] -.-> A E -.-> G["Receiver
reassembles full image"] ``` ## 4.5 CoAP Observe Pattern (RFC 7641) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as CoAP Client participant S as CoAP Server / Sensor Note over C,S: 1. Client registers interest C->>S: CON GET /temp Observe:0 Token=R1 S->>C: ACK [2.05] Observe:0 Token=R1 Payload="25.0" Note over S: 2. Resource changes value S->>C: NON [2.05] Observe:10 Token=R1 Payload="26.5" S->>C: NON [2.05] Observe:11 Token=R1 Payload="27.0" Note over C: 3. Client terminates subscription C->>S: CON GET /temp Observe:1 Token=R1 S->>C: ACK ``` ## 4.6 CoAP Protocol Stack Position in IoT ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["Application Layer"] X1["CoAP / HTTP / MQTT"] end subgraph SEC["Security Layer"] X2["DTLS / TLS"] end subgraph TR["Transport Layer"] X3["UDP / TCP"] end subgraph NW["Network Layer"] X4["IPv6 / RPL / 6LoWPAN"] end subgraph LL["Link / Physical Layer"] X5["IEEE 802.15.4 / LoRa / BLE / Wi-Fi"] end APP --> SEC SEC --> TR TR --> NW NW --> LL ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define CoAP. Mention the RFC number and the default port on which it operates.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is a specialized web transfer protocol defined by the **IETF in RFC 7252**, designed for resource-constrained IoT devices and networks. It provides a RESTful communication model using methods similar to HTTP (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) but operates over **UDP** to minimize overhead. The default UDP port for unsecured CoAP is **5683**, while the DTLS-secured variant **CoAPs** uses port **5684**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for RFC, 1 mark for port.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] List the four CoAP message types and explain the difference between CON and NON.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four CoAP message types are: 1. **CON** (Confirmable) — requires an ACK. 2. **NON** (Non-Confirmable) — no ACK expected. 3. **ACK** (Acknowledgment) — confirms a CON or carries a response. 4. **RST** (Reset) — indicates context loss at the receiver. **Difference:** A **CON** message implements reliable delivery through stop-and-wait ACK with exponential back-off and is retransmitted on timeout, suitable for critical control commands. A **NON** message is fire-and-forget with no retransmission, used for periodic sensor readings where occasional loss is acceptable. *(1 mark for listing, 1 mark for CON, 1 mark for NON.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks) > **ESE Module Question — Module 3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the CoAP message format. Describe the fields Ver, Type, TKL, Code, and Message ID. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the CoAP Confirmable message exchange with the exponential back-off algorithm. A CoAP client sends a CON message with initial timeout **2 seconds** and the default maximum retransmissions. Calculate the total time before the client declares the transmission as failed. *(7 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare CoAP, HTTP, and MQTT in terms of transport, header overhead, reliability, and best-fit IIoT use cases. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the CoAP **Observe** pattern (RFC 7641) and the **Block-wise transfer** mechanism (RFC 7959) with suitable diagrams. Why are these extensions important for industrial IoT? *(7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Step-by-Step Model Solutions ### Solution to Question A(a) The CoAP message format consists of **three sections**: a **4-byte base header**, **optional fields** (Token + Options), and an **optional payload**. **Header Fields:** 1. **Ver (2 bits):** Protocol version, currently fixed to `01` (decimal **1**). Receivers reject any other value. 2. **Type (2 bits):** Identifies the message type — - `00` = CON (Confirmable) - `01` = NON (Non-Confirmable) - `10` = ACK (Acknowledgment) - `11` = RST (Reset) 3. **TKL (4 bits):** Token Length — indicates the number of bytes (0–8) that follow the header as a Token. 4. **Code (8 bits):** Encoded as **class.detail** in the format `c.dd` (e.g., `0.01` = GET, `2.05` = Content). 5. **Message ID (16 bits):** A unique identifier used for **duplicate detection** and matching CON/ACK pairs. After the header, the **Token** (0–8 bytes) provides application-level transaction correlation. **Options** are encoded in TLV form for URI components, content formats, and other metadata. The **Payload** (if present) is preceded by a single marker byte `0xFF`. *(Allocate 2 marks for the diagram, 5 marks for field explanations.)* --- ### Solution to Question A(b) The **Confirmable message exchange** works as follows: 1. The client sends a CON message and starts a **retransmission timer** set to the initial timeout $T_0 = 2\ \text{s}$. 2. If an ACK is received within the timer, the transaction is complete. 3. If the timer expires, the client **retransmits** the same CON message and **doubles the timeout** (exponential back-off). 4. After the maximum number of retransmissions, the application is notified of failure. **Calculation of total failure time:** Using the geometric series formula derived earlier: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{total}} &= T_0 \cdot (2^N - 1) \\ &= 2 \cdot (2^4 - 1) \\ &= 2 \cdot 15 \\ &= 30\ \text{seconds} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **subtract 1** inside the parentheses, or they confuse the **number of retries** with the **number of transmissions**. The default is **4 retries = 5 total transmission attempts**, so $N = 4$ in the formula. Writing $N = 5$ will cost 1 mark. **Engineering significance:** The 30-second bound gives an upper limit on the latency for any reliable IIoT control command, which is critical when designing **safety-instrumented systems** (e.g., emergency shut-off valves triggered over CoAP). *(Allocate 4 marks for the algorithm explanation, 3 marks for the calculation.)* --- ### Solution to Question B(a) The comparison table is provided in **Section 3.1.1** of these notes. Students should reproduce it cleanly and add **2–3 lines of explanation** per protocol on best-fit use cases: - **HTTP** is best for **human-facing dashboards** and web applications where browsers need to access the data. - **CoAP** is best for **device-to-device** constrained communication, RESTful sensor/actuator APIs, and LPWAN/6LoWPAN networks. - **MQTT** is best for **many-to-many pub/sub telemetry** with broker-based fan-out, ideal for fleet management. *(Allocate 1 mark per protocol for the four parameters = 4 marks, plus 3 marks for use-case justification.)* --- ### Solution to Question B(b) **CoAP Observe (RFC 7641):** Allows a CoAP client to **subscribe** to a resource on a server. The server then **pushes** notifications whenever the resource state changes, using a sequence number in the `Observe` option. The subscription is cancelled by sending a new GET with `Observe: 1` (deregistration). This mimics the publish-subscribe model but keeps the RESTful semantics. **Block-wise Transfer (RFC 7959):** CoAP's default UDP datagram has a practical size limit (~1024 bytes to avoid IP fragmentation). To transfer large resources (firmware updates, configuration files), the payload is split into **blocks** (default block size = 256, 512, or 1024 bytes), each carrying a `Block1` or `Block2` option indicating block number, more-flag, and block size. **Importance for IIoT:** - **Observe** enables push-based monitoring without polling, drastically reducing radio-on-time on battery-powered field sensors. - **Block-wise transfer** enables **firmware-over-the-air (FOTA) updates** to constrained devices without requiring a TCP connection. *(Allocate 3 marks for Observe, 3 marks for Block-wise, 1 mark for IIoT significance.)* --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in CoAP questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Token and Message ID.** Token = application-level correlation (echoed by server). Message ID = transport-level duplicate detection. Conflating them costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the version field.** Many students write the CoAP header as having only 3 fields (Type, Code, Message ID). The **Ver** field is mandatory. > 3. **Wrong code interpretation.** `0.01` is GET, **not** POST. Mixing these up is a common slip. > 4. **Skipping the payload marker `0xFF`.** Examiners expect a one-line mention of this when discussing the message format. > 5. **Not mentioning the default port (5683).** This is a 1-mark giveaway that is often missed. > 6. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts.** A labelled CoAP header diagram is worth at least 2 marks in any 7-mark "explain the message format" question. ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for CoAP — High-Density Summary:** > > - CoAP is defined in **RFC 7252** and runs on **UDP port 5683** (5684 for CoAPs over DTLS). > - It provides **RESTful** HTTP-like methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for **constrained IoT devices**. > - The base header is **4 bytes** with 5 fields: **Ver (2b)**, **Type (2b)**, **TKL (4b)**, **Code (8b)**, **Message ID (16b)**. > - **Four message types:** CON, NON, ACK, RST. > - **CON uses exponential back-off** with $T_0 = 2\ \text{s}$, doubling, and max 4 retries → **30 s total timeout**. > - **Code field format** is `class.detail` (e.g., `0.01` = GET, `2.05` = Content, `4.04` = Not Found). > - **Token** (0–8 bytes) is for **application-level request/response matching**; **Message ID** is for **duplicate detection**. > - **Options** carry URI components, content-format, and metadata in **TLV encoding**. > - The **payload marker** is the magic byte **0xFF**. > - **CoRE Link Format (RFC 6690)** enables resource discovery via `/.well-known/core`. > - **Observe (RFC 7641)** provides pub/sub-style push notifications with sequence numbering. > - **Block-wise transfer (RFC 7959)** allows large payloads (e.g., firmware) to be split into 256/512/1024-byte blocks. > - **DTLS** is the security layer, providing confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. > - CoAP is best suited for **machine-to-machine (M2M)** constrained communication; MQTT for pub/sub telemetry; HTTP for human-facing web dashboards.

topics.title: Publish/Subscribe Pattern: MQTT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT755 - INDUSTRIAL IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Publish/Subscribe Pattern: MQTT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is an OASIS-standardized, lightweight **Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) messaging protocol** designed specifically for resource-constrained IoT devices and unreliable, low-bandwidth, high-latency, or expensive networks. Originally invented by Dr. Andy Stanford-Clark (IBM) and Arlen Nipper (Eurotech) in **1999** for linking oil pipeline telemetry systems over satellite links, it operates over TCP/IP (typically port **1883** for unencrypted and **8883** for TLS-encrypted communication).

The **Publish/Subscribe Pattern** is an asynchronous messaging architecture that decouples message producers (**Publishers**) from message consumers (**Subscribers**). Communication is mediated by a central message broker, and message routing is based on a logical addressing scheme known as a **Topic** rather than a physical device address. This decoupling is the cornerstone of scalable Industrial IoT (IIoT) data distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MQTT is the *de facto* standard messaging protocol for IIoT and is part of the official Module 4 syllabus of PECIT755. Students must master the architecture, packet structure, QoS levels, and broker logic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **newspaper publishing system**:
- A **Publisher** is a journalist who writes an article (data) and hands it to the newspaper's central office.
- The newspaper's **central office** is the **Broker** — it receives, stores, filters, and routes articles.
- A **Subscriber** is a reader who subscribes to specific topics (e.g., "Sports", "Weather", "Finance"). The broker only delivers articles whose topic matches the reader's subscription.
- The journalist **never knows** who is reading the article, and the reader **never knows** who wrote it. They are completely *decoupled*. The broker is the only point of contact.

In MQTT terms:
- The journalist is the **IoT sensor** publishing temperature data.
- The central office is the **MQTT Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX).
- The reader is the **dashboard or analytics engine** subscribed to the topic `factory/floor1/temperature`.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Entities (KTU Board Focus)

| Entity | Role | Industrial Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Publisher** | Generates and sends messages tagged with a Topic | PLC writing sensor data |
| **Subscriber** | Receives messages by expressing interest in Topics | SCADA dashboard |
| **Broker** | Central server that filters, queues, and forwards messages | Industrial gateway / middleware |
| **Topic** | Hierarchical UTF-8 string used to route messages | File path in a database |
| **Client** | Any device running an MQTT library (publisher, subscriber, or both) | Edge sensor, actuator, or HMI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoupling Dimensions in MQTT:**
> 1. **Space Decoupling** — Publisher and Subscriber do not need to know each other's IP/MAC.
> 2. **Time Decoupling** — Broker can queue messages for offline subscribers (with QoS & persistent sessions).
> 3. **Synchronization Decoupling** — Both sides operate asynchronously; neither blocks the other.

## 1.4 The MQTT Topic Tree (Hierarchical Addressing)

Topics are slash-delimited (`/`) UTF-8 strings forming a logical tree. Example used in a smart factory:

```
factory/floor1/machineA/temperature
factory/floor1/machineA/vibration
factory/floor1/machineB/temperature
factory/floor2/+/temperature       (Wildcard - all floor 2 machine temps)
factory/+/machineA/#              (Wildcard - all machineA data on any floor)
```

| Wildcard | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Single-level | `+` | Matches exactly one topic level |
| Multi-level | `#` | Matches zero or more remaining levels (must be last) |

> [!WARNING]
> Publishers must **never** use wildcards in their publish topic — wildcards are *exclusively* a subscription feature. This is a common KTU exam pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pub/Sub over Request/Response in IIoT?

In classical HTTP **Request/Response** (Client-Server), a sensor must repeatedly poll a server. This wastes bandwidth, battery, and CPU. In contrast, **Pub/Sub** allows:

- **Push-based delivery** — broker pushes data the moment it arrives.
- **Many-to-many** — one publisher can serve thousands of subscribers without modification.
- **Asynchronous operation** — ideal for sleeping IoT nodes (e.g., battery-powered vibration sensor waking every 5 minutes).
- **Network resilience** — QoS mechanisms guarantee delivery even over flaky LPWAN/mesh networks.

## 2.2 The MQTT Control Packet Structure

Every MQTT packet (PDU) contains a fixed header and an optional variable header + payload. The fixed header is **2 bytes minimum**.

| Byte 1 (Control Packet Type + Flags) | Byte 2+ (Remaining Length) |
|---|---|
| 4-bit type + 4-bit flags | Variable-length encoded (1–4 bytes) |

**Control Packet Types (the 14 MQTT packet types):**

| Type (Hex) | Name | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x10` | CONNECT | C→B | Client joins broker |
| `0x20` | CONNACK | B→C | Connection acknowledged |
| `0x30` | PUBLISH | C↔B | Send message |
| `0x40` | PUBACK | Both | QoS 1 acknowledgment |
| `0x50` | PUBREC | Both | QoS 2 step 1 |
| `0x60` | PUBREL | Both | QoS 2 step 2 |
| `0x70` | PUBCOMP | Both | QoS 2 step 3 |
| `0x80` | SUBSCRIBE | C→B | Subscribe to topic(s) |
| `0x90` | SUBACK | B→C | Subscription acknowledged |
| `0xA0` | UNSUBSCRIBE | C→B | Unsubscribe |
| `0xB0` | UNSUBACK | B→C | Unsub acknowledged |
| `0xC0` | PINGREQ | C→B | Keep-alive request |
| `0xD0` | PINGRESP | B→C | Keep-alive response |
| `0xE0` | DISCONNECT | C→B | Clean disconnect |
| `0xF0` | AUTH | C↔B | Authentication exchange (v5.0) |

## 2.3 The Three Quality of Service (QoS) Levels

This is the **most heavily tested MQTT concept in KTU exams**.

| QoS Level | Acronym | Guarantee | Handshake | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget, no ACK | None | Sensor telemetry where loss is tolerable |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Guaranteed delivery, possible **duplicates** | PUBLISH → PUBACK | Critical actuator commands (valve close) |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Guaranteed, **no duplicates** | PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP | Billing, regulatory, safety interlocks |

## 2.4 Other Critical Flags & Features

- **RETAIN Flag**: When set on a PUBLISH packet, the broker stores the *last known good value* of that topic and immediately delivers it to any new subscriber. Essential for state-aware sensors (e.g., "current pump status").
- **Clean Session Flag** (in CONNECT): `true` = non-persistent; `false` = persistent session with offline message queuing.
- **Will Message (LWT — Last Will and Testament)**: A pre-configured message the broker publishes on behalf of a client if it disconnects *unexpectedly* (no DISCONNECT received). Used to detect dead IoT nodes.
- **Keep Alive Timer**: Client sends PINGREQ at most 1.5× the negotiated interval; broker disconnects otherwise.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" in MQTT are predominantly **engineering heuristics** for sizing brokers, bandwidth, and latency.

| Quantity | Formula / Metric | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth per message** | $B = H + L$ | bytes | $H$ = header overhead (2–4 B), $L$ = payload length |
| **Total broker throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_m \cdot B}{t}$ | bytes/s | $N_m$ = messages/sec, $t$ = window |
| **Round-trip latency for QoS 1** | $RTT_1 = 2 \cdot t_p + t_{proc}$ | seconds | $t_p$ = propagation, $t_{proc}$ = broker processing |
| **QoS 2 total handshake latency** | $RTT_2 = 4 \cdot t_p + 4 \cdot t_{proc}$ | seconds | 4-way handshake |
| **Keep-alive interval** | $K \in [0, 65535]$ | seconds | 0 = disables keep-alive |
| **Maximum QoS 2 throughput** | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1}{RTT_2}$ | msgs/s | Theoretical ceiling |
| **Max concurrent clients per broker** | $C_{max} = \dfrac{Memory_{broker}}{S_{client}}$ | clients | $S_{client} \approx 32$ KB per session |
| **In-flight message cap (per client)** | $I_{max} = 65535$ | messages | MQTT v3.1.1; v5 raises to 16-bit value |
| **Maximum topic length** | $L_{topic} = 65535$ | bytes | UTF-8 encoded |
| **Max payload size (MQTT itself)** | $L_{payload} = 256$ MB | bytes | Practical limit is broker-dependent |

## 2.6 Real-World IIoT Utility

- **Predictive Maintenance**: Vibration sensor publishes to `plant/pump3/vibration`; analytics subscriber computes FFT and triggers maintenance alerts.
- **SCADA Integration**: MQTT bridges Modbus/OPC-UA data into cloud dashboards (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub natively support MQTT).
- **Edge–Cloud Pipeline**: Edge gateways act as both subscribers (to field devices) and publishers (to cloud), forming a *topic-tunneled* architecture.
- **Fleet Management**: Vehicles publish `fleet/vehicle42/gps`; dispatcher subscribes to `fleet/+/gps` for live tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: End-to-End MQTT Message Flow

The following step-by-step trace shows exactly what happens when a temperature sensor pushes data to a dashboard over QoS 1.

### Step 1 — TCP Connection Establishment
The MQTT client opens a TCP socket to the broker on port **1883** (or 8883 for TLS). This is the *transport* layer; MQTT itself is an application-layer protocol on top.

### Step 2 — CONNECT Packet Exchange
The client sends a CONNECT packet containing:
- `ClientID` (unique identifier, e.g., `sensor-temp-001`)
- `CleanSession` flag (here = `true`)
- `KeepAlive` (e.g., 60 seconds)
- `WillFlag`, `WillTopic`, `WillMessage` (if configured)
- `Username` / `Password` (if broker requires auth)

### Step 3 — CONNACK Acknowledgment
The broker validates credentials and replies with CONNACK. The **Return Code** field indicates status:

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 0 | Connection Accepted |
| 1 | Refused: unacceptable protocol version |
| 2 | Refused: identifier rejected |
| 3 | Refused: server unavailable |
| 4 | Refused: bad username or password |
| 5 | Refused: not authorized |

### Step 4 — SUBSCRIBE Establishment (Dashboard side)
The dashboard sends:
- `PacketID` = 1
- `TopicFilter` = `factory/+/temperature`
- `Requested QoS` = 1

The broker registers this subscription in its **in-memory topic trie** and replies with SUBACK confirming granted QoS.

### Step 5 — PUBLISH from Sensor (QoS 1)
The sensor transmits:

$$
\text{PUBLISH}(Topic = \texttt{factory/floor1/temperature}, \ QoS = 1, \ PacketID = 17, \ Payload = \{value: 78.4\})
$$

The broker:
1. Receives the packet.
2. Looks up the topic in its subscription registry.
3. Matches against all active subscriptions — finds `factory/+/temperature` matches `factory/floor1/temperature` (single-level wildcard `+`).
4. Forwards the PUBLISH to the dashboard with a *new* PacketID (since the broker regenerates the ID for downstream delivery).
5. Generates a **PUBACK** back to the sensor (with PacketID = 17) confirming receipt.

### Step 6 — PUBACK from Sensor
The sensor marks PacketID 17 as "complete" and is now free to publish again.

### Step 7 — Keep-Alive Maintenance
Every 60 seconds, the client sends PINGREQ. The broker responds with PINGRESP. If the broker receives no packet for $1.5 \times 60 = 90$ seconds, it disconnects the client and (if a Will was set) publishes the Will message.

### Step 8 — DISCONNECT (Graceful Closure)
On shutdown, the client sends DISCONNECT; broker closes the TCP socket cleanly.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Bandwidth Estimation for a Sensor Network

**Problem Statement:** A factory floor has $N = 500$ vibration sensors. Each sensor publishes one message every $T = 5$ seconds with an average payload of $L_p = 128$ bytes. The MQTT header overhead is $H = 4$ bytes. Compute:
(a) Total bandwidth at the broker ingress.
(b) Mean inter-arrival time and whether the broker can handle it on a $10$ Mbps link with $40\%$ reserved for MQTT.

### Step 3.2.1 — Message Rate per Sensor

$$
\lambda_{sensor} = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2 \ \text{messages/second}
$$

### Step 3.2.2 — Total Message Rate at Broker

$$
\lambda_{total} = N \cdot \lambda_{sensor} = 500 \cdot 0.2 = 100 \ \text{msgs/s}
$$

### Step 3.2.3 — Per-Message Size on Wire

$$
M = H + L_p = 4 + 128 = 132 \ \text{bytes}
$$

Converting to bits:

$$
M_{bits} = 132 \cdot 8 = 1056 \ \text{bits}
$$

### Step 3.2.4 — Aggregate Bandwidth

$$
B_{agg} = \lambda_{total} \cdot M_{bits} = 100 \cdot 1056 = 105{,}600 \ \text{bits/s} \approx 103.1 \ \text{Kbps}
$$

### Step 3.2.5 — Feasibility on the Reserved Link

$$
B_{reserved} = 0.40 \cdot 10 \ \text{Mbps} = 4 \ \text{Mbps} = 4{,}096 \ \text{Kbps}
$$

$$
\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{B_{agg}}{B_{reserved}} = \dfrac{103.1}{4096} \approx 0.0252 = 2.52\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The MQTT traffic consumes only ~2.5% of the reserved capacity, leaving ample headroom for QoS-1 PUBACK overhead (which adds another 2 bytes per publish, so negligible impact) and bursts.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: QoS 2 Four-Way Handshake (The "Exactly Once" Mechanism)

QoS 2 is a four-step handshake that prevents duplicates. The state machine ensures a message is delivered **once and only once** even over unreliable networks.

| Step | Direction | Packet | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Publisher → Broker | PUBLISH | Initial send, QoS=2, PacketID=X |
| 2 | Broker → Publisher | PUBREC | Broker received, will not accept another with same ID |
| 3 | Publisher → Broker | PUBREL | Publisher releases message for delivery |
| 4 | Broker → Publisher | PUBCOMP | Broker confirms completion |

The broker discards the original PUBLISH and only forwards to subscribers *after* receiving PUBREL. This guarantees a single forward path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logic for QoS 2:**
> - After Step 1, the broker has the message but holds it.
> - After Step 2, the publisher knows the broker received it, and stops retrying Step 1.
> - After Step 3, the publisher authorises forward delivery.
> - After Step 4, both sides clear the PacketID for reuse.
> 
> This is **why QoS 2 is slower** — 4 RTTs vs 2 RTTs for QoS 1, and why it's reserved for the most critical messages.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Latency Budget for QoS 2

A sensor is 1500 km from the broker. Propagation speed through fiber is $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Each broker hop adds 5 ms processing delay.

**Step 1 — One-way propagation delay:**

$$
t_p = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{2 \times 10^8} = 7.5 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Total QoS 2 handshake latency:**

$$
RTT_2 = 4 \cdot t_p + 4 \cdot t_{proc} = 4 \cdot 7.5 + 4 \cdot 5 = 30 + 20 = 50 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum throughput (assuming one outstanding message at a time):**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1}{RTT_2} = \dfrac{1}{0.050} = 20 \ \text{msgs/s per client}
$$

This shows why QoS 2 is unsuitable for high-frequency sensor streams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Pub/Sub Architecture for Industrial IoT

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FIELD["Field Layer - Sensors and Actuators"]
        S1[Temp Sensor Floor1]
        S2[Vibration Sensor Floor1]
        S3[Pressure Sensor Floor2]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["Edge Layer - Gateway or Local Broker"]
        B[MQTT Broker - Mosquitto or HiveMQ]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["Cloud Layer - Subscribers"]
        D1[SCADA Dashboard]
        D2[Predictive Maintenance AI]
        D3[Cloud Data Lake]
        D4[Mobile Alert App]
    end

    S1 -- "PUBLISH factory/floor1/temp" --> B
    S2 -- "PUBLISH factory/floor1/vib" --> B
    S3 -- "PUBLISH factory/floor2/pressure" --> B

    B -- "factory/+/temp" --> D1
    B -- "factory/+/vib" --> D2
    B -- "factory/#" --> D3
    B -- "factory/floor2/pressure" --> D4
```

## 4.2 QoS 1 Handshake — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher Sensor
    participant BR as MQTT Broker
    participant SUB as Subscriber Dashboard

    Note over P,BR: TCP connection established on port 1883

    P->>BR: CONNECT (ClientID, KeepAlive=60)
    BR-->>P: CONNACK (ReturnCode=0)

    SUB->>BR: SUBSCRIBE (TopicFilter=factory/+/temp, QoS=1)
    BR-->>SUB: SUBACK (Granted QoS=1)

    P->>BR: PUBLISH (Topic=factory/floor1/temp, QoS=1, PacketID=17, Payload=78.4)
    BR->>BR: Match topic against subscriptions
    BR-->>P: PUBACK (PacketID=17)
    BR->>SUB: PUBLISH (Topic=factory/floor1/temp, QoS=1, NewPacketID=42, Payload=78.4)
    SUB-->>BR: PUBACK (PacketID=42)

    loop Every 60 seconds
        P->>BR: PINGREQ
        BR-->>P: PINGRESP
    end

    P->>BR: DISCONNECT
    Note over P,BR: TCP socket closed gracefully
```

## 4.3 QoS 2 Handshake — Exactly-Once Delivery State Machine

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher
    participant BR as Broker

    P->>BR: PUBLISH QoS=2 PacketID=99
    Note over BR: Message stored, not yet forwarded
    BR-->>P: PUBREC PacketID=99
    Note over P: Publisher knows broker has it

    P->>BR: PUBREL PacketID=99
    Note over BR: Now authorized to forward to subscribers
    BR-->>P: PUBCOMP PacketID=99
    Note over P,BR: PacketID 99 freed for reuse
```

## 4.4 Topic Tree and Wildcard Resolution (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Factory Root Topic]

    ROOT --> F1[floor1]
    ROOT --> F2[floor2]
    ROOT --> F3[floor3]

    F1 --> MA[machineA]
    F1 --> MB[machineB]

    MA --> TEMP[temperature]
    MA --> VIB[vibration]

    MB --> TEMP2[temperature]
    MB --> VIB2[vibration]

    F2 --> MC[machineC]
    MC --> PRES[pressure]
    MC --> FLOW[flowrate]

    F3 --> MD[machineD]
    MD --> TEMP3[temperature]

    subgraph SUBSCRIPTIONS["Active Subscriptions"]
        SUB1[factory/+/temperature - all floor temps]
        SUB2[factory/floor1/# - entire floor1 data]
        SUB3[factory/floor2/machineC/pressure - exact match]
    end

    TEMP -.covered by.-> SUB1
    TEMP2 -.covered by.-> SUB1
    TEMP3 -.covered by.-> SUB1
    TEMP -.covered by.-> SUB2
    VIB -.covered by.-> SUB2
    MB -.covered by.-> SUB2
    PRES -.covered by.-> SUB3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not a physical drawing?**
> The topic tree is fundamentally a *graph/architecture* concept; a Mermaid diagram conveys the hierarchy and wildcard matching with absolute clarity, which is what examiners evaluate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT with their delivery guarantees.**

**Model Answer:**
MQTT provides three QoS levels that govern the reliability of message delivery between publisher and broker (and broker to subscriber):
1. **QoS 0 — At most once:** Fire-and-forget. The publisher sends the PUBLISH packet once; no acknowledgment. If lost, the message is gone. Used for non-critical telemetry.
2. **QoS 1 — At least once:** A two-way handshake (PUBLISH → PUBACK) guarantees delivery but **duplicates are possible** if PUBACK is lost and the publisher retries. Used for important but duplicate-tolerant commands.
3. **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** A four-way handshake (PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP) guarantees single delivery with no duplicates. Highest reliability, highest latency. Used for billing or safety-critical control.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of an MQTT Broker? Differentiate between `+` and `#` wildcards.**

**Model Answer:**
**Broker Role:** The MQTT Broker is the central server that receives all PUBLISH messages, filters them by topic, maintains subscription registries, queues messages for offline clients (persistent sessions), and forwards matching messages to all interested subscribers. It is the *only* mandatory component in a Pub/Sub system.

**Wildcard Differences:**
- `+` (single-level): Matches **exactly one** topic level. Example: `factory/+/temperature` matches `factory/floor1/temperature` but not `factory/floor1/machineA/temperature`.
- `#` (multi-level): Matches **zero or more remaining levels** and must be the last character. Example: `factory/floor1/#` matches `factory/floor1/machineA/temperature`, `factory/floor1/machineB/vibration`, and even `factory/floor1` itself.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Publish/Subscribe architecture** of MQTT. List the responsibilities of the Broker. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **MQTT control packet format** and the **CONNECT/CONNACK** handshake in detail. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution A:**

#### Part (a) — Pub/Sub Architecture & Broker Responsibilities

**Architecture:** Refer to *Section 4.1* of these notes. The architecture consists of three layers — Field (publishers), Edge (broker), and Cloud (subscribers). Publishers send messages tagged with topics; the broker routes them to matching subscribers; subscribers receive only relevant messages.

**Broker Responsibilities (write any 6 for full 7 marks):**
1. Accepting TCP/TLS connections from clients.
2. Authenticating clients via username/password or certificates.
3. Maintaining the **subscription registry** (topic trie) for fast matching.
4. Receiving PUBLISH packets and forwarding them to all matching subscribers.
5. Implementing **QoS handshakes** (PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP).
6. Storing **retained messages** for new subscribers.
7. Detecting dead clients via **Keep-Alive** timeout and publishing **Will messages**.
8. Implementing persistent sessions and offline message queuing.
9. Topic-level **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** for security.

#### Part (b) — Packet Format & CONNECT/CONNACK

**Control Packet Format (Fixed Header — 2 bytes minimum):**
- **Byte 1:** 4-bit packet type (e.g., `0001` = CONNECT) + 4-bit flags (DUP, QoS, RETAIN).
- **Byte 2–5:** Remaining Length (variable byte integer, 1–4 bytes, LSB first, max 268,435,455).
- **Variable Header:** Packet-specific fields (e.g., Packet Identifier for QoS > 0).
- **Payload:** Application data (e.g., JSON sensor reading).

**CONNECT Packet Contents:**
- Fixed header (type 1, flags 0).
- Variable header: Protocol Name ("MQTT"), Protocol Level (4 for v3.1.1, 5 for v5.0), Connect Flags (Clean Session, Will Flag, Will QoS, Will Retain, Password Flag, Username Flag), Keep Alive (seconds).
- Payload: Client Identifier (mandatory), Will Topic, Will Message, Username, Password.

**CONNACK Packet Contents:**
- Fixed header (type 2, flags 0).
- Variable header: 
  - **Connect Acknowledge Flags** (bit 0 = Session Present).
  - **Connect Return Code** (0 = Accepted, 1–5 = various refusals — see Section 3.1 Step 3).
- No payload.

The handshake establishes session parameters and authenticates the client before any data exchange.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **three QoS levels** of MQTT with sequence diagrams. Compute the theoretical maximum throughput for QoS 2 if the round-trip propagation delay is 80 ms and broker processing delay is 10 ms per hop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the significance of the **RETAIN flag**, **Last Will and Testament (LWT)**, and **Clean Session** flag in industrial IoT applications. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution B:**

#### Part (a) — QoS Levels and Throughput Calculation

**QoS 0 (At most once):** Publisher sends PUBLISH once; no ACK. Loss possible. Sequence: `P → B: PUBLISH`. Used for periodic non-critical telemetry.

**QoS 1 (At least once):** Two-way handshake. `P → B: PUBLISH`, `B → P: PUBACK`. Duplicates possible if PUBACK is lost and publisher retransmits. Used for actuator commands.

**QoS 2 (Exactly once):** Four-way handshake. `P → B: PUBLISH`, `B → P: PUBREC`, `P → B: PUBREL`, `B → P: PUBCOMP`. Guarantees single delivery. Used for billing/safety.

**Throughput Calculation:**

Given: $t_p = 80$ ms one-way propagation, $t_{proc} = 10$ ms per broker hop.

For QoS 2 there are 4 packets traversing the path (each pays propagation + broker processing):

$$
RTT_2 = 4 \cdot t_p + 4 \cdot t_{proc} = 4(80) + 4(10) = 320 + 40 = 360 \ \text{ms}
$$

Theoretical maximum throughput (one outstanding message at a time):

$$
\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1}{RTT_2} = \dfrac{1}{0.360} \approx 2.78 \ \text{msgs/s per client}
$$

If the broker permits multiple in-flight messages ($I_{max}$), the throughput scales linearly.

#### Part (b) — RETAIN, LWT, and Clean Session

**RETAIN Flag:** When a publisher sets this flag, the broker stores the last message for that topic as the *retained message*. Any future subscriber immediately receives this retained value upon subscription, even if no new publish occurs. **Industrial use:** New SCADA dashboards instantly know the current state of a pump or valve without waiting for the next sensor update.

**Last Will and Testament (LWT):** A message pre-configured by the client at connection time. If the broker detects an *abnormal* disconnection (no DISCONNECT packet received within keep-alive window), it automatically publishes the LWT on behalf of the dead client. **Industrial use:** A vibration sensor on a critical pump sets LWT = `pump3/offline`. If the battery dies, the broker publishes the alert, triggering a maintenance workflow.

**Clean Session Flag:** 
- `CleanSession = true`: Broker discards all subscriptions, queued messages, and session state on disconnect. Lightweight.
- `CleanSession = false`: Broker maintains a persistent session; offline messages (per QoS) are queued and delivered on reconnect. 
**Industrial use:** A remote oil-pipeline sensor connecting over flaky cellular network sets `CleanSession = false` so QoS-1 commands issued while the sensor was asleep are delivered when it reconnects.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing QoS 1 and QoS 2:** Writing "QoS 1 guarantees no duplicates" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. The exact wording is "**at least once**" with possible duplicates.
> 2. **Wildcards in publish topic:** Students sometimes write "the sensor publishes to `factory/#`" — wildcards are subscription-only.
> 3. **Confusing broker with publisher:** The broker does not generate data; it only routes. Stating "broker publishes to subscriber" is technically a misnomer — it *forwards* (the publisher's authorship is preserved).
> 4. **Forgetting the Will Trigger:** LWT fires only on *ungraceful* disconnect, not on a normal DISCONNECT. Many students miss this distinction.
> 5. **Omitting the TCP/TLS layer:** MQTT is an application-layer protocol. Skipping the transport layer in diagrams costs a mark.
> 6. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Writing "360" without "ms" for latency or "2.78" without "msgs/s" for throughput loses easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] MQTT = lightweight **Pub/Sub** protocol on top of TCP/IP, default port **1883** (8883 for TLS).
- [ ] Three components: **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, **Broker** — the broker is the only mandatory server.
- [ ] **Three decoupling dimensions:** space, time, synchronization.
- [ ] **14 control packet types**; most important are CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT.
- [ ] **QoS 0** = fire-and-forget; **QoS 1** = 2-way handshake (at least once); **QoS 2** = 4-way handshake (exactly once).
- [ ] **RETAIN** stores the last message for new subscribers.
- [ ] **LWT (Will Message)** fires only on ungraceful disconnect.
- [ ] **Clean Session = false** enables persistent offline message queuing.
- [ ] **Wildcards:** `+` = single level; `#` = multi-level, must be last. Subscribe-only.
- [ ] **Keep Alive Timer:** Client must send a packet within $1.5 \times K$ seconds or be considered dead.
- [ ] **MQTT v5.0 additions:** Reason codes, user properties, shared subscriptions, request/response correlation, message expiry interval.
- [ ] **IIoT use cases:** SCADA bridging, predictive maintenance, fleet telemetry, edge-cloud pipelines.
- [ ] **Common brokers:** Mosquitto (open-source, C-based), HiveMQ (enterprise Java), EMQX (Erlang, high scale), AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub (cloud-managed).
- [ ] **Comparison with HTTP:** MQTT is push-based, stateful, low-overhead (2-byte header minimum), and ideal for constrained devices; HTTP is pull-based, stateless, and verbose.
- [ ] **Comparison with CoAP:** CoAP is UDP-based RESTful; MQTT is TCP-based Pub/Sub. CoAP has lower latency, MQTT has stronger delivery guarantees.
- [ ] **Bandwidth math:** $B_{agg} = N \cdot \frac{H + L_p}{T}$ bits/s.
- [ ] **QoS 2 latency:** $4(t_p + t_{proc})$; **QoS 1:** $2(t_p + t_{proc})$; **QoS 0:** $t_p + t_{proc}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
