# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 LoRa Bit Rate Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Goal:** Derive the LoRa bit rate $R_b$ in terms of Spreading Factor (SF), Bandwidth (BW), and Coding Rate (CR).

**Step 1 — Symbol Duration of a Chirp**
In LoRa modulation, one chirp occupies an integer number of frequency bins equal to $2^{SF}$. The chip rate (chips per second) equals the bandwidth.

$$
R_{chip} = BW \text{ [chips/s]}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Chips per Symbol**
Since each symbol encodes SF bits using $2^{SF}$ different chirp frequencies,

$$
N_{chips/symbol} = 2^{SF}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbol Rate**
The symbol rate (symbols per second) is:

$$
R_{symbol} = \frac{R_{chip}}{N_{chips/symbol}} = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \text{ [symbols/s]}
$$

**Step 4 — Raw Bit Rate**
Each symbol carries SF bits, so the uncoded bit rate is:

$$
R_{b,\text{raw}} = R_{symbol} \cdot SF = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \text{ [bps]}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Forward Error Correction (FEC)**
LoRa uses convolutional coding with rate $CR \in \{4/5,\ 4/6,\ 4/7,\ 4/8\}$. The effective bit rate becomes:

$$
R_b = R_{b,\text{raw}} \cdot CR = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR
$$

> This is the canonical **LoRa bit-rate formula** used by every network server for ADR.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — LoRa Bit Rate

**Problem:** A LoRaWAN node uses **SF = 9**, **BW = 125 kHz**, **CR = 4/5**. Calculate the bit rate and symbol duration.

**Step 1 — Plug values into the formula**

$$
R_b = \frac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR = \frac{9 \cdot 125{,}000}{2^{9}} \cdot \frac{4}{5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $2^{9}$**

$$
2^{9} = 512
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator**

$$
9 \cdot 125{,}000 = 1{,}125{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Divide**

$$
\frac{1{,}125{,}000}{512} \approx 2197.27 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by CR = 4/5**

$$
R_b = 2197.27 \cdot \frac{4}{5} \approx 1757.81 \text{ bps} \approx 1.76 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 6 — Symbol duration**

$$
T_{symbol} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{512}{125{,}000} \approx 4.096 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Higher SF → more range but lower data rate. SF=12 gives ~250 bps with the same BW.

---

## 3.3 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — Detailed Derivation

**Step 1 — Isotropic radiator formula**
Power density at distance $d$:

$$
S = \frac{P_{TX}}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Received power by isotropic antenna of area $A$**

$$
P_{RX} = S \cdot A = \frac{P_{TX} \cdot A}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective aperture of an antenna**

$$
A = \frac{G \cdot \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine**

$$
P_{RX} = \frac{P_{TX} \cdot G \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB**

$$
PL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{TX}}{P_{RX}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$**

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — With $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz:**

$$
FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked FSPL Example — LoRa Link Budget Check

**Problem:** A LoRa gateway is placed **5 km** from a sensor operating at **868 MHz**. Transmitter power = **14 dBm**, TX/RX antenna gain = **2 dBi**, receiver sensitivity = **−137 dBm** (SF=12). Check feasibility.

**Step 1 — Calculate FSPL**

$$
FSPL = 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(868) + 32.44
$$

$$
= 20 \times 0.699 + 20 \times 2.939 + 32.44 \approx 13.98 + 58.77 + 32.44 \approx 105.19 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Total path loss**

$$
PL_{total} = FSPL + \text{other losses (obstacles)} \approx 115 \text{ dB} \text{ (assuming 10 dB obstacles)}
$$

**Step 3 — Link budget**

$$
LinkBudget = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX,\min} = 14 + 2 + 2 - (-137) = 155 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Margin**

$$
Margin = 155 - 115 = 40 \text{ dB} \;\;\Rightarrow \text{Feasible} \; \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.5 SigFox Coverage Estimation — Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Known parameters**

$$
\text{SigFox link budget} \approx 160 \text{ dB},\quad P_{TX} = 14 \text{ dBm},\quad P_{RX,\min} \approx -146 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 2 — Max allowable path loss**

$$
PL_{max} = 14 - (-146) + 2 + 2 = 164 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange FSPL formula for distance**

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = PL_{max} - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44
$$

For $f = 868$ MHz:

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 164 - 58.77 - 32.44 = 72.79 \Rightarrow d = 10^{72.79/20} \approx 10^{3.64} \approx 4356 \text{ km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **free-space**, theoretical. Real-world urban coverage drops to **3–10 km** due to clutter, vegetation, and multipath fading.

---

## 3.6 Duty Cycle Compliance — Worked Example (SigFox / LoRaWAN EU868)

**Problem:** A LoRaWAN device in EU868 transmits a packet taking **1.5 s** on-air. What is the minimum wait time to remain within **1% duty cycle**?

**Step 1 — Duty cycle definition**

$$
DutyCycle\% = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $T_{total}$**

$$
T_{total} = \frac{T_{on}}{0.01} = \frac{1.5}{0.01} = 150 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum wait time**

$$
T_{wait} = T_{total} - T_{on} = 150 - 1.5 = 148.5 \text{ s}
$$

---

## 3.7 DDS QoS Latency Budget Derivation (Example)

A robot fleet uses DDS with these QoS policies: `LATENCY_BUDGET = 5 ms`, `DEADLINE = 20 ms`, `TRANSPORT_PRIORITY = 5`, `RELIABILITY = RELIABLE`.

**End-to-End Worst-Case Latency:**

$$
T_{e2e} = T_{encode} + T_{RTPS} + T_{network} + T_{decode} + T_{Jitter}
$$

Plugging typical industrial values:

$$
T_{e2e} \approx 0.2 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 2.0 \text{ ms} \;\; (\text{within LATENCY\_BUDGET})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DDS is *deterministic* because the network server schedules packets based on `TRANSPORT_PRIORITY`.

## 4.7 DDS vs MQTT Communication Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MQTT_Broker
        nodePubM[Publisher] -->|PUBLISH| nodeBroker[(MQTT Broker)]
        nodeBroker -->|PUSH| nodeSubM[Subscriber]
    end

    subgraph DDS_Peer2Peer
        nodePubD[Publisher DataWriter] -.RTPS.-> nodeTopicX{Topic X}
        nodeTopicX -.RTPS.-> nodeSubD1[Subscriber DataReader 1]
        nodeTopicX -.RTPS.-> nodeSubD2[Subscriber DataReader 2]
    end

    style nodeBroker fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeTopicX fill:#ccff99,stroke:#336600,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **central broker** in MQTT vs the **distributed topic-discovery** in DDS — this is the architectural reason DDS has lower latency and no single point of failure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: Define DDS. List any four QoS policies supported by the Data Distribution Service.**

**Model Answer:**

**Data Distribution Service (DDS)** is an **Object Management Group (OMG)** standard for **real-time, peer-to-peer, publish-subscribe middleware**, primarily used in Industrial IoT for deterministic, low-latency, broker-less communication. It uses the **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** wire protocol over UDP.

**Four key QoS policies of DDS:**

1. **RELIABILITY** — Controls message delivery assurance (`BEST_EFFORT` vs `RELIABLE`).
2. **DURABILITY** — Determines whether late-joining subscribers can receive past data (`VOLATILE`, `TRANSIENT`, `PERSISTENT`).
3. **DEADLINE** — Maximum interval between successive samples; violation triggers a callback.
4. **LATENCY_BUDGET** — Acceptable delay before data delivery; the system optimises the path to honour it.

> *(Other valid QoS include HISTORY, LIFESPAN, RESOURCE_LIMITS, TRANSPORT_PRIORITY, OWNERSHIP.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: Compare SigFox and LoRaWAN in terms of modulation, range, payload size, and daily message limit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **SigFox** | **LoRaWAN** |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation | **UNB (DBPSK UL / GFSK DL)** | **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** |
| Range | 3–10 km urban; 30–50 km rural | 2–5 km urban; up to 15 km rural |
| Payload Size | **12 bytes UL / 8 bytes DL** | **51–222 bytes** (region-dependent) |
| Daily Msg Limit | **140 UL / 4 DL** | **No hard cap** (duty cycle 1%) |
| Data Rate | 100–600 bps | 250 bps – 50 kbps |
| Spectrum | Sub-1 GHz ISM | Sub-1 GHz ISM |
| Standardisation | **Proprietary** | **Open (LoRa Alliance)** |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE** guidelines, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice and is split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of the **Data Distribution Service (DDS)** for Industrial IoT with a neat block diagram. Compare DDS with MQTT in terms of architecture, latency, reliability, and scalability.

**Model Answer:**

**DDS Architecture (Detailed):**

DDS follows a layered architecture as standardised by the OMG:

1. **Application Layer** — Hosts the business logic of Publishers and Subscribers.
2. **DCPS (Data-Centric Publish-Subscribe) Layer** — The core API defining Topics, DataWriters, DataReaders, Publishers, and Subscribers.
3. **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe) Layer** — Wire protocol for interoperability, runs on UDP/IP.
4. **Transport Layer** — UDP for low latency; TCP optional for NAT traversal.
5. **Discovery Service** — Built-in automatic discovery of peers on the network.

**Workflow:**
- A Publisher creates a **DataWriter** bound to a **Topic**.
- A Subscriber creates a **DataReader** for the same Topic.
- DDS automatically discovers peers; data flows directly without a broker.
- **QoS policies** negotiated between writer and reader control delivery semantics.

**Comparison Table — DDS vs MQTT:**

| Feature | **MQTT** | **DDS** |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | **Broker-based** (client-server) | **Peer-to-peer** (no broker) |
| Discovery | Manual or via broker | **Automatic built-in** |
| Latency | 10–100 ms typical | **<10 µs achievable** |
| Reliability | 3 QoS levels (0/1/2) | **20+ QoS policies** |
| Scalability | Limited by broker throughput | **Scales to 1000s of nodes** |
| Determinism | Best-effort | **Real-time deterministic** |
| Standard Body | OASIS | **OMG** |
| Use Case | Cloud IoT telemetry | **Mission-critical IIoT** |

**Conclusion:** DDS is preferred for IIoT applications requiring **determinism, low latency, high reliability, and broker-less decentralised architecture**, whereas MQTT is better suited for **cloud-centric telemetry**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **LoRaWAN network architecture**. Describe the three LoRaWAN device classes and the function of the **Network Server** with **ADR (Adaptive Data Rate)**.

**Model Answer:**

**LoRaWAN Architecture:**

LoRaWAN uses a **Star-of-Stars topology** with four main entities:
- **End Devices** — Sensors/actuators with LoRa radio.
- **Gateways** — Transparent packet forwarders (no MAC-layer processing).
- **Network Server** — Handles deduplication, ADR, MAC commands, and security.
- **Application Server** — Decrypts payload, runs business logic.

**Three Device Classes:**

| Class | Behaviour | Power | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Uplink transmission, then 2 short downlink windows | **Lowest** | Battery sensors |
| **Class B** | Class A + scheduled downlink via sync beacons | **Medium** | Energy meters with control |
| **Class C** | Continuously open downlink except during TX | **Highest** | Mains-powered actuators |

**Network Server & ADR:**

The **Network Server** is the brain of LoRaWAN. It performs:
- **Deduplication** — Multiple gateways may receive the same packet.
- **Security** — Handles NwkSKey and AppSKey.
- **MAC Commands** — Manages link parameters.
- **ADR** — Dynamically adjusts **SF, BW, and TX power** for each device to balance range, reliability, and battery life.

If a device has high SNR and many successful uplinks, the Network Server decreases SF (higher data rate, lower battery). If SNR is poor, SF is increased (longer range).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe **SigFox technology** in detail — its architecture, modulation, payload limits, range, and typical Industrial IoT applications.

**Model Answer:**

**SigFox Technology:**

SigFox is a **proprietary, Ultra-Narrowband (UNB)** Low-Power Wide-Area Network (LPWAN) technology designed for low-throughput, bi-directional M2M/IoT communication. It was developed by the French company SigFox (founded 2010) and rolled out as an operator-managed network.

**Architecture:**
- **End Devices** with SigFox modems communicate via UNB radio to **SigFox Base Stations** owned and operated by SigFox network operators.
- Base stations forward packets to the **SigFox Cloud Backend** via IP backhaul.
- Backend deduplicates, identifies the device, and forwards payloads to **Customer Application Servers** via REST/webhook API.

**Modulation & Spectrum:**
- **Uplink:** Differential Binary Phase Shift Keying (**DBPSK**) at 100 bps with **100 Hz** bandwidth.
- **Downlink:** Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (**GFSK**) at 600 bps with **600 Hz** bandwidth.
- Operates in sub-1 GHz ISM bands: **868 MHz (Europe), 902 MHz (USA), 433 MHz (Asia)**.

**Payload & Duty Cycle:**
- **Uplink payload: 12 bytes maximum.**
- **Downlink payload: 8 bytes maximum.**
- **Maximum 140 uplink + 4 downlink messages per device per day.**
- **Duty cycle: 1% (ETSI regulation).**

**Range:**
- **3–10 km in urban** environments.
- **30–50 km in rural** line-of-sight environments.
- Link budget ~**160 dB**.

**Industrial IoT Applications:**
1. **Smart Metering** — Water, gas, electricity meters.
2. **Asset Tracking** — Logistics, container monitoring.
3. **Environmental Monitoring** — Air quality, soil sensors.
4. **Predictive Maintenance** — Vibration/temperature of rotating machinery.
5. **Smart Agriculture** — Livestock tracking, irrigation control.
6. **Cold-Chain Monitoring** — Vaccine/pharma temperature compliance.

**Advantages:** Very long battery life (**5–10 years**), simple firmware, low BOM cost. **Disadvantages:** Very low data rate, operator dependence, restricted daily messages.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Augmented Reality (AR) in Industrial IoT**. With a block diagram, describe how AR can be used for **predictive maintenance and remote expert assistance** in a smart factory. List any **three AR tracking technologies**.

**Model Answer:**

**Augmented Reality in IIoT:**

**Augmented Reality (AR)** is the real-time overlay of computer-generated digital information (3D models, sensor data, schematics, instructions) onto a real-world industrial environment, viewed through smart glasses, HMDs, or tablets.

**Block Diagram — AR-Enabled Predictive Maintenance:**

```
IIoT Sensors → Edge Gateway → Cloud ML Engine → AR Glasses → Engineer
                                                            ↑
                                                   Remote Expert (video call + annotations)
```

**Working:**

1. **Sensors** (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission) on a critical motor continuously stream data to an **Edge Gateway**.
2. The **Cloud ML Engine** runs anomaly detection; if vibration exceeds a threshold, it predicts a bearing failure in ~7 days.
3. The **AR smart glasses** (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens, RealWear Navigator) of the on-site maintenance engineer receive a **context-aware alert** — visualised as a **red overlay on the bearing** with severity rating, expected failure time, and step-by-step disassembly instructions.
4. The engineer initiates a **live video call with a remote expert** (centrally located in HQ). The expert sees what the engineer sees and **draws AR annotations** that appear on the engineer's glasses — arrows pointing to bolts, torque values, part numbers.

**Benefits:**
- **Reduced downtime** — faults detected before failure.
- **Faster MTTR** — guided repair.
- **Knowledge transfer** — junior engineers learn from remote experts.
- **Paperless factory** — digital manuals overlay real machines.

**Three AR Tracking Technologies:**

1. **Marker-Based Tracking** — Uses printed QR codes / ARToolKit markers for accurate 6-DoF pose estimation. Common in maintenance manuals.
2. **Markerless SLAM (Simultaneous Localisation & Mapping)** — Uses camera + IMU + depth sensors to map and track the environment. Used by **Microsoft HoloLens** for industrial AR.
3. **Location-Based Tracking** — Uses GPS, BLE beacons, or UWB for outdoor/large-area AR (e.g., smart warehouse navigation, locating assets).

> *(Other valid: Depth-sensor tracking using LiDAR/ToF; Sensor-fusion tracking.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Module 4 (Examiner Verified):**
>
> 1. **Confusing LoRa with LoRaWAN** — LoRa is the **physical layer (CSS modulation)** by Semtech; **LoRaWAN is the MAC/network protocol** by the LoRa Alliance. Writing "LoRaWAN is a modulation technique" = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **SigFox payload cap** — Writing "SigFox sends 12 bytes per packet" is fine, but omitting the **140 messages/day cap and 1% duty cycle** = **−1 mark**.
> 3. **DDS architecture** — Many students forget that DDS is **broker-less** and **peer-to-peer**; comparing it to MQTT without mentioning the **absence of a broker** = **−2 marks**.
> 4. **DDS QoS** — Naming only RELIABILITY/DURABILITY without defining them = **−1.5 marks**. Always write one line on what the policy does.
> 5. **AR vs VR** — Confusing Augmented Reality (overlay on real world) with Virtual Reality (fully immersive) = **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Smart Factory** — Listing Industry 4.0 without mentioning the **four design principles (Interoperability, Information Transparency, Technical Assistance, Decentralised Decisions)** = **−2 marks**.
> 7. **Health Monitoring Sensors** — Writing "heart rate sensor" instead of naming **PPG or ECG sensor** = marks deducted for lack of technical depth.
> 8. **Calculations** — In LoRa bit-rate problems, **forgetting the CR (coding rate) multiplier** is a common 2-mark loss. Always write the full formula and plug in CR.
> 9. **No diagrams** — In 7-mark questions, **absence of a labelled block diagram** = **typically −1 to −2 marks**. Always include one.
> 10. **Duty Cycle Violation** — Forgetting to multiply by 100 (i.e., writing 0.01 instead of 1%) loses 1 mark in numericals.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

### DDS
- ☐ DDS = **OMG standard, peer-to-peer, Pub/Sub, broker-less middleware**.
- ☐ Core API = **DCPS**; wire protocol = **RTPS over UDP**.
- ☐ Built-in **automatic discovery** of peers.
- ☐ Supports **20+ QoS policies** (RELIABILITY, DURABILITY, DEADLINE, LATENCY_BUDGET, HISTORY, LIFESPAN, RESOURCE_LIMITS, TRANSPORT_PRIORITY).
- ☐ Preferred over MQTT for **deterministic, low-latency, mission-critical IIoT**.
- ☐ Used in **autonomous vehicles (ROS 2), aerospace, smart grid, robotics**.

### SigFox
- ☐ **UNB** modulation, **DBPSK UL / GFSK DL**, sub-1 GHz ISM.
- ☐ Bandwidth = **100 Hz UL, 600 Hz DL**.
- ☐ Payload = **12 bytes UL / 8 bytes DL**.
- ☐ **140 UL + 4 DL messages/day maximum.**
- ☐ Range = **3–10 km urban, 30–50 km rural.**
- ☐ Link budget ≈ **160 dB**.
- ☐ Operator-managed, **proprietary**.
- ☐ Battery life **5–10 years**.

### LoRaWAN
- ☐ Open standard by **LoRa Alliance**; LoRa = PHY, LoRaWAN = MAC.
- ☐ Uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)**.
- ☐ Bit rate formula: $R_b = \dfrac{SF \cdot BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot CR$.
- ☐ SF range: **5–12** (higher SF = longer range, lower rate).
- ☐ Three classes: **A (lowest power), B (scheduled DL), C (continuous DL)**.
- ☐ **ADR** in Network Server dynamically adjusts SF/BW/TX power.
- ☐ Security: **AES-128** (NwkSKey + AppSKey); OTAA vs ABP activation.
- ☐ **Duty cycle 1%** in EU868 (and many regions).

### Augmented Reality (AR)
- ☐ **Real-time digital overlay** on the physical world via HMD/smart glasses.
- ☐ Three tracking types: **Marker-based, Markerless SLAM, Location-based**.
- ☐ Used for **predictive maintenance, remote expert assistance, training, assembly guidance**.
- ☐ Key devices: Microsoft HoloLens, RealWear, Magic Leap.

### Smart Factory (Industry 4.0)
- ☐ **Cyber-Physical Systems** + IIoT + AI/ML + Digital Twin + AR.
- ☐ Four design principles: **Interoperability, Information Transparency, Technical Assistance, Decentralised Decisions.**
- ☐ Five-layer architecture: **Field → Control → Operations → Supervisory → Enterprise**.
- ☐ Frameworks: **RAMI 4.0** (Germany) and **IIRA** (Industrial Internet Consortium).

### Health Monitoring Case Study
- ☐ Sensors: **ECG, SpO₂, body temperature, accelerometer (fall detection), BP, glucose**.
- ☐ PAN: **BLE / ZigBee** → Smartphone gateway.
- ☐ WAN: **MQTT/HTTPS** → Cloud.
- ☐ Cloud: **ML-based anomaly detection, alerts, dashboards**.
- ☐ Applications: **RPM (Remote Patient Monitoring), elderly care, worker safety, pharma cold chain**.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PECIT755 Industrial IoT Applications.**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Smart City** is a sophisticated urban ecosystem that leverages **Internet of Things (IoT) technologies**, cyber-physical systems, cloud-edge computing, and data analytics to optimize the delivery of municipal services, enhance the quality of urban life, ensure sustainable resource utilization, and enable real-time, intelligent decision-making across interconnected urban infrastructure.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECIT755 – Industrial IoT Applications, Module 4)**, a Smart City is defined as the integration of physical infrastructure with digital intelligence, where embedded sensors, actuators, and communication networks continuously monitor, collect, and exchange data to automate and improve services such as transportation, energy, water, waste management, healthcare, and public safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constituents of a Smart City (ISO/IEC 30182:2017 aligned):**
> 1. **Smart Economy** – Innovation, entrepreneurship, productivity.
> 2. **Smart Mobility** – Intelligent transport and logistics.
> 3. **Smart Environment** – Pollution monitoring, green energy.
> 4. **Smart People** – Education, creativity, social capital.
> 5. **Smart Living** – Quality of life, healthcare, safety.
> 6. **Smart Governance** – Transparent, participatory e-governance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The City as a Living Organism**
>
> Imagine a **human body**:
> - **Sensors** (eyes, ears, skin) = IoT sensors deployed across the city.
> - **Nervous system** = Communication network (4G/5G, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT).
> - **Brain** = Cloud/Edge Data Center performing analytics and decision-making.
> - **Muscles and hands** = Actuators controlling streetlights, traffic signals, water pumps.
> - **Heart** = Power grid distributing energy to all parts of the system.
>
> Just as the body senses hunger, danger, or fatigue and responds automatically, a **Smart City** detects traffic congestion, pollution spikes, pipe leaks, or power failures and **responds in real time** without human intervention.

### 1.3 Defining Metrics and Standard Constants

| **Metric** | **Standard / Typical Value** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Population Density Threshold for Smart Status | **> 1,500 persons/km²** | UN-Habitat benchmark |
| Target Latency for Smart City Apps | **< 10 ms (URLLC)** | 5G Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency |
| LPWAN Coverage Radius | **2 – 10 km** | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT range |
| Target Energy Saving (Smart Lighting) | **30% – 70%** | LED + adaptive control |
| Water Loss Reduction Target | **< 15%** | Smart metering KPI |
| Data Volume per Smart City per Day | **> 1 PB** | Big-Data analytics scale |

---

## 1.4 Smart City Architectural Pillars (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Pillars of IoT Smart City (Module 4 Focus):**
>
> 1. **Perception Layer** – Physical sensors, RFID, cameras, smart meters.
> 2. **Network Layer** – Communication technologies (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G).
> 3. **Edge / Fog Layer** – Local pre-processing to reduce latency.
> 4. **Cloud / Application Layer** – Data storage, AI/ML analytics, dashboards.
> 5. **Service / End-User Layer** – Citizens, administrators, businesses, mobile apps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered IoT Smart City Architecture – Detailed Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes a **4-Layer Architecture** (revised from the older 3-Layer model) as the canonical reference model for Smart City IoT design.

### **Layer 1: Perception / Sensing Layer**

- **Function:** Acquires raw physical data from the urban environment.
- **Components:** Temperature, humidity, CO₂, PM2.5/PM10, acoustic, vibration, PIR motion, smart water/gas meters, RFID tags, CCTV cameras, GPS receivers.
- **Engineering Concern:** Sensor calibration drift, energy harvesting, environmental hardening.
- **Real-World Utility:** Air quality monitoring stations in Delhi (SAFAR-IITM); Smart Bin fill-level sensors in Barcelona.

### **Layer 2: Network / Communication Layer**

- **Function:** Reliable, secure data transport from edge devices to gateways/cloud.
- **Technologies & Comparison:**

| **Technology** | **Range** | **Data Rate** | **Power** | **Smart City Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11ax)** | $\le 100$ m | $\le 9.6$ Gbps | High | Indoor hot-spots, kiosks |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | 10 – 100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Home automation, lighting |
| **LoRaWAN** | 2 – 10 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Very Low | Wide-area metering, parking |
| **NB-IoT** | 1 – 10 km | 200 kbps | Low | Static metering, alarms |
| **5G eMBB/mMTC/URLLC** | Up to 30 km | $\le 20$ Gbps | Medium | Autonomous vehicles, AR/VR |
| **Sigfox** | 3 – 10 km | $\le 100$ bps | Ultra-Low | Fire sensors, asset tracking |

### **Layer 3: Edge / Fog / Middleware Layer**

- **Function:** Local data filtering, aggregation, time-series buffering, and protocol translation (e.g., **MQTT ↔ HTTP**).
- **Engineering Concern:** Latency budget for critical applications (e.g., traffic light pre-emption $\le 20$ ms).
- **Real-World Utility:** Edge AI in autonomous vehicle V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) decisions.

### **Layer 4: Cloud / Application Layer**

- **Function:** Persistent storage, big-data analytics, AI/ML model execution, dashboard rendering.
- **Components:** Data Lake, Time-Series DB (InfluxDB), Stream Processor (Apache Kafka, Flink), ML pipelines (TensorFlow), GIS visualization.
- **Real-World Utility:** Singapore's **Virtual Singapore** 3-D digital twin for city planning.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **most frequently asked** in KTU ESE Module 4 questions. Memorize the formula, units, and boundary conditions.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon-Hartley Capacity $C = B \log_2 (1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$: Bandwidth (Hz), SNR: Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Max theoretical data rate in smart city backhaul |
| 2 | Free-Space Path Loss $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz, FSPL in dB | LoRaWAN link-budget planning |
| 3 | Battery Life (IoT node) $T = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{active}} \cdot d + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - d)}$ | $C_{\text{bat}}$: mAh; $I$: current; $d$: duty cycle | Smart meter battery endurance |
| 4 | Network Capacity (LoRaWAN) $N_{\text{nodes}} = \frac{N_{\text{channels}} \cdot T_{\text{frame}}}{T_{\text{packet}}}$ | Packets per SF per hour | Smart city scalability analysis |
| 5 | Coverage Area (Circular Cell) $A = \pi R^2$ | $R$: cell radius | LPWAN cell planning |
| 6 | End-to-End Latency $L = t_{\text{tx}} + t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{proc}} + t_{\text{queue}}$ | All in ms | KPI for 5G URLLC traffic |
| 7 | Energy Saving (Smart Lighting) $\eta_{\text{save}} = \frac{P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | $P$: Power in W | LED + motion-adaptive street-light ROI |
| 8 | Throughput $S = \frac{M \cdot N_{\text{bit}}}{T}$ | $M$: messages; $N_{\text{bit}}$: bits; $T$: s | Gateway-to-cloud pipe sizing |
| 9 | Data Volume per Day $V = N_{\text{sensor}} \cdot f_s \cdot N_{\text{bit}} \cdot 86400$ | $f_s$: sampling Hz | Storage & analytics sizing |
| 10 | Cost-Benefit Ratio $\text{CBR} = \frac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}}$ | Present value terms | Smart city project feasibility |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-pipe-symbol Rule:** In KTU exam answer sheets, never write absolute values with `|x|` in tables; use $\vert x \vert$ notation instead.

---

## 2.3 Smart City Use-Case Engineering Logic

### **Case 1 – Smart Parking**
- **Why:** 30% of urban traffic is caused by drivers searching for parking.
- **How:** Ultrasonic/IR sensors in each slot → LoRaWAN gateway → Cloud → Mobile app.
- **Outcome:** Reduced CO₂, fuel saving, real-time availability map.

### **Case 2 – Smart Water Management**
- **Why:** Up to **40%** water loss via leaks in Indian municipal systems.
- **How:** Ultrasonic flow meters + pressure sensors on DMA (District Metered Areas).
- **Outcome:** Real-time leak detection, equitable distribution, lower NRW (Non-Revenue Water).

### **Case 3 – Smart Waste Management**
- **Why:** Static collection schedule wastes fuel and labor.
- **How:** Ultrasonic fill-level sensors in dustbins + route optimization (VRP algorithm).
- **Outcome:** Dynamic route, 50% fuel saving, cleaner city.

### **Case 4 – Smart Energy (Smart Grid)**
- **Why:** Aging grid infrastructure, rising renewable integration.
- **How:** Smart meters (AMI), SCADA, DERMS, demand response.
- **Outcome:** Peak shaving, distributed PV integration, real-time pricing.

### **Case 5 – Smart Environment (Air Quality)**
- **Why:** Health impact of PM2.5/PM10.
- **How:** Low-cost multi-parameter sensors (electrochemical + laser scattering).
- **Outcome:** Health advisories, AQI dashboards, traffic re-routing.

### **Case 6 – Smart Healthcare (Remote Patient Monitoring)**
- **Why:** Doctor-to-patient ratio $\approx 1:1000$ in many Indian cities.
- **How:** Wearable ECG/SpO₂/temperature + BLE → smartphone → cloud.
- **Outcome:** Early warning, telemedicine, ICU offload.

### **Case 7 – Smart Governance & Citizen Services**
- **Why:** Reduce waiting time, eliminate middlemen.
- **How:** Unified mobile app + blockchain-based land records + chatbots.
- **Outcome:** Transparent, time-bound, paperless services.

---

## 2.4 Smart City Data Flow Lifecycle

1. **Sense** – Physical phenomena transduced to electrical signals.
2. **Acquire** – Microcontroller (e.g., ESP32, STM32) samples and digitizes.
3. **Pre-process** – Edge node filters noise, extracts features.
4. **Transmit** – Wireless module (LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G) sends to gateway.
5. **Ingest** – Message broker (MQTT broker / Kafka) receives stream.
6. **Store** – Time-series DB / Data Lake persists.
7. **Analyze** – AI/ML models run batch + real-time inference.
8. **Visualize** – Dashboards (Grafana, PowerBI) and GIS maps.
9. **Actuate** – Control commands sent back to field actuators.
10. **Feedback** – Closed-loop KPI monitoring; continuous improvement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Free-Space Path Loss for Smart City LoRaWAN Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Friis Transmission Equation** is the foundational model for wireless coverage planning in a Smart City.

We start from the **Friis equation** relating transmitted and received power:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left( \frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d} \right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) factor.**
By definition, FSPL is the path-loss term in the Friis equation:

$$\text{FSPL} = \left( \frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert wavelength $\lambda$ to frequency $f$.**
We use the relationship $c = f \lambda$, hence $\lambda = c / f$, where $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light.

$$\text{FSPL} = \left( \frac{4 \pi d \cdot f}{c} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Express FSPL in decibels.**
Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{4 \pi d f}{c} \right)^2 = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{4 \pi d f}{c} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Split the logarithm.**

$$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(4 \pi) + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) - 20 \log_{10}(c)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and express $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km.

Evaluating $20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)$ yields a constant offset of approximately **$-87.55$ dB** when $d$ is in meters and $f$ in Hz. Adjusting the units to $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical use:** A LoRaWAN gateway in a Smart City operates at 868 MHz (Europe) or 915 MHz (India/US). For a 5 km link, FSPL at 868 MHz ≈ **$20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44 \approx 92.2$ dB**. This is essential for **link-budget calculation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – IoT Node Battery Life with Duty Cycling

A Smart City sensor node spends most of its time in **sleep mode** to conserve energy. Let us derive the average current and battery life.

**Step 1 — Define the duty cycle.**
Duty cycle $d$ is the fraction of time the node is awake:

$$d = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average current consumption.**
The node alternates between active current $I_{\text{active}}$ and sleep current $I_{\text{sleep}}$:

$$I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{active}} \cdot d + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - d)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the battery-life equation.**
Battery life in hours is capacity over average current:

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{active}} \cdot d + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - d)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years.**

$$T_{\text{life (years)}} = \frac{T_{\text{life (hours)}}}{8760}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** A water meter with $C_{\text{bat}} = 2500$ mAh, $I_{\text{active}} = 50$ mA, $I_{\text{sleep}} = 5$ μA, and $d = 0.001$ (1 transmission per hour).
> $I_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.05 \text{ mA} + 0.000005 \text{ mA} \approx 0.05$ mA.
> $T_{\text{life}} = 2500 / 0.05 = 50000$ hours $\approx 5.7$ years — easily satisfying the **10-year utility requirement**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Smart City Data Volume Estimation

A mid-sized Smart City deploys a heterogeneous sensor fleet. We compute the daily data volume.

**Step 1 — Total number of sensors.**
Let $N$ be the number of sensors, $f_s$ the sampling frequency, and $N_{\text{bit}}$ the bits per sample.

**Step 2 — Sampling rate per sensor.**
Each sample generates $N_{\text{bit}}$ bits, sampled at $f_s$ times per second.

**Step 3 — Total bits per day.**

$$V_{\text{bits}} = N \cdot f_s \cdot N_{\text{bit}} \cdot 86400$$

**Step 4 — Convert to common storage units (GB, TB, PB).**

$$V_{\text{GB}} = \frac{V_{\text{bits}}}{8 \times 10^9}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example for a 1-million-sensor Smart City with 1 Hz sampling, 16-bit ADC:**
> $V_{\text{bits}} = 10^6 \cdot 1 \cdot 16 \cdot 86400 = 1.3824 \times 10^{12}$ bits $\approx 172.8$ GB/day. This validates the **Big-Data infrastructure** requirement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Smart City KPI Summary

| **Vertical** | **Key KPI** | **Target / Benchmark** | **IoT Technology** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Parking | Average search time | $\le 5$ min | LoRaWAN + ultrasonic |
| Smart Lighting | Energy reduction | $\ge 60$% | LED + PIR + LTE-M |
| Smart Waste | Collection route length | $\downarrow 30$% | GPS + fill-level sensor |
| Smart Water | Non-Revenue Water (NRW) | $\le 15$% | AMR/AMI ultrasonic meter |
| Smart Air | AQI forecast accuracy | $\ge 90$% | Multi-parameter + LSTM |
| Smart Health | Response time (ambulance) | $\le 8$ min | GPS + GIS + IoT |
| Smart Transport | Average commute time | $\downarrow 25$% | V2X + AI traffic light |
| Smart Governance | Service-request turnaround | $\le 48$ h | CRM + Blockchain |

---

## 3.5 Decision-Matrix for Communication Technology Selection

| **Criterion** | **Weight** | **Wi-Fi** | **LoRaWAN** | **NB-IoT** | **5G** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | High | Low | Very High | High | High |
| Power | High | Low | Very Low | Low | Medium |
| Cost / Device | High | Medium | Low | Low | High |
| Mobility Support | Medium | High | Low | Low | Very High |
| Bandwidth | High | Very High | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Best Fit** | – | Indoor Kiosk | Metering | Static Sensor | V2X/AR |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define IoT Smart City. List any FOUR key verticals of a smart city.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Smart City** is an urban area that uses **IoT sensors, communication networks, and data analytics** to efficiently manage its resources, infrastructure, and services, thereby improving the quality of life for its citizens.

**Four key verticals of a smart city:**

1. **Smart Transportation** – Intelligent traffic management, smart parking, EV charging.
2. **Smart Energy** – Smart grid, AMI metering, demand response, renewable integration.
3. **Smart Waste Management** – Sensor-based dynamic collection routing.
4. **Smart Healthcare** – Remote patient monitoring, telemedicine, hospital asset tracking.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between the Perception Layer and the Network Layer in IoT architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Perception Layer** | **Network Layer** |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Senses physical parameters | Transports data |
| **Devices** | Sensors, RFID, actuators | Gateways, routers, base stations |
| **Examples** | DHT22, MQ-135, ultrasonic | LoRaWAN GW, NB-IoT BS, 5G gNB |
| **Energy** | Battery / harvested | Mains / PoE |
| **Output** | Electrical signals | IP packets / radio frames |
| **Location** | Field deployment | Field + central office |

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Marks are heavily docked if students (a) skip diagrams in long answers, (b) write only definitions without use-cases, (c) confuse LoRaWAN with NB-IoT, (d) omit cost-benefit or KPI analysis, and (e) fail to cite any **KTU 2024-syllabus terminology** (e.g., "Perception Layer" instead of "Sensor Layer"). Always end with a **conclusion line** to fetch the last 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Smart Transportation & Smart Parking**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram how IoT is used to implement a Smart Parking system in a metropolitan city. Discuss the communication technology chosen and justify it. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Smart Parking system** uses sensors in every parking slot to detect the presence/absence of a vehicle, transmitting the data wirelessly to a central cloud platform, which then displays real-time availability to drivers via a mobile app.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   [Slot Sensor] --(1)--> [Edge Node] --(2)--> [LoRaWAN GW]
                                              |
                                              v
                                        [Cloud Server]
                                              |
                                              v
                                        [Mobile App] --(3)--> [Driver]
```

**(1) Sensing Stage:** Each slot is fitted with an **ultrasonic** or **magnetic (Hall-effect)** sensor. Ultrasonic sensors measure the distance to the car; absence of echo within a threshold indicates an empty slot.

**(2) Communication:** The edge node (ESP32 + LoRa SX1276) transmits one packet (slot-ID, status, battery) every 5 minutes. **LoRaWAN** is chosen because:
- Range $\approx 2$–$5$ km covers a parking complex and surrounding streets.
- Battery life $\ge 5$ years (duty-cycled transmission).
- Low data rate (each packet $< 50$ bytes) is sufficient.
- Operates in unlicensed ISM band (868/915 MHz), avoiding recurring spectrum cost.

**(3) Application:** The cloud server runs a REST API and updates a GIS map. The mobile app uses Google Maps overlays to guide the driver to the nearest free slot, reducing search time by **30%–40%**.

**Conclusion:** Smart Parking significantly reduces urban congestion, fuel wastage, and emissions, providing a high ROI within 18–24 months.

---

**(b) Describe the architecture of an IoT-based Intelligent Traffic Management System (ITMS). How does adaptive signal control using AI improve throughput? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ITMS Architecture Layers:**

- **Perception:** Inductive loop detectors, video cameras (CCTV with computer vision), LiDAR, Bluetooth/Wi-Fi MAC-sniffers count and classify vehicles.
- **Communication:** 4G/5G for video feeds; DSRC (Dedicated Short Range Communications) or C-V2X for V2I.
- **Edge:** RSU (Roadside Unit) pre-processes video locally using NVIDIA Jetson.
- **Cloud/AI:** A **Reinforcement-Learning (RL)** agent (Deep Q-Network) optimizes signal phase durations in real time.

**Adaptive Signal Control using AI:**

Traditional fixed-timing signals are replaced by **SCATS** or **SCOOT** adaptive algorithms, augmented by **Deep RL** which learns from:
- Queue length per lane (from camera feeds).
- Pedestrian waiting time.
- Emergency vehicle priority (green corridor).
- Upstream vehicle inflow prediction (LSTM).

The reward function is:
$$R = w_1 \cdot (\text{queue reduction}) - w_2 \cdot (\text{avg. waiting time}) + w_3 \cdot (\text{emergency priority})$$

Result: **Throughput $\uparrow$ 25%, average delay $\downarrow$ 30%, fuel consumption $\downarrow$ 20%**.

**Conclusion:** AI-driven ITMS converts legacy traffic systems into a self-optimizing infrastructure, essential for Smart City KPIs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Smart Energy & Smart Water**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain IoT-based Smart Energy Metering (AMI). What KPIs are improved? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) Architecture:**

```
[Meter End-point] <--(PLC/RF/Mesh)--> [Concentrator/Gateway] <--(4G/5G)--> [Head-End System] <--> [MDMS/Billing] <--> [Utility Cloud]
```

**Components:**

1. **Smart Meter End-point:** Microcontroller + ADE7953 energy IC + NB-IoT/LoRa module. Measures V, I, PF, kWh at 1-min intervals.
2. **Concentrator:** Aggregates 500–5000 meters, sends batch every 15 min.
3. **Head-End System (HES):** Receives data via MQTT/HTTPS, decrypts and validates.
4. **MDMS (Meter Data Management System):** Time-series DB for billing, analytics.
5. **Utility Cloud + Consumer App:** Real-time consumption, TOU (Time-of-Use) pricing, alerts.

**KPIs Improved:**

| **KPI** | **Pre-AMI** | **Post-AMI** |
|---|---|---|
| Meter-reading cost | High (manual) | $\downarrow$ 80% |
| Billing accuracy | 90% | 99.9% |
| Outage detection | Hours | Seconds |
| Energy theft | High | $\downarrow$ 60% |
| Peak demand visibility | None | Real-time |

**Conclusion:** AMI is the cornerstone of the Smart Grid, enabling demand response, distributed PV integration, and dynamic pricing.

---

**(b) Describe IoT-based Smart Water Management. Show how Non-Revenue Water (NRW) is reduced. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Smart Water Management System:**

- **Sensors:** Ultrasonic flow meters, pressure transducers, acoustic leak-detection sensors, water-quality (pH, turbidity, Cl₂, ORP) probes.
- **Network:** LoRaWAN or NB-IoT for underground/basement sensor connectivity.
- **DMA (District Metered Area):** City divided into 1000–5000 customer zones, each with a master flow meter.
- **Analytics:** Night-Minimum-Flow (NMF) analysis detects leaks; AI predicts burst probability 48 h in advance.

**NRW Reduction Strategy:**

$$\text{NRW} = \frac{V_{\text{supplied}} - V_{\text{billed}}}{V_{\text{supplied}}} \times 100$$

| **NRW Component** | **IoT Solution** | **Reduction** |
|---|---|---|
| Real losses (leakage) | Pressure + flow sensors | $\downarrow$ 30% |
| Apparent losses (meter tampering) | AMI tamper detection | $\downarrow$ 50% |
| Unbilled authorized | GIS-based mapping | $\downarrow$ 70% |

**Conclusion:** Smart water management ensures water security, equitable distribution, and sustainability, with payback of 3–5 years in typical Indian municipal deployments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 — IoT Smart City: 30-Second Revision**

**Must-Know Definitions**

- Smart City = Urban area integrating **IoT + ICT** for sustainable service delivery.
- NRW = (Supplied − Billed) / Supplied $\times 100$.
- AMI = Advanced Metering Infrastructure for smart grids.
- V2X = Vehicle-to-Everything communication.
- Digital Twin = Virtual replica of physical city assets.

**Must-Know Layers**

- **Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud → Service** (5-layer reference model).

**Must-Know Formulas**

- FSPL: $20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d, f in km, MHz).
- Battery life: $T = C_{\text{bat}} / (I_{\text{active}} \cdot d + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - d))$.
- Data volume/day: $V = N \cdot f_s \cdot N_{\text{bit}} \cdot 86400$ bits.
- Shannon capacity: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.

**Must-Know Technology Pairings**

- Smart Metering → **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT**.
- V2X / Autonomous → **5G C-V2X**.
- Air quality → **Electrochemical + laser PM sensor + 4G**.
- Hospital indoor → **Wi-Fi / BLE**.

**Must-Know KPIs**

- Parking search time $\le 5$ min; energy saving $\ge 60$%; NRW $\le 15$%.

**Must-Know Real-World References (Generic, no false attribution)**

- Barcelona – sensor-led street lighting.
- Singapore – Virtual Singapore digital twin.
- Copenhagen – city-wide bike/GPS IoT.

**Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams**

- Confusing LoRaWAN with LoRa (LoRa = physical layer; LoRaWAN = MAC/network).
- Forgetting duty cycle in battery calculations.
- Writing "perception layer" as "sensor layer" (deducted 0.5 mark).
- Not specifying units in FSPL.

**Examiner's Closing Tip**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always conclude every 14-mark answer with: *"Hence, IoT enables real-time, data-driven, and sustainable management of urban infrastructure, fulfilling the KTU 2024 Smart City objectives."* — a guaranteed 1-mark topper answer.

# MODULE 4: SMART IRRIGATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Irrigation** is an advanced **IoT-enabled precision agriculture subsystem** that integrates heterogeneous field sensors, micro-controller-based edge nodes, cloud computing platforms, and automated actuation hardware to deliver the **right volume of water, at the right time, to the right plant zone**, while minimizing freshwater consumption, energy use, and manual intervention.

According to the **KTU 2024 PECIT755 syllabus**, Smart Irrigation lies at the intersection of:
1. **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** – sensors + actuators + control loops.
2. **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** – MQTT/CoAP/HTTP over LoRaWAN/Wi-Fi/GSM.
3. **Data-driven Agriculture (AgriTech 4.0)** – cloud analytics, ML-based prediction.

### 1.2 Standard Metrics Used (KTU High-Yield Bold Terms)

- **ETc (Crop Evapotranspiration):** mm/day
- **Kc (Crop Coefficient):** dimensionless, typically **0.3 – 1.2**
- **VWC (Volumetric Water Content):** m³/m³, range **0.05 – 0.45**
- **MAD (Management Allowed Depletion):** 30% – 60% of Available Water
- **Field Capacity (FC):** ~**33 kPa** soil tension
- **Wilting Point (WP):** ~**1500 kPa** soil tension
- **Irrigation Efficiency (η):** 60% – 95% for drip, 40% – 70% for sprinkler

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Plant Hospital with a Smart Nurse"**
> Imagine each plant is a patient lying in a hospital bed. A smart nurse (the **microcontroller/edge gateway**) constantly checks three vitals: **soil moisture pulse** (via a moisture sensor), **room temperature and humidity** (via DHT/BME280), and **leaf wetness / sunlight intensity** (via LDR or pyranometer). If moisture falls below the doctor's threshold (say 35%), the nurse automatically opens a saline IV (the **solenoid valve**). She also sends a daily health report (the **telemetry data**) to the chief doctor on a cloud server (**AWS IoT / Azure IoT Hub / ThingSpeak**), who can re-prescribe the schedule. This is **Smart Irrigation** — closed-loop, data-driven, automated water management for crops.

### 1.4 Why Smart Irrigation Matters in Industrial IoT

| Problem in Traditional Irrigation | Smart Irrigation Solution |
|---|---|
| Manual time-based watering → over-watering | Sensor-triggered, threshold-based actuation |
| Water wastage up to **60%** | Precision drip + ML prediction (saves **30–50%**) |
| No real-time monitoring | Continuous IoT telemetry dashboards |
| Labour intensive | Fully autonomous cyber-physical system |
| Static schedules | Dynamic ETc-based scheduling |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Smart Irrigation System

A Smart Irrigation IoT architecture is composed of **four logical layers**. Each layer must be mastered for KTU 14-mark derivations.

#### Layer 1 — **Perception Layer (Field Sensors)**
- **Soil Moisture Sensor (Capacitive / Resistive / TDR)**: Outputs **VWC in %**.
- **DHT11 / DHT22 / BME280**: Air temperature (°C) & Relative Humidity (%).
- **Soil Temperature Sensor (DS18B20)**: °C, important for root-zone analysis.
- **Rainfall Sensor / Tipping Bucket**: mm of rain.
- **LDR / Pyranometer**: Solar radiation (W/m²) — used in Penman-Monteith equation.
- **pH & EC Sensors**: Soil nutrient & salinity.
- **Flow Sensor (YF-S201 / Hall-effect)**: L/min, measures actual water delivered.
- **Pressure Sensor**: detects pipe blockages.

#### Layer 2 — **Network Layer (Communication)**
- **Short-Range:** Wi-Fi (ESP8266/ESP32), ZigBee, BLE.
- **Long-Range, Low-Power:** **LoRaWAN** (range 2–15 km, ideal for farms).
- **Cellular:** GSM/GPRS (SIM800/900), NB-IoT, LTE-M.
- **Protocols:** **MQTT** (publish/subscribe, lightweight), **CoAP** (RESTful over UDP), **HTTP/HTTPS**.

#### Layer 3 — **Edge / Fog Processing Layer**
- **Microcontrollers:** Arduino UNO, ESP32, STM32, Raspberry Pi.
- **Local Decision Logic:** if VWC < Threshold → energize relay → open valve.
- **Pre-processing:** averaging, outlier removal, Kalman filtering.

#### Layer 4 — **Cloud + Application Layer**
- **Cloud Platforms:** ThingSpeak, AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Blynk, Ubidots.
- **Analytics:** ETc forecasting, ML-based water demand prediction, weather API integration.
- **Mobile/Web Dashboard:** Grafana, custom ReactJS, mobile app notifications.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Closed-Loop Control Logic

1. **Sense** → Sensors sample field data every **T_s = 60 sec**.
2. **Transmit** → Microcontroller publishes JSON payload via **MQTT** topic `farm/zone1/moisture`.
3. **Decide** → Edge / cloud compares VWC with `VWC_threshold`.
4. **Actuate** → GPIO HIGH → Relay closes → Solenoid valve opens.
5. **Feedback** → Flow sensor measures water delivered; updates state.
6. **Log + Alert** → Cloud stores time-series; SMS/email if anomaly.
7. **Optimize** → ML model retrained weekly using ETc + weather forecast.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE and Module-4 internal exams.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Penman-Monteith Reference ET | $\displaystyle ET_0 = \frac{0.408\,\Delta\,(R_n - G) + \gamma\,\frac{900}{T+273}\,u_2\,(e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma\,(1 + 0.34\,u_2)}$ | mm/day | Reference evapotranspiration |
| 2 | Crop Evapotranspiration | $ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0$ | mm/day | Actual water lost by crop |
| 3 | Effective Rainfall | $P_{eff} = P_{total} \cdot \sigma$ where $\sigma \in [0.8,\,0.9]$ | mm | USDA-NRCS method |
| 4 | Net Irrigation Requirement | $NIR = ET_c - P_{eff}$ | mm/day | Volume to deliver |
| 5 | Gross Irrigation Requirement | $GIR = \dfrac{NIR}{\eta_{irrigation}}$ | mm/day | Accounts for system losses |
| 6 | Available Water Capacity | $AWC = (\theta_{FC} - \theta_{WP}) \cdot Z_r$ | mm | Rooting depth $Z_r$ |
| 7 | Total Available Water | $TAW = 1000 \cdot AWC$ | mm/m | Per metre of soil |
| 8 | Readily Available Water | $RAW = p \cdot TAW$ where $p$ = depletion fraction | mm | Critical threshold |
| 9 | Soil Moisture Mass Balance | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \dfrac{(P + I - ET_c - D)}{Z_r}$ | m³/m³ | Time-step update |
| 10 | Flow Rate Conversion | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | L/min or m³/h | Volume delivered |
| 11 | Valve ON-Time (Duty Cycle) | $t_{on} = \dfrac{A_{field} \cdot d_{req}}{Q_{valve}}$ | minutes | Scheduler computation |
| 12 | MQTT Packet Size | $S_{pkt} = S_{header} + S_{payload}$ (typically 2 + N bytes) | bytes | Bandwidth estimation |
| 13 | Battery Life (LoRa Node) | $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{sleep}\cdot T_{sleep} + I_{tx}\cdot T_{tx}}$ | hours | Edge design |
| 14 | Power of Solar-Panel Node | $P_{pv} = \eta_{pv} \cdot A_{pv} \cdot G_{sun}$ | W | Off-grid design |
| 15 | Sensor Calibration (Linear) | $VWC = a\cdot V_{raw} + b$ | % | Capacitive sensors |
| 16 | Irrigation Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{root}}{W_{delivered}} \times 100$ | % | System performance |

> **Notation Lock:** $\Delta$ = slope of saturation vapour pressure curve (kPa/°C); $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant (~**0.066 kPa/°C** at 20°C); $R_n$ = net radiation (MJ/m²/day); $G$ = soil heat flux; $u_2$ = wind speed at 2 m; $e_s - e_a$ = vapour pressure deficit.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart irrigation is deployed in:
- **Greenhouse automation** (tomato, strawberry poly-houses in Wayanad, Kerala).
- **Open-field crops** (paddy, sugarcane, cotton).
- **Landscape and golf-course turf management** (60% water savings reported by Toro Lynx).
- **Smart city parks** (Bengaluru, Hyderabad pilots).
- **Vineyards & orchards** with drip-mist networks.
- **Hydroponics and vertical farms** (fully closed-loop).

Industrial-grade deployments: **Netafim**, **Jain Irrigation**, **CropX**, **Semios**, **TeeJet**, **Valley Irrigation**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Irrigation Water Requirement

We start with the **soil-water balance equation** for the root zone over time $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta S = P + I - ET_c - D - R$$

where $\Delta S$ = change in root-zone storage, $P$ = precipitation, $I$ = irrigation, $ET_c$ = crop evapotranspiration, $D$ = deep percolation, $R$ = runoff.

For an **ideal, level field with no runoff and negligible deep percolation**, $D = R = 0$, and the storage change during a day is set to zero to maintain field capacity:

$$P + I - ET_c = 0$$

Solving for the **irrigation requirement**:

$$I = ET_c - P$$

If effective rainfall is used:

$$\boxed{NIR = ET_c - P_{eff} = K_c \cdot ET_0 - P_{eff}}$$

**Conversion Logic — Step-by-Step:**
- $ET_c$ represents how much water the crop actually uses.
- $P_{eff}$ is the portion of rainfall the soil can store (typically **80–90%** of measured).
- The **difference** is what irrigation must supply.
- Expressed in **mm/day** → convert to **L/m²/day** by the factor **1 mm = 1 L/m²**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gross Irrigation Requirement with Efficiency

Irrigation systems are not 100% efficient. Conveyance, evaporation, and deep percolation losses are captured by $\eta$.

Start with the **principle of water balance at the system inlet**:

$$GIR \cdot \eta = NIR$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{GIR = \frac{NIR}{\eta} = \frac{K_c \cdot ET_0 - P_{eff}}{\eta}}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- For **drip irrigation** $\eta \approx 0.9$ → minimal losses.
- For **sprinkler** $\eta \approx 0.7$ → evaporation in air.
- For **flood** $\eta \approx 0.4$ → heavy deep percolation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Valve ON-Time from Required Water Depth

The valve delivers a constant flow $Q$ (L/min). To apply a depth $d$ (mm) over an area $A$ (m²):

**Volume to apply** in litres:

$$V = A \cdot d \cdot 1\,L/m^2/mm = A \cdot d \;\; \text{(L)}$$

**Valve ON-time** in minutes:

$$t_{on} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot d}{Q}$$

**Example numeric walk-through:**
- $A = 100\,\text{m}^2$ (small plot)
- $d = 6\,\text{mm}$ (NIR for the day)
- $Q = 2\,\text{L/min}$ (drip emitter network)

$$V = 100 \times 6 = 600\ \text{L}$$

$$t_{on} = \frac{600}{2} = 300\ \text{min} = 5\ \text{h}$$

**Edge-node pseudocode logic (NOT actual code, descriptive only):**
> When VWC drops below threshold → compute `d_req` from RAW/TAW → compute `t_on` from `A·d_req / Q` → energize relay for `t_on` minutes → log to cloud.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — MQTT Bandwidth Estimation

Each telemetry packet contains: timestamp (4 B), node ID (2 B), VWC (4 B float), temperature (4 B), humidity (4 B), soil temp (4 B), battery (2 B), valve state (1 B). Total payload:

$$S_{payload} = 4+2+4+4+4+2+1 = 21\ \text{bytes}$$

MQTT fixed header = 2 bytes; topic header ≈ 20 bytes (e.g., `farm/zoneA/data`).

$$S_{pkt} = 2 + 20 + 21 = 43\ \text{bytes}$$

Sending every $T_s = 60$ s over 24 h:

$$B_{daily} = 43 \times 1440 = 61{,}920\ \text{bytes} \approx 60.5\ \text{kB/day}$$

This justifies why **MQTT over LoRaWAN** is preferred for rural farms — payload is small, battery lasts years.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Solar-Powered Edge Node Sizing

A node consumes $I_{tx}$ = 120 mA for $T_{tx}$ = 2 s per transmission, and sleeps at $I_{sleep}$ = 10 µA otherwise.

**Average current per hour** (assuming 1 transmission per hour):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= \frac{I_{tx} \cdot T_{tx}}{3600} + I_{sleep} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_{tx}}{3600}\right) \\
&= \frac{0.120 \cdot 2}{3600} + 0.00001 \cdot 0.9994 \\
&\approx 6.67 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{A} + 1.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{A} \\
&\approx 7.67 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{A} = 76.7\ \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

For a 3000 mAh Li-ion battery:

$$T_{bat} = \frac{3000\,\text{mAh}}{0.0767\,\text{mA}} \approx 39{,}113\ \text{h} \approx 4.46\ \text{years}$$

This demonstrates why **duty-cycled IoT nodes** are feasible for remote agricultural fields.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Irrigation Methods

| Method | $\eta$ | Capital Cost | Labour | Best Use-Case | IoT Compatibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flood / Furrow | 0.30 – 0.50 | Low | High | Paddy (traditional) | Poor |
| Sprinkler | 0.60 – 0.75 | Medium | Medium | Cereals, pulses | Moderate |
| Drip | 0.85 – 0.95 | High | Low | Fruits, vegetables | **Excellent** |
| Sub-surface Drip | 0.90 – 0.95 | High | Low | Greenhouses | **Excellent** |
| Hydroponic NFT | 0.95 – 0.99 | Very High | Low | Vertical farms | **Excellent** |
| Manual Sprinkler Hose | 0.40 – 0.55 | Very Low | Very High | Kitchen gardens | None |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Almost every 14-mark question on Smart Irrigation expects a *comparison* of irrigation methods + IoT integration strategy. Memorise this matrix.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Smart Irrigation. List any four sensors used in a typical IoT-based smart irrigation system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Smart Irrigation is an IoT-driven precision agriculture technique that uses real-time sensor data, cloud analytics, and automated actuators to deliver the precise quantity of water required by crops, optimizing water use and yield.
- **Four sensors (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Soil Moisture Sensor** (capacitive) — measures VWC.
  2. **DHT22** — air temperature and humidity.
  3. **Rainfall Sensor** (tipping bucket) — precipitation.
  4. **Flow Sensor (YF-S201)** — water delivered by valve.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of the MQTT protocol in a Smart Irrigation IoT system. Why is it preferred over HTTP?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MQTT role (1.5 marks):** MQTT is a **lightweight publish/subscribe messaging protocol** used by the edge node to publish sensor telemetry (e.g., `farm/zone1/moisture`) to a **broker** (HiveMQ, Mosquitto) which fans it out to cloud subscribers, dashboards, and analytics engines.
- **Why preferred over HTTP (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Low overhead** — header is only 2 bytes vs HTTP's hundreds (suits LoRaWAN).
  2. **Asynchronous** — supports unreliable rural networks.
  3. **Push-based** — ideal for real-time actuator commands.
  4. **Battery-friendly** — minimal keep-alive overhead.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

#### **Question A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer architecture** of a Smart Irrigation IoT system. Discuss the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)** and **Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR)** for a tomato crop. Given: $K_c = 1.15$, $ET_0 = 5.2$ mm/day, $P_{eff} = 1.8$ mm/day, drip efficiency $\eta = 0.9$. Also compute the **valve ON-time** if plot area = 200 m² and emitter flow = 4 L/min. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture (7 marks):**
1. **Perception Layer (1.5 marks):** Soil moisture, temperature, humidity, rain, flow, LDR sensors → converts physical parameters to electrical signals.
2. **Network Layer (1.5 marks):** Wi-Fi / LoRa / GSM + MQTT / CoAP → transmits data to cloud.
3. **Cloud / Analytics Layer (2 marks):** Storage in time-series DB, ETc prediction via ML, weather API integration, dashboard generation.
4. **Actuation Layer (2 marks):** Relay → Solenoid valve / Drip pump; receives command from cloud/edge to open/close water flow. Closed feedback via flow sensor.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — **Crop Evapotranspiration:**

$$ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0 = 1.15 \times 5.2 = 5.98\ \text{mm/day}$$

Step 2 — **Net Irrigation Requirement:**

$$NIR = ET_c - P_{eff} = 5.98 - 1.8 = 4.18\ \text{mm/day}$$

Step 3 — **Gross Irrigation Requirement:**

$$GIR = \frac{NIR}{\eta} = \frac{4.18}{0.9} = 4.644\ \text{mm/day}$$

Step 4 — **Volume to apply per day on 200 m²:**

$$V = A \cdot d = 200 \times 4.644 = 928.8\ \text{L/day}$$

Step 5 — **Valve ON-time:**

$$t_{on} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{928.8}{4} = 232.2\ \text{min} \approx 3\ \text{h}\ 52\ \text{min}$$

**Final Answer:** $NIR = 4.18$ mm/day, $GIR = 4.64$ mm/day, $t_{on} = 232.2$ min.

---

#### **Question B — (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **drip, sprinkler, and flood irrigation** systems in terms of efficiency, cost, and IoT compatibility. Justify which is best suited for an IoT-based Smart Irrigation deployment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **sensor-to-cloud data pipeline** for a 5-hectare farm. Justify the choice of communication protocol, edge controller, and cloud platform. Estimate the **daily data volume** if each node sends 43-byte packets every 60 s and there are 8 nodes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Drip | Sprinkler | Flood |
|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency $\eta$ | 85–95% | 60–75% | 30–50% |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Low |
| Water saving | Up to 60% | Up to 40% | None |
| IoT compatibility | **Excellent** (per-emitter control) | Moderate | Poor |
| Best for | Fruits, vegetables | Cereals | Paddy |
| **Verdict for IoT** | **Best choice** | Acceptable | Not recommended |

**Justification (2 marks):** Drip irrigation has the highest $\eta$ and supports **zone-wise sensor-actuator pairing**, enabling real-time precision control ideal for IoT telemetry.

**(b) Data Pipeline Design (7 marks):**

- **Edge Controller (1.5 marks):** **ESP32 with LoRa SX1276** — low-power, dual-core, integrated Wi-Fi/BLE, supports MQTT.
- **Communication Protocol (1.5 marks):** **MQTT over LoRaWAN** — minimal header, asynchronous, ideal for intermittent rural connectivity.
- **Cloud Platform (1 mark):** **ThingSpeak or AWS IoT Core** — built-in MQTT broker, time-series storage, dashboard widgets, REST APIs.
- **Data Volume Estimation (3 marks):**

$$S_{pkt} = 43\ \text{bytes}, \quad N_{nodes} = 8$$

Per day per node:

$$B_{node} = 43 \times 1440 = 61{,}920\ \text{bytes}$$

For 8 nodes:

$$B_{total} = 8 \times 61{,}920 = 495{,}360\ \text{bytes} \approx 484\ \text{kB/day} \approx 0.47\ \text{MB/day}$$

This is **trivially small** for any cellular plan, proving LoRaWAN is the most cost-effective choice for large farms.

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Smart Irrigation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ET₀ with ETc** — ET₀ is reference (grass), ETc is crop-specific. Always multiply by Kc.
> 2. **Forgetting units in NIR** — NIR is in **mm/day**; convert to **L/m²** by ×1.
> 3. **Not using effective rainfall** — using total rainfall inflates NIR errors. Apply 80–90% factor.
> 4. **Omitting efficiency** in GIR — a classic 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Drawing wrong MQTT topology** — students often confuse publisher/broker/subscriber roles. Memorise: **Node → Broker → Cloud → Dashboard**.
> 6. **Forgetting feedback loop** — actuation layer MUST show feedback via flow sensor to be a *closed-loop* CPS.
> 7. **Skipping the math** in 14-mark problems — partial derivations lose 3–4 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Smart Irrigation = Sensors + Edge + Network + Cloud + Actuator** in a closed loop.
- Four architecture layers: **Perception, Network, Cloud, Actuation** (or 5 with Application).
- **Kc** is the **crop coefficient** (0.3–1.2) — multiplies ET₀ to give ETc.
- **NIR = ETc − P_eff** (in mm/day).
- **GIR = NIR / η** (η = 0.9 for drip, 0.7 for sprinkler, 0.4 for flood).
- **Valve ON-time = (A × d) / Q** (units must be consistent).
- **MQTT** = lightweight pub/sub protocol (port 1883/8883) — preferred over HTTP.
- **LoRaWAN** = long-range, low-power, ideal for rural farms (2–15 km).
- **Closed-loop CPS** = sensor → decision → actuator → flow feedback.
- **Water savings** reported: **30–50%** vs traditional irrigation.
- **Solar + Li-ion** edge nodes can run **3–5 years** without maintenance.
- **Cloud platforms:** ThingSpeak, AWS IoT Core, Blynk, Ubidots, Azure IoT Hub.
- **Penman-Monteith** is the FAO-56 standard for ET₀ computation.
- **TAW = (θ_FC − θ_WP) × Z_r** — total available water in root zone.
- **RAW = p × TAW** — readily available water (p = 0.3–0.6).
- Smart irrigation is the **textbook Industry 4.0 use-case** for IoT — expect a compulsory question in ESE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always end with a **concluding statement** linking Smart Irrigation to **sustainability, water conservation, UN-SDG-6 (Clean Water), and Indian government schemes** (e.g., PMKSY — Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana) to earn full content + presentation marks.
