topics.title: Robot Surveillance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT755 - INDUSTRIAL IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Surveillance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Robot Surveillance — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Robot Surveillance** refers to the deployment of autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic agents equipped with an array of sensors, actuators, communication interfaces, and onboard processing units to continuously monitor, inspect, and report on the state of a physical environment in an industrial setting. The system operates as a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** where sensing, computation, and actuation converge in real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU PECIT755 (Industrial IoT Applications) framework, Robot Surveillance is treated as a **mobile sensing node** within the larger Industrial IoT (IIoT) fabric. It is not merely a robot — it is a **data acquisition, edge-processing, and actuation endpoint** that feeds Industrial Analytics platforms.

---

## 1.1 What is Robot Surveillance in Industrial IoT?

In a modern smart factory or hazardous industrial plant (refineries, chemical plants, nuclear facilities, large warehouses), human inspection is often:
- **Expensive** (continuous shifts, fatigue)
- **Dangerous** (toxic gases, high temperature, radiation)
- **Inconsistent** (subjective, error-prone)

A **Surveillance Robot** solves this by acting as a tireless, sensor-rich, mobile platform that can patrol predefined routes, react to anomalies, and stream telemetry to a central IIoT dashboard.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Watchful Night Guard"**
> Imagine hiring a security guard who **never sleeps, never gets bored, sees in the dark, hears ultrasonic frequencies, smells gas leaks, and instantly reports everything to a control room**. That is exactly what a surveillance robot does in an industrial plant. It is the "immortal, multi-sensed night guard" of Industry 4.0.

---

## 1.2 Core Capabilities of a Surveillance Robot

A surveillance robot typically performs four classes of operations:

| Capability Class | Industrial Function | Typical Sensors / Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Temperature, humidity, toxic gas, radiation | DHT22, MQ-series gas sensors, Geiger counters |
| **Visual Inspection** | Defect detection, asset tracking, intrusion | RGB cameras, IR cameras, LiDAR, depth cameras |
| **Acoustic / Vibration Monitoring** | Machine health, leak detection | MEMS microphones, accelerometers, piezoelectric sensors |
| **Navigation & Mapping** | Path planning, obstacle avoidance | Wheel encoders, IMU, GPS, LiDAR, ultrasonic sensors |

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Benchmarks)

The following metrics are the **gold standard** for evaluating a robot surveillance system in any KTU-style analytical question:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Coverage Ratio (C):** Fraction of the target area physically visited/sensed by the robot over a mission cycle. Target benchmark: **C ≥ 0.95** (95%) for safety-critical plants.
> - **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD):** Average delay between an anomaly occurrence and robot detection. Industrial benchmark: **MTTD ≤ 30 seconds**.
> - **False Alarm Rate (FAR):** Spurious triggers per 1000 scans. Benchmark: **FAR ≤ 2**.
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Industrial-grade robots target **MTBF ≥ 10,000 hours**.
> - **Operational Endurance:** Battery/charge endurance, typically **8 to 24 hours** per mission.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Surveillance Robots

Surveillance robots are classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Mobility Domain**
   - *Ground-based* — wheeled, tracked, legged (e.g., wheeled patrol robots in warehouses)
   - *Aerial* — quadcopters/drones (e.g., indoor drone inspection of factory ceilings)
   - *Marine / Underground* — pipelines, tanks (specialty domains)

2. **Autonomy Level** (per SAE J3016-inspired industrial adaptation)
   - *Level 0:* Teleoperated (no autonomy)
   - *Level 1:* Assisted navigation
   - *Level 2:* Partial autonomy (waypoint following)
   - *Level 3:* Conditional autonomy (dynamic re-routing)
   - *Level 4:* Full autonomy (self-decision on patrol, anomaly response)

3. **Communication Architecture**
   - *Standalone* (no network)
   - *Connected* (Wi-Fi / 5G to IIoT gateway)
   - *Swarm* (multi-robot mesh, peer-to-peer)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Functional Architecture

Every industrial surveillance robot, irrespective of brand, conforms to a three-layer functional model. Understanding this structure is the single most important KTU takeaway from this topic.

### Layer 1 — Perception Layer (The "Senses")
- Multi-modal sensor array (vision, range, chemical, thermal, acoustic).
- Performs **Sensor Fusion** — combining heterogeneous streams into a unified environmental model.
- Output: a structured representation (e.g., occupancy grid, 3D point cloud, semantic map).

### Layer 2 — Cognition Layer (The "Brain")
- Runs on an **Edge Computing** unit (NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi + Coral TPU, Intel NUC).
- Hosts AI/ML models for:
  - Anomaly detection (autoencoders, isolation forests)
  - Object detection (YOLO, SSD)
  - SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
- Output: decisions, classifications, route replans.

### Layer 3 — Actuation Layer (The "Muscles")
- Motor controllers, wheel drivers, pan-tilt units, robotic arms (for sample collection or valve operation).
- Communicates with the IIoT cloud via **MQTT / CoAP / OPC-UA** protocols.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this layered model?**
> The layered approach is the **de-facto KTU answer template** for any question of the form *"Explain the architecture of a robot surveillance system."* Memorize the three layers — Perception, Cognition, Actuation.

---

## 2.2 Sensor Fusion — The Mathematical Heart

Sensor fusion is what makes a surveillance robot *reliable* rather than just *sensor-rich*. The two most common fusion methods in industrial robots are:

### 2.2.1 Complementary Filter (Low-Cost IMU Fusion)

For combining a gyroscope (high-frequency noise, drifts over time) with an accelerometer (low-frequency, noisy short-term):

$$
\theta_{k} = \alpha \cdot (\theta_{k-1} + \omega_{k} \cdot \Delta t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{\text{accel},k}
$$

Where:
- $\theta_{k}$ = fused tilt angle estimate at time step $k$ (radians)
- $\omega_{k}$ = gyroscope reading (rad/s)
- $\Delta t$ = sampling interval (seconds)
- $\theta_{\text{accel},k}$ = tilt derived from accelerometer
- $\alpha$ = filter coefficient, typically $\alpha = 0.95$ to **0.98**

### 2.2.2 Kalman Filter (Optimal Linear Fusion)

The **Kalman Filter** is the gold standard for linear Gaussian sensor fusion. It operates in two steps:

**Prediction Step:**
$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_{k}
$$
$$
P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^{T} + Q
$$

**Update Step:**
$$
K_{k} = P_{k\vert k-1} H^{T} (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^{T} + R)^{-1}
$$
$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_{k} (z_{k} - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})
$$
$$
P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_{k} H) P_{k\vert k-1}
$$

Where:
- $\hat{x}$ = state estimate vector
- $P$ = state covariance matrix
- $A$ = state transition matrix
- $B$ = control input matrix
- $Q$ = process noise covariance
- $R$ = measurement noise covariance
- $H$ = observation matrix
- $K$ = Kalman gain
- $z_{k}$ = sensor measurement vector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pickup Line:** When a question asks *"How does a robot handle noisy sensor data?"* — the expected KTU answer is **Kalman Filtering** (or Extended Kalman Filter / Particle Filter for non-linear cases).

---

## 2.3 SLAM — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping

SLAM is the *core competency* of an autonomous surveillance robot. Without SLAM, a robot cannot answer: *"Where am I?"* and *"What does my environment look like?"* simultaneously.

The SLAM problem is formalized as estimating the joint posterior:

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})
$$

Where:
- $x_{1:t}$ = robot trajectory (poses from time 1 to $t$)
- $m$ = map (set of landmarks or occupancy grid)
- $z_{1:t}$ = observations / sensor measurements
- $u_{1:t-1}$ = control inputs (odometry, motor commands)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** SLAM is a chicken-and-egg problem. *To localize, you need a map; to build a map, you need to know your location.* The math resolves this through iterative Bayesian updates.

**Variants covered under KTU:**
- **EKF-SLAM** — Extended Kalman Filter based, good for small maps.
- **FastSLAM** — Particle filter based, scalable to larger maps.
- **Graph-SLAM** — Pose-graph optimization (used in modern ROS-based systems).
- **Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM, RTAB-Map)** — Pure camera based, used in drones.

---

## 2.4 Path Planning Formulas

### 2.4.1 Coverage Path Planning (CPP)

For a rectangular industrial floor of dimensions $L \times W$ and a robot with sensor swath $s$:

$$
N_{\text{lanes}} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{s} \right\rceil
$$

$$
L_{\text{total path}} = N_{\text{lanes}} \cdot L + (N_{\text{lanes}} - 1) \cdot \pi \cdot R_{\text{turn}}
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{lanes}}$ = number of back-and-forth sweeps
- $L_{\text{total path}}$ = total distance traveled (meters)
- $R_{\text{turn}}$ = turning radius of the robot

### 2.4.2 Time to Complete Mission

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = \frac{L_{\text{total path}}}{v_{\text{avg}}} + N_{\text{checkpoints}} \cdot T_{\text{stop}}
$$

Where $v_{\text{avg}}$ is the average speed (m/s) and $T_{\text{stop}}$ is the dwell time at each checkpoint.

### 2.4.3 Battery Energy Consumption

$$
E_{\text{mission}} = P_{\text{idle}} \cdot T_{\text{mission}} + P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sensing}} \cdot T_{\text{sensing}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Hint:** Coverage Path Planning questions are extremely common in Part B (14-mark) questions. Always show your assumption for sensor swath, robot speed, and number of checkpoints.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{k} = \alpha(\theta_{k-1} + \omega_k \Delta t) + (1-\alpha)\theta_{\text{accel},k}$ | IMU sensor fusion | rad |
| 2 | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $p(x_{1:t}, m \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ | SLAM posterior | probability |
| 4 | $N_{\text{lanes}} = \lceil W / s \rceil$ | Coverage path planning | count |
| 5 | $L_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{lanes}} \cdot L + (N_{\text{lanes}}-1)\pi R_{\text{turn}}$ | Total patrol distance | meters |
| 6 | $T_{\text{mission}} = L_{\text{total}} / v_{\text{avg}} + N \cdot T_{\text{stop}}$ | Mission time | seconds |
| 7 | $C = A_{\text{covered}} / A_{\text{total}}$ | Coverage ratio | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{anomaly},i})$ | Detection latency | seconds |
| 9 | $E_{\text{mission}} = \sum_j P_j \cdot T_j$ | Energy budget | Joules / Wh |
| 10 | $v_{\text{linear}} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ | Robot speed | m/s |
| 11 | $\omega_{\text{angular}} = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$ | Robot angular speed | rad/s |
| 12 | $P_{\text{comms}} = P_{tx} + P_{rx}$ | Communication power | Watts |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Robot surveillance systems are deployed in:
- **Oil & Gas** — Methane leak detection, flare stack inspection.
- **Power Plants** — Cooling tower inspection, switchyard monitoring.
- **Warehousing & Logistics** — Inventory scanning, intruder detection.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Cleanroom compliance, cold-chain monitoring.
- **Smart Cities** — Pipeline inspection, underground cable surveys.
- **Defense & Border** — Perimeter surveillance, threat detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — IMU Complementary Filter Derivation

**Problem Statement:** A surveillance robot's IMU returns gyroscope reading $\omega = 0.05$ rad/s and accelerometer-derived tilt $\theta_{\text{accel}} = 0.12$ rad. Sampling interval $\Delta t = 0.02$ s, prior tilt estimate $\theta_{k-1} = 0.10$ rad, filter coefficient $\alpha = 0.96$. Compute the fused tilt angle $\theta_k$.

**Step 1 — Predict angular change from gyroscope:**

$$
\Delta \theta_{\text{gyro}} = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0.05 \times 0.02
$$

$$
\Delta \theta_{\text{gyro}} = 0.001 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Gyroscope-based prediction of current angle:**

$$
\theta_{\text{gyro},k} = \theta_{k-1} + \Delta \theta_{\text{gyro}} = 0.10 + 0.001
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{gyro},k} = 0.101 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply complementary filter (weighted fusion):**

$$
\theta_{k} = \alpha \cdot \theta_{\text{gyro},k} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{\text{accel},k}
$$

$$
\theta_{k} = 0.96 \times 0.101 + 0.04 \times 0.12
$$

$$
\theta_{k} = 0.09696 + 0.00480
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{k} = 0.10176 \text{ rad} \approx 5.83^{\circ}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The filter trusts the gyroscope 96% (short-term stability) and the accelerometer 4% (long-term drift correction). This is precisely the engineering compromise that makes IMU fusion robust.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Coverage Path Planning for an Industrial Hall

**Problem Statement:** A robot must surveil a rectangular factory floor of $L = 50$ m (length) $\times$ $W = 20$ m (width). The robot's sensor swath (effective coverage width) is $s = 4$ m. Turning radius $R_{\text{turn}} = 1.5$ m. Compute (a) number of lanes, (b) total path length.

**Step 1 — Compute number of lanes:**

$$
N_{\text{lanes}} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{20}{4} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{lanes}} = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total path length:**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{lanes}} \cdot L + (N_{\text{lanes}} - 1) \cdot \pi \cdot R_{\text{turn}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 50 + (5 - 1) \times \pi \times 1.5
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 250 + 4 \times 4.7124
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 250 + 18.8496
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{total}} = 268.85 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mission time (extension):** Assuming $v_{\text{avg}} = 0.5$ m/s and $N_{\text{checkpoints}} = 5$ with $T_{\text{stop}} = 10$ s each:

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = \frac{L_{\text{total}}}{v_{\text{avg}}} + N_{\text{checkpoints}} \cdot T_{\text{stop}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = \frac{268.85}{0.5} + 5 \times 10
$$

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = 537.7 + 50
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{mission}} = 587.7 \text{ s} \approx 9.8 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SLAM Posterior Simplification (Bayes' Rule)

The full SLAM posterior:

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})
$$

Applying Bayes' rule and the Markov assumption (current state depends only on previous state and current control):

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \vert z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1}) = \eta \cdot p(z_{t} \vert x_{t}, m) \cdot p(x_{t} \vert x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \cdot p(x_{1:t-1}, m \vert z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-2})
$$

Where $\eta$ is a normalization constant. This recursive formulation is the basis of EKF-SLAM and FastSLAM algorithms.

**Recursive nature shown explicitly:**

$$
\text{Belief}(x_t, m) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \int p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \cdot \text{Belief}(x_{t-1}, m) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This integral over the previous belief is what makes SLAM computationally expensive — leading to the development of particle filters and graph-based optimizations.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Kalman Filter One-Step Numerical

**Problem:** A 1D robot position estimation with state transition $A = 1$, control $B \cdot u = 0.5$ m, prior state $\hat{x}_{k-1} = 2.0$ m, prior covariance $P_{k-1} = 0.04$, process noise $Q = 0.01$, measurement $z_k = 3.2$ m, observation matrix $H = 1$, measurement noise $R = 0.09$.

**Step 1 — Prediction:**

$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u = 1 \times 2.0 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1} A^T + Q = 1 \times 0.04 \times 1 + 0.01 = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_k = \frac{P_{k\vert k-1} H^T}{H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R} = \frac{0.05 \times 1}{1 \times 0.05 \times 1 + 0.09}
$$

$$
K_k = \frac{0.05}{0.14} = 0.3571
$$

**Step 3 — State Update:**

$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = 2.5 + 0.3571 \times (3.2 - 2.5)
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = 2.5 + 0.3571 \times 0.7
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = 2.5 + 0.250 = 2.750 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Covariance Update:**

$$
P_{k\vert k} = (1 - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1} = (1 - 0.3571) \times 0.05
$$

$$
P_{k\vert k} = 0.6429 \times 0.05 = 0.0321
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** The covariance dropped from $0.05 \rightarrow 0.0321$. This reduction represents the robot's *increased confidence* in its position estimate after incorporating the new measurement. This is precisely the value of sensor fusion in surveillance robots.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Computational Cost | Map Size | Sensor Type | Accuracy | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EKF-SLAM | Low (O(N²)) | Small (< 100 landmarks) | Range / Bearing | High (linear) | Indoor wheeled robots |
| FastSLAM | Medium (O(M log N)) | Medium | Range | Medium-High | Mixed indoor/outdoor |
| Graph-SLAM | High (offline optimization) | Large | LiDAR / Camera | Very High | Post-mission analysis |
| Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM) | High | Medium | Monocular / Stereo | Medium | Drones, lightweight bots |
| Particle Filter SLAM | Very High (many particles) | Medium | Any | High (non-linear) | Non-Gaussian noise environments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Layer Robot Surveillance Architecture

    end

    S1 --> SF

## 4.2 Robot Surveillance Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Mission Power On"] --> B["Initialize Sensors and Self Test"]
    B --> C{"Self Test Pass"}
    C -- "No" --> Z["Return to Charging Dock and Report Fault"]
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Connect to IIoT Gateway via MQTT"]
    D --> E["Load Patrol Map and Waypoints"]
    E --> F["Begin Localizing using SLAM"]
    F --> G["Navigate to First Waypoint"]
    G --> H{"Waypoint Reached"}
    H -- "No" --> I["Read Sensors Continuously"]
    I --> J{"Obstacle Detected"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Replan Path using A Star"]
    K --> G
    J -- "No" --> G
    H -- "Yes" --> L["Capture Image and Sensor Snapshot"]
    L --> M["Run AI Inference Object Detection"]
    M --> N{"Anomaly Detected"}
    N -- "Yes" --> O["Trigger Alarm and Send Alert to Cloud"]
    O --> P["Stream Telemetry Video and Sensor Data"]
    N -- "No" --> Q["Log Normal Snapshot to Database"]
    Q --> R{"All Waypoints Visited"}
    P --> R
    R -- "No" --> G
    R -- "Yes" --> S["Return to Charging Dock"]
    S --> T["Upload Mission Report to Cloud"]
    T --> U["End Mission"]
```

---

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Data Flow (Kalman Filter Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Gyroscope Sensor"] --> B["State Predict Block A x hat plus B u"]
    C["Accelerometer Sensor"] --> D["Measurement Model H x"]
    B --> E["Predicted State x hat k given k minus 1"]
    B --> F["Predicted Covariance P k given k minus 1"]
    F --> G["Kalman Gain K Computation"]
    D --> G
    E --> H["Innovation y equals z minus H x hat"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Updated State Estimate x hat k given k"]
    H --> J["Updated Covariance P k given k"]
    I --> K["Output Fused Estimate"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Next Time Step Feedback"]
    L --> A
    L --> C
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECIT755, Module 4]**
**Define Robot Surveillance in the context of Industrial IoT. List any four types of sensors commonly used in an industrial surveillance robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Robot Surveillance is the use of autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic agents equipped with sensors, processors, and communication modules to continuously monitor, inspect, and report on the state of an industrial environment as part of a Cyber-Physical System / Industrial IoT framework.

> **Sensors (1.5 marks — any 4):**
> 1. **LiDAR / Ultrasonic** — for distance and obstacle detection
> 2. **RGB / Thermal Camera** — for visual inspection
> 3. **Gas Sensor (MQ-series)** — for chemical leak detection
> 4. **IMU (Accelerometer + Gyroscope)** — for orientation and motion
> 5. **PIR / Infrared** — for intruder / motion detection
> 6. **Microphone / Accelerometer** — for acoustic anomaly detection

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECIT755, Module 4]**
**What is SLAM? Why is it considered the most critical module in an autonomous surveillance robot?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SLAM — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (1.5 marks):** SLAM is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of the robot's location within that map. It is mathematically formulated as estimating the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ where $x$ is trajectory, $m$ is map, $z$ are observations, and $u$ are control inputs.

**Critical Role (1.5 marks):**
- Without SLAM, the robot cannot determine *where it is* in a GPS-denied industrial indoor environment.
- Without SLAM, the robot cannot return to a previously detected anomaly location.
- SLAM enables autonomous re-routing when obstacles are detected.
- It underpins coverage path planning and patrol efficiency.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECIT755, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the three-layer architecture (Perception, Cognition, Actuation) of a robot surveillance system used in an industrial plant. Explain the role of sensor fusion in the Perception layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A surveillance robot must patrol a rectangular factory floor of dimensions $L = 80$ m and $W = 32$ m. The sensor swath is $s = 4$ m and turning radius is $R = 2$ m. The robot moves at an average speed of $0.6$ m/s and stops at each waypoint for $T_{\text{stop}} = 8$ seconds. Compute (i) the number of lanes required, (ii) the total path length, and (iii) the total mission time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Three-Layer Architecture (7 marks):**

The three-layer architecture is the standard reference model for industrial robot surveillance systems:

**1. Perception Layer (Sensing) — 2 marks:**
This layer interfaces the physical world with the digital system. It includes:
- Vision sensors (RGB, IR, depth cameras)
- Range sensors (LiDAR, ultrasonic, radar)
- Environmental sensors (temperature, humidity, gas, radiation)
- Inertial sensors (IMU, gyroscope, accelerometer)
- The output is raw, noisy, multi-modal data that is forwarded to the cognition layer.

**2. Cognition Layer (Decision-making) — 2 marks:**
This is the on-board edge computing unit. It runs:
- **Sensor Fusion** (Kalman / Complementary filters) — combines heterogeneous data into a single, reliable state estimate.
- **SLAM** — builds the map and tracks the robot pose.
- **AI/ML Inference** — object detection, anomaly classification.
- **Path Planning** — A*, RRT, D* for dynamic re-routing.
- **Decision logic** — alarm triggering, mission replan.

**3. Actuation Layer (Action) — 2 marks:**
This includes:
- Motor controllers (wheel drives, BLDC motors)
- Pan-tilt units for cameras
- Optional robotic arms for sample pickup
- Audio/visual alarms and sirens
- Network transceivers (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa) for IIoT uplink

**Role of Sensor Fusion (1 mark):** Sensor fusion combines data from multiple noisy sensors to produce a single estimate that is *more accurate and more reliable* than any individual sensor. In surveillance robots, fusing IMU + wheel odometry + LiDAR enables robust localization even when individual sensors fail or are temporarily occluded.

**Block Diagram (schematic representation — textual):**
```
   [Sensors] -> [Signal Conditioning] -> [Sensor Fusion + SLAM]
                                              |
                                              v
   [Motors] <- [Motor Controllers] <- [Path Planner / AI Engine]
        |
   [IIoT Gateway] -> [Cloud Dashboard]
```

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 80$ m, $W = 32$ m, $s = 4$ m, $R = 2$ m
- $v_{\text{avg}} = 0.6$ m/s, $T_{\text{stop}} = 8$ s

**(i) Number of lanes:**

$$
N_{\text{lanes}} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{32}{4} \right\rceil = 8
$$

**$\boxed{N_{\text{lanes}} = 8 \text{ lanes}}$ (2 marks)**

**(ii) Total path length:**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{lanes}} \cdot L + (N_{\text{lanes}} - 1) \cdot \pi \cdot R
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 80 + (8 - 1) \times \pi \times 2
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 640 + 7 \times 6.2832
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 640 + 43.98
$$

**$\boxed{L_{\text{total}} \approx 683.98 \text{ m}}$ (2 marks)**

**(iii) Total mission time:**

Number of waypoints = $N_{\text{lanes}} = 8$ (one per lane end).

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = \frac{L_{\text{total}}}{v_{\text{avg}}} + N_{\text{checkpoints}} \cdot T_{\text{stop}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = \frac{683.98}{0.6} + 8 \times 8
$$

$$
T_{\text{mission}} = 1139.97 + 64
$$

**$\boxed{T_{\text{mission}} \approx 1203.97 \text{ s} \approx 20.07 \text{ minutes}}$ (3 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECIT755, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the Kalman Filter algorithm used for sensor fusion in surveillance robots. List the prediction and update equations with their physical meaning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses a Complementary Filter to combine gyroscope and accelerometer data for tilt estimation. Given:
- Gyroscope reading: $\omega = 0.08$ rad/s
- Accelerometer-derived tilt: $\theta_{\text{accel}} = 0.15$ rad
- Sampling interval: $\Delta t = 0.01$ s
- Prior tilt estimate: $\theta_{k-1} = 0.13$ rad
- Filter coefficient: $\alpha = 0.97$
- Compute the fused tilt angle $\theta_k$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Kalman Filter Explanation (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The Kalman Filter is an optimal recursive estimator for linear dynamic systems with Gaussian noise. In surveillance robots, it is used to fuse noisy measurements from multiple sensors (IMU, GPS, wheel odometry, LiDAR) into a single, statistically optimal state estimate — typically the robot's position, velocity, and orientation.

**State Definition (1 mark):** The state vector $\mathbf{x}_k$ may contain $[x, y, \theta, v_x, v_y, \omega]^T$ representing position, orientation, and velocities.

**Prediction Step (2 marks):**
- $\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1|k-1} + B u_k$ — predicts the current state from the prior state and control input.
- $P_{k|k-1} = A P_{k-1|k-1} A^T + Q$ — projects uncertainty forward, adding process noise $Q$.

**Update Step (2 marks):**
- $K_k = P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ — computes Kalman gain, balancing trust between prediction and measurement.
- $\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k|k-1})$ — corrects prediction using innovation.
- $P_{k|k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}$ — reduces uncertainty after measurement.

**Physical Meaning:** A high $K$ means the sensor is trusted more; a low $K$ means the model is trusted more. The filter self-adjusts $K$ at every step.

---

**Part (b) — Complementary Filter Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\omega = 0.08$ rad/s, $\theta_{\text{accel}} = 0.15$ rad, $\Delta t = 0.01$ s, $\theta_{k-1} = 0.13$ rad, $\alpha = 0.97$

**Step 1 — Gyroscope prediction (3 marks):**

$$
\theta_{\text{gyro},k} = \theta_{k-1} + \omega \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{gyro},k} = 0.13 + 0.08 \times 0.01 = 0.13 + 0.0008 = 0.1308 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply complementary filter (3 marks):**

$$
\theta_k = \alpha \cdot \theta_{\text{gyro},k} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{\text{accel},k}
$$

$$
\theta_k = 0.97 \times 0.1308 + 0.03 \times 0.15
$$

$$
\theta_k = 0.126876 + 0.0045
$$

$$
\theta_k = 0.131376 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees (1 mark):**

$$
\theta_k = 0.131376 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 7.53^{\circ}
$$

**$\boxed{\theta_k \approx 0.1314 \text{ rad} \approx 7.53^{\circ}}$**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks)**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in lane computation:** If $W$ is not an exact multiple of $s$, students often compute $N = W/s$ as a decimal and forget to round up. Always apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
> 2. **Forgetting to add turn-around distance:** Many students compute $N_{\text{lanes}} \cdot L$ but omit the $(N_{\text{lanes}} - 1) \pi R$ term, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Kalman Filter matrices:** Writing the prediction step but forgetting $Q$ (process noise) in $P_{k|k-1}$ loses a mark.
> 4. **Wrong units in mission time:** Always state final answer in both seconds and minutes for clarity.
> 5. **In IMU filter questions, students often use $\alpha = 0.5$ instead of 0.95+.** This is physically wrong — the gyroscope must dominate at high frequency. Examiners will deduct marks.

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Robot Surveillance (PECIT755 — Module 4):**

- [x] **Definition:** Autonomous/semi-autonomous robot with sensors, processors, and IIoT comms for industrial monitoring.
- [x] **Three-layer model:** Perception → Cognition → Actuation (always mention this in descriptive answers).
- [x] **Common sensors:** Camera, LiDAR, IMU, gas sensor, ultrasonic, microphone, encoder.
- [x] **SLAM posterior:** $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ — memorize this exact form.
- [x] **SLAM variants:** EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM).
- [x] **Kalman Filter:** Two steps — Predict ($A, B, Q$) and Update ($H, R, K$).
- [x] **Complementary Filter formula:** $\theta_k = \alpha \theta_{\text{gyro}} + (1-\alpha)\theta_{\text{accel}}$ with $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.98]$.
- [x] **Coverage Path Planning:** $N_{\text{lanes}} = \lceil W/s \rceil$ and $L_{\text{total}} = N L + (N-1)\pi R$.
- [x] **Mission time:** $T_{\text{mission}} = L_{\text{total}} / v + N \cdot T_{\text{stop}}$.
- [x] **Coverage ratio:** $C = A_{\text{covered}} / A_{\text{total}}$, target $\geq 0.95$.
- [x] **Communication protocols:** MQTT, CoAP, OPC-UA for IIoT uplink.
- [x] **Autonomy levels:** SAE-inspired 0–4 scale; industrial robots target Level 3–4.
- [x] **Swarm robotics:** Peer-to-peer mesh (802.11s) + MQTT cloud uplink.
- [x] **Real-world deployments:** Oil & gas, power plants, warehouses, pharma cleanrooms, defense.
- [x] **Key industrial metrics:** MTTD, FAR, MTBF, Coverage Ratio, Endurance.
- [x] **Edge computing platforms:** NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi + Coral TPU, Intel NUC.
- [x] **AI models on-board:** YOLO for object detection, autoencoders for anomaly detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always structure Part B answers as **(i) Diagram, (ii) Explanation, (iii) Numerical/Application, (iv) Real-world deployment example**. This is the scoring pattern examiners look for. Also, **always write the units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners award a half-mark specifically for correct units.
